The pricing structure is transparent, and users can enjoy a 60% savings, including rightsizing recommendations, list price differences, and sustained usage discounts.

    * Conclusion

    Google Cloud Platform proves to be a robust and user-friendly cloud computing solution, providing a wide range of services with cost-effectiveness and reliability. @string/gpc_tutorial

    SQL, short for Structured Query Language, is a powerful tool designed for managing data in relational database management systems (RDBMS). It enables users to interact with databases, perform various operations such as data insertion, retrieval, and modification, and ensure data integrity and security. SQL plays a pivotal role in handling structured data stored in tabular form.

    Why SQL?

    In today\'s data-driven world, SQL is widely adopted in various industries for its versatility and effectiveness. Key reasons for its popularity include:

    1. Data Manipulation: SQL allows users to perform essential operations such as inserting, updating, and deleting data in databases.

    2. Data Retrieval: It facilitates the retrieval of data from databases, enabling users to extract relevant information for analysis and decision-making.

    3. Data Definition: SQL helps in defining and organizing the structure of databases, including tables, views, and stored procedures.

    4. Data Security: It enables users to implement security measures such as access control and encryption to safeguard sensitive information.

    5. Data Integrity: SQL ensures data consistency and integrity by enforcing constraints and validations on database entries.

    History of SQL

    SQL traces its origins back to the seminal paper "A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks" by E.F. Codd in 1970. Inspired by Codd\'s work, IBM researchers Raymond Boyce and Donald Chamberlin developed the initial version of SQL, originally named SEQUEL (Structured English Query Language), in 1970. Over the years, SQL evolved into a standardized language, with the first implementation by Relational Software Inc. (now Oracle Corporation) in 1979.

    Process of SQL

    SQL operates through various components, including the Query Dispatcher, Optimization Engines, and SQL Query Engine. These components work together to interpret and execute SQL commands efficiently, ensuring optimal performance and reliability.

    SQL Tutorial

    SQL offers a comprehensive set of commands for database management. Some commonly used SQL commands include:

    • CREATE: Used to create databases, tables, views, and other database objects.

    • UPDATE: Enables users to modify existing data in databases.

    • DELETE: Allows users to remove records from database tables.

    • SELECT: Used to retrieve data from one or more database tables based on specified criteria.

    • DROP: Used to delete entire tables or other database objects.

    • INSERT: Enables users to add new records to database tables.

    Advantages:

    • Simplicity: SQL offers a straightforward syntax, making it easy to learn and use for database management tasks.

    • Speed: SQL commands execute quickly and efficiently, enabling rapid data retrieval and manipulation.

    • Standardization: SQL adheres to established standards, ensuring compatibility across different database systems and platforms.

    • Portability: SQL can be used across various devices and platforms, providing flexibility and accessibility.

    • Interactivity: SQL enables interactive communication with databases, allowing users to perform complex queries and obtain results promptly.

    Disadvantages:

    • Cost: Some SQL implementations may incur high operational costs, limiting access for certain users or organizations.

    • Complexity: The complexity of SQL interfaces can pose challenges for novice users, requiring additional training and expertise.

    • Limited Control: Users may have limited control over underlying database structures and business rules, leading to potential constraints in data management.

    SQL Syntax

    When performing operations on data within a database, it\'s essential to adhere to the predefined syntax of SQL. The structured query language\'s syntax is a distinct set of rules and guidelines established and maintained by ISO and ANSI standards. Here are some key points to remember about SQL syntax:

    • Keywords in SQL can be written in both uppercase and lowercase, although using uppercase enhances query readability.

    • SQL statements or syntax can span single or multiple text lines, facilitating readability and organization.

    • Most database operations can be executed using SQL statements.

    • SQL syntax is influenced by relational algebra and tuple relational calculus.

    Example SQL Statement

    SQL statements convey to the database the operation you wish to perform on the structured data and the information you intend to access from the database. SQL statements are straightforward and easy to comprehend, resembling plain English but with a specific syntax.

    SELECT "column_name" FROM "table_name";

    Every SQL statement commences with SQL keywords and concludes with a semicolon (;). The semicolon serves to separate multiple SQL statements executed within the same call. Throughout this SQL tutorial, we\'ll include a semicolon (;) at the conclusion of each SQL statement.

    Data Types in SQL

    Data types in SQL are fundamental for representing the nature of data that can be stored in a database table. They are categorized into three main groups: string data types, numeric data types, and date and time data types. Let\'s explore the data types used in MySQL, SQL Server, and Oracle databases.

    MySQL Data Types

    MySQL offers a range of data types for various purposes:

    • String Data Types:

      • CHAR(Size): Fixed-length string for storing alphanumeric characters.

      • VARCHAR(Size): Variable-length string for storing alphanumeric characters.

      • BINARY(Size): Similar to CHAR() but for binary byte strings.

      • VARBINARY(Size): Similar to VARCHAR() but for binary byte strings.

      • TEXT(Size): String with a maximum length of 255 characters.

      • TINYTEXT, MEDIUMTEXT, LONGTEXT: String data types with increasing maximum lengths.

      • ENUM(val1, val2, val3,...): String object with a single value chosen from a list of options.

      • SET(val1, val2, val3,...): String with zero or more values chosen from a list.

      • BLOB(Size): For binary large objects.

    • Numeric Data Types:

      • BIT(Size): Bit-value type.

      • INT(Size), INTEGER(Size): Integer value with varying display width.

      • FLOAT(Size, d), FLOAT(p): Floating point number with specified precision.

      • DOUBLE(Size, d): Floating point number with a specific number of digits.

      • DECIMAL(Size, d), DEC(Size, d): Fixed-point number.

      • BOOL: Boolean values.

    • Date and Time Data Types:

      • DATE: Date format YYYY-MM-DD.

      • DATETIME(fsp): Date and time combination.

      • TIMESTAMP(fsp): Timestamp.

      • TIME(fsp): Time format.

      • YEAR: Year in four-digit format.

    SQL Server Data Types

    SQL Server provides a range of data types as well:

    • String Data Types:

      • char(n), varchar(n), varchar(max), text: Character and text data types.

      • nchar, nvarchar, ntext: Unicode string data types.

      • binary(n), varbinary, image: Binary string data types.

    • Numeric Data Types:

      • bit, tinyint, smallint, int, bigint: Integer data types.

      • float(n), real, money: Floating-point and monetary data types.

    • Date and Time Data Types:

      • datetime, datetime2, date, time: Date and time data types.

    • Other Data Types:

      • sql_variant, XML, cursor, table, uniqueidentifier.

    Oracle Data Types

    Oracle also offers a variety of data types:

    • String Data Types:

      • CHAR(size), NCHAR(size), VARCHAR2(size), NVARCHAR2(size): Character and string data types.

      • NUMBER(p, s), FLOAT(p): Numeric data types.

      • BINARY_FLOAT, BINARY_DOUBLE: Binary precision data types.

    • Date and Time Data Types:

      • DATE, TIMESTAMP: Date and time data types.

    • Large Object Data Types (LOB Types):

      • BLOB, BFILE, CLOB, NCLOB, RAW(size), LONG RAW: For storing unstructured binary and character data.

    SQL Operators Overview

    SQL operators are fundamental components used by database administrators and users to manipulate and retrieve data from database tables and views. These operators are utilized within SQL queries, particularly with the WHERE clause, to filter data based on specific conditions.

    Precedence of SQL Operators

    The precedence of SQL operators determines the order in which they are evaluated within an expression. Higher precedence operators are evaluated before lower precedence ones.

    • Exponentiation operator (^)
    • Identity operator (+), Negation operator (-)
    • Multiplication operator (*), Division operator (/)
    • Addition operator (+), Subtraction operator (-), String Concatenation operator (||)
    • Comparison Operators (=, !=, <, >, <=, >=, IS NULL, LIKE, BETWEEN, IN)
    • Logical negation operator (NOT)
    • Conjunction operator (AND)
    • Inclusion operator (OR)

    Types of SQL Operators

    SQL operators are categorized into various types:

    • Arithmetic Operators
    • Comparison Operators
    • Logical Operators
    • Set Operators
    • Bit-wise Operators
    • Unary Operators

    Examples of SQL Arithmetic Operators

    • Addition Operator (+)
    • Subtraction Operator (-)
    • Multiplication Operator (*)
    • Division Operator (/)
    • Modulus Operator (%)

    Examples of SQL Comparison Operators

    • Equal Operator (=)
    • Not Equal Operator (!=)
    • Greater Than Operator (>)
    • Greater Than Equals to Operator (>=)
    • Less Than Operator (<)
    • Less Than Equals to Operator (<=)

    SQL Logical Operators

    The SQL logical operators are used to perform logical operations on Boolean expressions in SQL queries. These operators include:

    • AND: Returns true if both operands are true.
    • OR: Returns true if at least one operand is true.
    • NOT: Returns true if the operand is false and false if the operand is true.

    SQL Set Operators

    SQL set operators are used to combine the results of two or more SELECT statements. These operators include:

    • UNION: Combines the results of two or more SELECT statements into a single result set.
    • UNION ALL: Similar to UNION but retains duplicate rows.
    • INTERSECT: Returns the intersection of the results of two SELECT statements.
    • MINUS (or EXCEPT in some databases): Returns the results of the first SELECT statement minus the results of the second SELECT statement.

    SQL Bit-wise Operators

    SQL bit-wise operators are used to perform bit-level operations on integer values. These operators include:

    • Bitwise AND (&): Performs a bitwise AND operation.
    • Bitwise OR (|): Performs a bitwise OR operation.

    SQL Unary Operators

    SQL unary operators are operators that perform operations on a single operand. These operators include:

    • Unary Positive (+): Indicates a positive value.
    • Unary Negative (-): Indicates a negative value.
    • Unary Bitwise NOT (~): Performs a bitwise NOT operation.
    @string/what_is_sql @string/sql_syntax @string/sql_data_types @string/sql_operators

    Creating a Database in SQL

    In SQL, the \'CREATE DATABASE\' statement serves as the initial step for organizing structured data within a database system.

    Developers and users utilize this SQL statement to establish new databases, specifying a name for the database within the statement.

    The syntax for the \'CREATE DATABASE\' statement in SQL is straightforward:

    CREATE DATABASE Database_Name;

    Here, \'Database_Name\' denotes the desired name for the new database.

    Several key points are crucial to grasp when creating a database:

    • Opt for a simple and distinctive database name for easy identification.
    • The database name should not exceed 128 characters.

    In MySQL, the same command is employed for creating a new database:

    CREATE DATABASE Database_Name;

    However, in Oracle systems, there\'s no explicit need to create databases separately. Instead, tables can be created directly within the Oracle database.

    Let\'s illustrate the \'CREATE DATABASE\' statement with examples:

    Example 1: Creating the \'Student\' database.

    CREATE DATABASE Student;

    Upon successful execution, a confirmation message "Database created successfully" is typically displayed.

    To confirm the creation, one can use the following query:

    SHOW DATABASES;

    In SQL, attempting to create a database with an existing name would yield an error, indicating that the database already exists. To replace an existing database, the \'DROP\' statement can be used, followed by the \'CREATE\' statement.

    Example 2: Creating the \'Employee\' database.

    CREATE DATABASE Employee;

    Similarly, after successful execution, the message "Database created successfully" is typically shown.

    To ensure the database\'s creation, one can use:

    SHOW DATABASES;

    Should one wish to replace an existing \'Employee\' database, the following query can be utilized:

    CREATE OR REPLACE DATABASE Employee;

    Dropping a Database in SQL

    The SQL \'DROP DATABASE\' statement is used to permanently delete an existing database from the database system. It\'s important to note that this action removes all associated views and tables, making it essential to exercise caution when using this query in SQL.

    Before proceeding with database removal, it\'s crucial to consider the following key points:

    • Deleting a database removes all data within it. For future data restoration, it\'s advisable to keep backups of the database.
    • Attempting to drop a database currently in use by another user will result in an error message indicating that the database is in use.

    Here\'s the syntax for the \'DROP DATABASE\' statement in SQL:

    DROP DATABASE Database_Name;

    Replace \'Database_Name\' with the name of the database you wish to permanently delete from the system. Multiple databases can also be deleted using a single statement, separated by commas:

    DROP DATABASE Database_Name1, Database_Name2, ..., Database_NameN;

    This syntax streamlines the process of deleting multiple databases without the need for separate statements.

    Let\'s explore examples of using the \'DROP DATABASE\' statement:

    Example 1: Deleting the \'Student\' database.

    First, verify the existence of the \'Student\' database in the system:

    SHOW DATABASES;

    If the \'Student\' database is present, execute the following SQL query to remove it:

    DROP DATABASE Student;

    If the \'Student\' database does not exist, attempting to execute the above query will result in an error message indicating that the database doesn\'t exist.

    Example 2: Removing the \'College\' database.

    Similarly, check for the existence of the \'College\' database:

    SHOW DATABASES;

    If the \'College\' database exists, use the following SQL query to delete it permanently:

    DROP DATABASE College;

    If the \'College\' database is not found in the system, executing the above query will yield an error message stating that the database doesn\'t exist.

    Renaming a Database in SQL

    The \'RENAME DATABASE\' statement in SQL allows users and administrators to modify the name of an existing database, which can be useful for various technical or organizational reasons.

    Here are the syntax variations for renaming a database in SQL:

    -- Using ALTER DATABASE statement

    ALTER DATABASE old_database_name MODIFY NAME = new_database_name;

    -- Using stored procedure sp_renamedb specific to some database systems

    EXEC sp_renamedb \'old_database_name\', \'new_database_name\';

    -- For MySQL specifically

    RENAME DATABASE old_database_name TO new_database_name;

    Now, let\'s explore examples of renaming databases:

    Example 1: Renaming the \'Student\' database to \'College\'.

    ALTER DATABASE Student MODIFY NAME = College;

    This query changes the database name from \'Student\' to \'College\'. It\'s important to ensure that the \'Student\' database exists in the current database server before executing this query to avoid errors.

    Example 2: Renaming the \'Department\' database to \'Company\'.

    ALTER DATABASE Department MODIFY NAME = Company;

    Similarly, this query changes the database name from \'Department\' to \'Company\'. It\'s crucial to confirm the existence of the \'Department\' database before running this query to prevent any errors.

    Using the USE Statement in SQL

    In SQL, users and administrators often need to operate on specific databases, performing tasks such as querying tables, views, and indexes. The initial step in this process involves selecting the desired database, which can be achieved using the \'USE\' statement.

    Here\'s the syntax for the \'USE\' statement in SQL:

    USE database_name;

    Replace \'database_name\' with the name of the database you wish to work with. It\'s important to note that the database name must be unique.

    For MySQL, the syntax remains the same:

    USE database_name;

    However, in Oracle, there\'s no need to select a database explicitly.

    Let\'s explore examples of using the \'USE\' statement in SQL:

    Example 1: Working with the \'Hospital\' database.

    USE Hospital;

    Before executing this statement, it\'s advisable to verify the existence of the \'Hospital\' database using:

    SHOW DATABASES;

    Example 2: Switching to the \'College\' database.

    USE College;

    Similarly, ensure the existence of the \'College\' database before executing this statement:

    SHOW DATABASES;

    Example 3: Selecting the \'School\' database.

    USE School;

    Before executing this statement, confirm the presence of the \'School\' database:

    SHOW DATABASES;

    @string/sql_create_database @string/sql_drop_database @string/sql_rename_database @string/sql_select_database

    Understanding SQL Table Variables

    A table is a collection of data organized in terms of rows and columns. In database management systems (DBMS), a table is referred to as a relation, and each row is called a tuple.

    A table consists of a specified number of columns, but it can accommodate any number of rows. It serves as a fundamental form of data storage and is considered a convenient representation of relations.

    Let\'s examine an example of an employee table:

    EMP_NAMEADDRESSSALARY
    AnkitLucknow15000
    RamanAllahabad18000
    MikeNew York20000

    In the above table, "Employee" is the table name, and "EMP_NAME," "ADDRESS," and "SALARY" are the column names. Each row consists of a combination of data from multiple columns, such as "Ankit," "Lucknow," and 15000.

    SQL TABLE Variable

    The SQL Table variable serves various purposes, including creating, modifying, renaming, copying, and deleting tables. It was introduced by Microsoft as an alternative to temporary tables, debuting with SQL Server 2000.

    Table variables function as temporary storage for records and results, similar to temporary tables. However, unlike temporary tables, table variables do not require explicit dropping.

    These variables are primarily used to store sets of records, with the declaration syntax resembling the CREATE TABLE syntax:

    CREATE TABLE "tablename" ( "column1" "data type", "column2" "data type", ..., "columnN" "data type" );

    Notably, when a transaction is rolled back, the data associated with a table variable is not rolled back. Additionally, table variables typically consume fewer resources compared to temporary variables.

    Understanding SQL CREATE TABLE Statement

    The SQL CREATE TABLE statement is utilized to create a table in a database. When creating a table, you need to specify the table name and define its columns along with each column\'s data type.

    Here\'s the simple syntax to create a table:

    CREATE TABLE "tablename" ("column1" "data type", "column2" "data type", "column3" "data type", ... "columnN" "data type");

    The data type of columns may vary from one database system to another. For instance, Oracle database supports the NUMBER data type for integers, whereas MySQL uses INT.

    Let\'s consider an example to create a "STUDENTS" table with "ID" as the primary key and NOT NULL constraints indicating that these fields cannot be NULL when creating records in the table:

    CREATE TABLE STUDENTS ( ID INT NOT NULL, NAME VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, AGE INT NOT NULL, ADDRESS CHAR(25), PRIMARY KEY (ID) );

    You can verify the successful creation of the table by checking the message displayed by the SQL Server. Alternatively, you can use the DESC command as follows:

    DESC STUDENTS;

    Field Type Null Key Default Extra
    ID Int(11) NO PRI
    NAME Varchar(20) NO
    AGE Int(11) NO
    ADDRESS Varchar(25) YES NULL

    Now you have the "STUDENTS" table available in your database, ready to store information related to students.

    Let\'s see examples of creating a table in different databases:

    MySQL:

    CREATE TABLE Employee ( EmployeeID INT, FirstName VARCHAR(255), LastName VARCHAR(255), Email VARCHAR(255), AddressLine VARCHAR(255), City VARCHAR(255) );

    Oracle:

    CREATE TABLE Employee ( EmployeeID NUMBER(10), FirstName VARCHAR2(255), LastName VARCHAR2(255), Email VARCHAR2(255), AddressLine VARCHAR2(255), City VARCHAR2(255) );

    Microsoft SQL Server:

    CREATE TABLE Employee ( EmployeeID INT, FirstName VARCHAR(255), LastName VARCHAR(255), Email VARCHAR(255), AddressLine VARCHAR(255), City VARCHAR(255) );

    To create a table using another table as a reference, you can use the following syntax:

    CREATE TABLE table_name AS SELECT column1, column2,... FROM old_table_name WHERE ...;

    For example:

    CREATE TABLE EmployeeCopy AS SELECT EmployeeID, FirstName, Email FROM Employee;

    Additionally, you can define a PRIMARY KEY constraint when creating a table using the CREATE TABLE statement:

    For MySQL:

    CREATE TABLE Employee( EmployeeID NOT NULL, FirstName VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, LastName VARCHAR(255), City VARCHAR(255), PRIMARY KEY

    Understanding SQL DROP TABLE Statement

    The SQL DROP TABLE statement is utilized to delete a table definition along with all its data from a database. It\'s crucial to exercise caution when using this command as once a table is deleted, all the information it contains is permanently lost.

    Here\'s the syntax to drop a table from the database:

    DROP TABLE "table_name";

    Let\'s consider an example:

    First, we verify the "STUDENTS" table and then proceed to delete it from the database:

    DESC STUDENTS;

    Field Type Null Key Default Extra
    ID Int(11) NO PRI
    NAME Varchar(20) NO
    AGE Int(11) NO
    ADDRESS Varchar(25) YES NULL

    This command displays the structure of the "STUDENTS" table, confirming its existence in the database. We can now drop it using the following command:

    DROP TABLE STUDENTS;

    To confirm whether the table has been successfully dropped, we can use the following command:

    DESC STUDENTS;

    Upon execution, this command should return a message indicating that the table does not exist anymore.

    SQL DROP TABLE Example in MySQL

    DROP TABLE table_name;

    SQL DROP TABLE Example in Oracle

    DROP TABLE table_name;

    SQL DROP TABLE Example in Microsoft SQL Server

    DROP TABLE table_name;

    Understanding SQL DELETE TABLE Statement

    The DELETE statement in SQL is employed to remove rows from a table. To delete a specific row from a table, you can use a WHERE condition.

    DELETE FROM table_name [WHERE condition];

    However, if you omit the WHERE condition, it will delete all rows from the table.

    DELETE FROM table_name;

    While there are similar statements like DROP and TRUNCATE, they have some differences.

    Difference between DELETE and TRUNCATE statements:

    • The DELETE statement deletes rows from the table based on the condition defined by the WHERE clause or deletes all rows from the table when no condition is specified. However, it does not free the space occupied by the table.
    • The TRUNCATE statement, on the other hand, deletes all rows from the table and frees the space occupied by the table.

    Consider an "employee" table:

    Emp_id Name Address Salary
    1 Aryan Allahabad 22000
    2 Shurabhi Varanasi 13000
    3 Pappu Delhi 24000

    To truncate the table, execute the following query:

    TRUNCATE TABLE employee;

    Difference between DROP and TRUNCATE statements:

    When you use the DROP statement, it deletes the table\'s rows along with the table\'s definition, rendering all relationships with other tables invalid.

    When you drop a table:

    • Table structure will be dropped
    • Relationships will be dropped
    • Integrity constraints will be dropped
    • Access privileges will also be dropped

    In contrast, when you TRUNCATE a table, the table structure remains intact, and you will not encounter any of the above issues.

    Understanding SQL RENAME TABLE Statement

    In certain scenarios, database administrators and users may need to change the name of a table in the SQL database to provide a more relevant identifier. This can be accomplished using the RENAME TABLE or ALTER TABLE statement in SQL.

    The syntax for the RENAME statement in SQL is:

    RENAME old_table_name TO new_table_name;

    Let\'s explore some examples to demonstrate how to change the name of an SQL table using the RENAME statement:

    Example 1:

    Suppose we have a table named "Cars":

    Car Name Car Color Car Cost
    Hyundai Creta White 10,85,000
    Hyundai Venue White 9,50,000
    Hyundai i20 Red 9,00,000
    Kia Sonet White 10,00,000
    Kia Seltos Black 8,00,000
    Swift Dezire Red 7,95,000

    If we wish to rename the "Cars" table to "Car_2021_Details", we would execute the following SQL statement:

    RENAME Cars TO Car_2021_Details;

    Example 2:

    Consider a table named "Employee":

    Emp_Id Emp_Name Emp_Salary Emp_City
    201 Abhay 25000 Goa
    202 Ankit 45000 Delhi
    203 Bheem 30000 Goa
    204 Ram 29000 Goa
    205 Sumit 40000 Delhi

    To change the name of the "Employee" table to "Coding_Employees", we use the following RENAME statement:

    RENAME Employee TO Coding_Employees;

    The syntax for the ALTER TABLE statement in SQL to rename a table is:

    ALTER TABLE old_table_name RENAME TO new_table_name;

    Here are some examples demonstrating how to change the name of a table in the SQL database using the ALTER TABLE statement:

    Example 1:

    Suppose we have a table named "Bikes":

    Bike_Name Bike_Color Bike_Cost
    KTM DUKE Black 185,000
    Royal Enfield Black NULL
    Pulsar Red 90,0000
    Apache White NULL
    Livo Black 80,000
    KTM RC Red 195,000

    To rename the "Bikes" table to "Bikes_Details" using the ALTER TABLE statement, we execute:

    ALTER TABLE Bikes RENAME TO Bikes_Details;

    Example 2:

    Consider a table named "Student":

    Stu_ID Stu_Name Stu_Marks
    1001 Abhay 85
    1002 Ankit 75
    1003 Bheem 60
    1004 Ram 79
    1005 Sumit 80

    To change the name of the "Student" table to "MCA_Student_Details" using ALTER TABLE, we use the following statement:

    ALTER TABLE Student RENAME TO MCA_Student_Details;

    Example 3:

    Suppose we have a table named "Employee":

    Emp_Id Emp_Name Emp_Salary Emp_City
    201 Abhay 25000 Goa
    202 Ankit 45000 Delhi
    203 Bheem 30000 Goa
    204 Ram 29000 Goa
    205 Sumit 40000 Delhi

    To rename the "Employee" table to "Coding_Employees" using the ALTER TABLE statement, we execute:

    ALTER TABLE Employee RENAME TO Coding_Employees;

    These examples demonstrate how to change the name of a table in the SQL database using both the RENAME TABLE and ALTER TABLE statements.

    SQL TRUNCATE TABLE

    The TRUNCATE TABLE statement in SQL is utilized to remove all rows, i.e., complete data, from a table. It functions similarly to the DELETE statement but does not require a WHERE clause.

    Comparison: TRUNCATE TABLE vs DELETE TABLE

    TRUNCATE TABLE is faster and consumes fewer resources compared to the DELETE TABLE command.

    Comparison: TRUNCATE TABLE vs DROP TABLE

    While both TRUNCATE TABLE and DROP TABLE can be used to delete an entire table, there are key differences. The DROP TABLE command removes the table\'s structure entirely, whereas TRUNCATE TABLE only removes the table\'s data, leaving the structure intact.

    Here\'s the syntax to truncate a table from the database:

    TRUNCATE TABLE table_name;

    For example, to truncate the data in the "Employee" table, you would execute the following command:

    TRUNCATE TABLE Employee;

    It\'s essential to note that the rollback process is not possible after executing the TRUNCATE TABLE statement. Once a table is truncated, you cannot use a flashback table statement to retrieve the content of the table.

    SQL COPY TABLE

    To copy data from one SQL table to another within the same SQL server, you can use the SELECT INTO statement in SQL. This statement copies the content from an existing table into a new table, utilizing the structure of the existing table.

    Syntax of SELECT INTO statement in SQL:

    SELECT * INTO New_table_name FROM old_table_name;

    Examples of SELECT INTO statement in SQL:

    Example 1: Suppose we have a table named "Cars" with three columns:

    Car Name Car Color Car Cost
    Hyundai Creta White 10,85,000
    Hyundai Venue White 9,50,000
    Hyundai i20 Red 9,00,000
    Kia Sonet White 10,00,000
    Kia Seltos Black 8,00,000
    Swift Dezire Red 7,95,000

    If we want to copy the content of the "Cars" table into a new table named "Car_Details", the SQL query would be:

    SELECT * INTO Car_Details FROM Cars;

    Example 2: Consider a table named "Employee" with four columns:

    Emp_Id Emp_Name Emp_Salary Emp_City
    201 Abhay 25000 Goa
    202 Ankit 45000 Delhi
    203 Bheem 30000 Goa
    204 Ram 29000 Goa
    205 Sumit 40000 Delhi

    To copy the records from the "Employee" table into a new table named "Coding_Employees", we execute:

    SELECT * INTO Coding_Employees FROM Employee;

    Example 3: Suppose we have a table named "Student" with four columns:

    RollNo Name Marks Age
    1001 Bhanu 88 17
    1002 Raman 82 16
    1003 Sumit 80 16
    1004 Shobhit 95 15
    1005 Akash 85 16

    To copy the records from the "Student" table into a new table named "Class_12_Students", we execute:

    SELECT * INTO Class_12_Students FROM Student;

    Example 4: Suppose we have a table named "Cars" with three columns:

    Car Name Car Color Car Cost
    Hyundai Creta White 10,85,000
    Hyundai Venue White 9,50,000
    Hyundai i20 Red 9,00,000
    Kia Sonet White 10,00,000
    Kia Seltos Black 8,00,000
    Swift Dezire Red 7,95,000

    To copy only the "Car_Color" and "Car_Name" columns from the "Cars" table into a new table named "Car_Color", we execute:

    SELECT Car_Name, Car_Color INTO Car_Color FROM Cars;

    These examples demonstrate how to copy the content of one table into another in SQL using the SELECT INTO statement.

    SQL TEMPORARY TABLE

    Temporary tables in SQL offer developers a flexible and efficient way to work with data during runtime. They can perform various operations like any regular table and are stored within the tempdb database. Temporary tables come in two types based on behavior and scope: local and global.

    Local Temporary Tables:

    Local temporary tables are only accessible during the current connection session. They are automatically deleted when the user disconnects from the database. These tables are prefixed with a hash (#) sign.

    CREATE TABLE #local_temp_table ( User_id INT, Username VARCHAR(50), User_address VARCHAR(150) );

    Global Temporary Tables:

    Global temporary tables, indicated by a double hash prefix (##), behave more like permanent tables. Once created, they persist beyond the current connection session and remain available to all users until all connections referencing them are terminated.

    CREATE TABLE ##global_temp_table ( User_id INT, Username VARCHAR(50), User_address VARCHAR(150) );

    SQL ALTER TABLE

    The ALTER TABLE statement in Structured Query Language allows you to add, modify, and delete columns of an existing table. This statement also allows database users to add and remove various SQL constraints on the existing tables.

    Any user can also change the name of the table using this statement.

    ALTER TABLE ADD Column statement in SQL

    In many situations, you may require to add the columns in the existing table. Instead of creating a whole table or database again you can easily add single and multiple columns using the ADD keyword.

    Syntax of ALTER TABLE ADD Column statement in SQL

    ALTER TABLE table_name ADD column_name column-definition;

    The above syntax only allows you to add a single column to the existing table. If you want to add more than one column to the table in a single SQL statement, then use the following syntax:

    ALTER TABLE table_name ADD (column_Name1 column-definition, column_Name2 column-definition, ..... column_NameN column-definition);

    Examples of ALTER TABLE ADD Column statement in SQL

    Here, we have taken the following two different SQL examples, which will help you how to add the single and multiple columns in the existing table using ALTER TABLE statement:

    Example 1: Let\'s take an example of a table named Cars:

    Car Name Car Color Car Cost
    Hyundai Creta White 10,85,000
    Hyundai Venue White 9,50,000

    Suppose, you want to add the new column Car_Model in the above table. For this, you have to type the following query in the SQL:

    ALTER TABLE Cars ADD Car_Model Varchar(20);

    This statement will add the Car_Model column to the Cars table.

    Example 2: Let\'s take an example of a table named Employee:

    Emp_Id Emp_Name Emp_Salary Emp_City
    201 Abhay 25000 Goa
    202 Ankit 45000 Delhi

    Suppose, you want to add two columns, Emp_ContactNo. and Emp_EmailID, in the above Employee table. For this, you have to type the following query in the SQL:

    ALTER TABLE Employee ADD ( Emp_ContactNo. Number(13), Emp_EmailID varchar(50) ;

    This statement will add Emp_ContactNo. and Emp_EmailID columns to the Employee table.

    ALTER TABLE MODIFY Column statement in SQL

    The MODIFY keyword is used for changing the column definition of the existing table.

    Syntax of ALTER TABLE MODIFY Column statement in SQL

    ALTER TABLE table_name MODIFY column_name column-definition;

    This syntax only allows you to modify a single column of the existing table. If you want to modify more than one column of the table in a single SQL statement, then use the following syntax:

    ALTER TABLE table_name MODIFY (column_Name1 column-definition, column_Name2 column-definition, ..... column_NameN column-definition);

    Examples of ALTER TABLE MODIFY Column statement in SQL

    Here, we have taken the following two different SQL examples, which will help you how to modify single and multiple columns of the existing table using ALTER TABLE statement:

    Example 1: Let\'s take an example of a table named Cars:

    Car Name Car Color Car Cost
    Hyundai Creta White 10,85,000
    Hyundai Venue White 9,50,000

    Suppose, you want to modify the datatype of the Car_Color column of the above table. For this, you have to type the following query in the SQL:

    ALTER TABLE Cars ADD Car_Color Varchar(50);

    Example 2: Let\'s take an example of a table named Employee:

    Emp_Id Emp_Name Emp_Salary Emp_City
    201 Abhay 25000 Goa
    202 Ankit 45000 Delhi

    Suppose, you want to modify the datatypes of two columns Emp_ContactNo. and Emp_EmailID of the above Employee table. For this, you have to type the following query in the SQL:

    ALTER TABLE Employee ADD ( Emp_ContactNo. Int, Emp_EmailID varchar(80) ;

    ALTER TABLE DROP Column statement in SQL

    In many situations, you may require to delete the columns from the existing table. Instead of deleting the whole table or database you can use DROP keyword for deleting the columns.

    Syntax of ALTER TABLE DROP Column statement in SQL

    ALTER TABLE table_name DROP Column column_name ;

    Examples of ALTER TABLE DROP Column statement in SQL

    Here, we have taken the following two different SQL examples, which will help you how to delete a column from the existing table using ALTER TABLE statement:

    Example 1: Let\'s take an example of a table named Cars:

    Car Name Car Color Car Cost
    Hyundai Creta White 10,85,000
    Hyundai Venue White 9,50,000

    Suppose, you want to delete the Car_Color column from the above table. For this, you have to type the following query in the SQL:

    ALTER TABLE Cars DROP COLUMN Car_Color ;

    Let\'s check using the following statement that the Car_Color column is deleted from the table or not:

    SELECT * FROM Cars;

    Example 2: Let\'s take an example of a table named Employee:

    Emp_Id Emp_Name Emp_Salary Emp_City
    201 Abhay 25000 Goa
    202 Ankit 45000 Delhi

    Suppose, you want to delete the Emp_Salary and Emp_City column from the above Employee table. For this, you have to type the following two different queries in the SQL:

    ALTER TABLE Cars DROP COLUMN Emp_Salary ; ALTER TABLE Cars DROP COLUMN Emp_City ;

    ALTER TABLE RENAME Column statement in SQL

    The RENAME keyword is used for changing the name of columns or fields of the existing table.

    Syntax of ALTER TABLE RENAME Column statement in SQL

    ALTER TABLE table_name RENAME COLUMN old_name to new_name;

    Examples of ALTER TABLE RENAME Column statement in SQL

    Here, we have taken the following two different SQL examples, which will help you how to change the name of a column of the existing table using ALTER TABLE statement:

    Example 1: Let\'s take an example of a table named Cars:

    Car Name Car Color Car Cost
    Hyundai Creta White 10,85,000
    Hyundai Venue White 9,50,000

    Suppose, you want to change the name of the Car_Color column of the above Cars table. For this, you have to type the following query in the SQL:

    ALTER TABLE Cars RENAME COLUMN Car_Color to Colors;

    This statement will change the name of a column of the Cars table. To see the changes, you have to type the following query:

    SELECT * FROM Cars;

    Example 2: Let\'s take an example of a table named Employee:

    Emp_Id Emp_Name Emp_Salary Emp_City
    201 Abhay 25000 Goa
    202 Ankit 45000 Delhi

    Suppose, you want to change the name of the Emp_City column of the above Employee table. For this, you have to type the following query in the SQL:

    ALTER TABLE Employee RENAME COLUMN Emp_City to Emp_Address;

    This statement will change the name of a column of the Employee table. To see the changes, you have to type the following query:

    SELECT * FROM Employee;

    SQL Select Statement

    The SELECT statement is a fundamental command in Structured Query Language (SQL) used to retrieve records from one or more database tables and views. It allows the user to access specific data from columns within a table, and the results are stored in what is known as a result-set table.

    Syntax of SELECT Statement in SQL:

    SELECT Column_Name_1, Column_Name_2, ..., Column_Name_N FROM Table_Name;

    To retrieve all rows from all fields of a table, the following syntax with the asterisk sign (*) is used:

    SELECT * FROM table_name;

    Examples of SELECT Statement in SQL:

    Example 1:

    CREATE TABLE Student_Records ( Student_Id INT PRIMARY KEY, First_Name VARCHAR(20), Address VARCHAR(20), Age INT NOT NULL, Percentage INT NOT NULL, Grade VARCHAR(10) ); INSERT INTO Student_Records VALUES (201, \'Akash\', \'Delhi\', 18, 89, \'A2\'), (202, \'Bhavesh\', \'Kanpur\', 19, 93, \'A1\'), (203, \'Yash\', \'Delhi\', 20, 89, \'A2\'), (204, \'Bhavna\', \'Delhi\', 19, 78, \'B1\'), (205, \'Yatin\', \'Lucknow\', 20, 75, \'B1\'), (206, \'Ishika\', \'Ghaziabad\', 19, 51, \'C1\'), (207, \'Vivek\', \'Goa\', 20, 62, \'B2\'); SELECT * FROM Student_Records;

    Student_ID First_Name Address Age Percentage Grade
    201 Akash Delhi 18 89 A2
    202 Bhavesh Kanpur 19 93 A1
    203 Yash Delhi 20 89 A2
    204 Bhavna Delhi 19 78 B1
    205 Yatin Lucknow 20 75 B1
    206 Ishika Ghaziabad 19 91 C1
    207 Vivek Goa 20 80 B2

    Example 2:

    SELECT Student_Id, Age, Percentage, Grade FROM Student_Records;

    SELECT Statement with WHERE clause:

    Example:

    CREATE TABLE Employee_Details ( Employee_ID INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, Emp_Name VARCHAR(50), Emp_City VARCHAR(20), Emp_Salary INT NOT NULL, Emp_Penalty INT NOT NULL ); INSERT INTO Employee_Details (Employee_ID, Emp_Name, Emp_City, Emp_Salary, Emp_Penalty) VALUES (101, \'Anuj\', \'Ghaziabad\', 25000, 500), (102, \'Tushar\', \'Lucknow\', 29000, 1000), (103, \'Vivek\', \'Kolkata\', 35000, 500), (104, \'Shivam\', \'Goa\', 22000, 500); SELECT * FROM Employee_Details WHERE Emp_Penalty = 500;

    Employee_Id Emp_Name Emp_City Emp_Salary Emp_Penalty
    101 Anuj Ghaziabad 25000 500
    103 Vivek Kolkata 35000 500
    104 Shivam Goa 22000 500

    SQL SELECT Statement with GROUP BY clause:

    Example:

    CREATE TABLE Cars_Details ( Car_Number INT PRIMARY KEY, Car_Name VARCHAR(50), Car_Price INT NOT NULL, Car_Amount INT NOT NULL ); INSERT INTO Cars_Details (Car_Number, Car_Name, Car_Amount, Car_Price) VALUES (2578, \'Creta\', 3, 1500000), (9258, \'Audi\', 2, 3000000), (8233, \'Venue\', 6, 900000), (6214, \'Nexon\', 7, 1000000);

    Car_Number Car_Name Car_Amount Car_Price
    2578 Creta 3 1000000
    9258 Audi 2 900000

    6214 Nexon 7 1000000

    SELECT COUNT(Car_Name), Car_Price FROM Cars_Details GROUP BY Car_Price;

    2 1000000
    2 900000

    SQL SELECT Statement with HAVING clause:

    Example:

    CREATE TABLE Employee_Having ( Employee_Id INT PRIMARY KEY, Employee_Name VARCHAR(50), Employee_Salary INT NOT NULL, Employee_City VARCHAR(50) ); INSERT INTO Employee_Having (Employee_Id, Employee_Name, Employee_Salary, Employee_City) VALUES (201, \'Jone\', 20000, \'Goa\'), (202, \'Basant\', 40000, \'Delhi\'), (203, \'Rashet\', 80000, \'Jaipur\'), (204, \'Anuj\', 20000, \'Goa\'), (205, \'Sumit\', 50000, \'Delhi\');

    204 Anuj 20000 Goa

    SELECT SUM(Employee_Salary), Employee_City FROM Employee_Having GROUP BY Employee_City HAVING SUM(Employee_Salary) > 5000;

    SQL SELECT UNIQUE

    The terms DISTINCT and UNIQUE are effectively synonymous in SQL.

    SELECT UNIQUE was a syntax previously used in Oracle, but the ANSI standard later established DISTINCT as the official keyword for retrieving unique or distinct elements from a table.

    Despite this standardization, Oracle retained the UNIQUE keyword for backward compatibility purposes.

    In essence, both SELECT UNIQUE and SELECT DISTINCT serve the same purpose: to retrieve unique or distinct elements from a table.

    Syntax of SELECT UNIQUE statement:

    SELECT UNIQUE column_name FROM table_name;

    Alternatively, the SQL SELECT DISTINCT statement can also achieve the same result.

    The SQL DISTINCT command is utilized with the SELECT keyword to retrieve only unique or distinct data from a table.

    In instances where duplicate values exist in a table, it may be necessary to retrieve only the unique values. This is where the SQL SELECT DISTINCT statement comes into play.

    Note: The SQL SELECT UNIQUE and SQL SELECT DISTINCT statements are essentially identical.

    Syntax of SELECT DISTINCT statement:

    SELECT DISTINCT column_name, column_name FROM table_name;

    Let\'s illustrate this with an example using the table provided below:

    Student_NameGenderMobile_NumberHOME_TOWN
    Rahul OjhaMale7503896532Lucknow
    Disha RaiFemale9270568893Varanasi
    Sonoo JaiswalMale9990449935Lucknow

    Suppose we wish to retrieve distinct home towns from this table. We would use the following query:

    SELECT DISTINCT HOME_TOWN FROM students;

    Executing this query would return two rows:

    HOME_TOWN
    Lucknow
    Varanasi

    The SQL COUNT() function is used to retrieve the number of records in a table.

    This function is typically employed with the SQL SELECT statement.

    Let\'s consider a simple scenario: If you have a list of voters in a particular area and need to determine the total number of voters, manually counting them could be challenging. However, using the SQL SELECT COUNT query makes this task effortless.

    Syntax of SELECT COUNT Function in SQL:

    SELECT COUNT(column_name) FROM table_name;

    In the syntax, the column\'s name is specified after the COUNT keyword, along with the name of the table on which the COUNT function is to be executed.

    Examples of SELECT COUNT Function in SQL

    Below are two SQL examples demonstrating the application of the COUNT function in a query:

    Example 1:

    Consider a table named Bike with three columns:

    Bike_NameBike_ColorBike_Cost
    PulsarBlack185,000
    ApacheBlackNULL
    KTM RCRed900,000
    Royal EnfieldWhiteNULL
    LivoBlack80,000
    KTM DUKERed195,000

    If you want to count the total number of bike colors from the Bike table, you would execute the following SQL statement:

    SELECT COUNT(Bike_Color) AS TotalBikeColor FROM Bike;

    This query would return the following output:

    TotalBikeColor
    6

    The output indicates six distinct bike colors because the Bike_Color column does not contain any NULL values.

    Now, if you want to count the total values in the Bike_Cost column, you would use:

    SELECT COUNT(Bike_Cost) AS TotalBikeCost FROM Bike;

    This query would return:

    TotalBikeCost
    4

    Here, the output is four because two values in the Bike_Cost column are NULL and are excluded from the count function.

    Example 2:

    Consider a table named Employee_Details with four columns:

    Emp_IdEmp_NameEmp_SalaryEmp_City
    2001Saurabh25,000NULL
    2002Ram29,000Delhi
    2003Sumit30,000NULL
    2004Ankit45,000Goa
    2005Bheem40,000NULL

    To count the total values in the Emp_City column, you would use:

    SELECT COUNT(Emp_City) AS TotalCity FROM Employee_Details;

    This would return:

    TotalCity
    2

    Here, the output is two because three values in the Emp_City column are NULL and are excluded from the count function.

    SELECT COUNT(*) Function in SQL

    The COUNT(*) function in SQL counts all the null and non-null records present in the table.

    Syntax of COUNT(*) Function in SQL:

    SELECT COUNT(*) FROM table_name;

    Example of COUNT(*) Function in SQL

    Consider the Bike table again:

    Bike_NameBike_ColorBike_Cost
    LivoBlack185,000
    ApacheRedNULL
    PulsarRed90,000
    Royal EnfieldBlackNULL
    KTM DUKEBlack80,000
    KTM RCWhite195,000

    To count the total number of records from the Bike table, you would use:

    SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Bike;

    This would return:

    Count(*)
    6

    SQL COUNT() Function With WHERE Clause

    The COUNT() function can also be used with the WHERE clause to filter records based on specified criteria.

    Syntax of COUNT() Function With WHERE clause in SQL:

    SELECT COUNT(column_name) FROM table_name WHERE [condition];

    Examples of COUNT Function With WHERE clause in SQL

    Consider the Bike table again:

    Bike_NameBike_ColorBike_Cost
    ApacheBlack90,000
    LivoBlackNULL
    KTM RCRed185,000
    KTM DUKEWhiteNULL
    Royal EnfieldRed80,000
    PulsarBlack195,000

    To count the total number of bikes whose color is black, you would use:

    SELECT COUNT(Bike_Name) AS TotalBikeBlackColor FROM Bike WHERE Bike_Color = \'Black\';

    This would return:

    TotalBikeBlackColor
    3

    To count the total number of employees who belong to the city of Delhi, you would use:

    SELECT COUNT(Emp_Name) AS TotalEmpCity FROM Employee_Details WHERE Emp_City = \'Delhi\';

    This would return:

    TotalEmpCity
    1

    SQL COUNT Function With DISTINCT keyword

    The DISTINCT keyword with the COUNT function counts only the number of unique rows of a column.

    Syntax of COUNT Function With DISTINCT keyword in SQL:

    SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT column_name) FROM table_name WHERE [condition];

    Examples of COUNT Function With DISTINCT keyword in SQL

    Consider the Cars table:

    Car_NameCar_ColorCar_Cost
    i20White1,085,000
    Hyundai VenueBlack950,000
    Swift DezireRed900,000
    Hyundai CretaWhite795,000
    Kia SeltosWhite800,000
    Kia SonetRed1,000,000

    To count the unique colors of cars from the Cars table, you would use:

    SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT Car_Color) AS Unique_Car_Color FROM Cars;

    This would return:

    Unique_Car_Color
    3

    To count the unique values in the Emp_Salary field from the Employee_Details table, you would use:

    SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT Emp_Salary) AS Unique_Salary FROM Employee_Details;

    This would return:

    Unique_Salary
    4

    SQL SELECT TOP

    The SELECT TOP statement in SQL is used to retrieve a limited number of records or rows from a database table. The TOP clause specifies how many rows are returned.

    Syntax of TOP Clause in SQL:

    SELECT TOP number | percent column_Name1, column_Name2, ....., column_NameN FROM table_name WHERE [Condition] ;

    In the syntax:

    • number

      denotes the number of rows to be shown from the top in the output.

    • column_Name

      denotes the column whose records are desired in the output.
    • We can also specify conditions using the WHERE clause.

    Examples of TOP Clause in SQL

    Here are four SQL examples demonstrating the use of the TOP clause:

    Example 1:

    Consider a table named Cars with three columns:

    Car NameCar ColorCar Cost
    Hyundai CretaWhite10,85,000
    Hyundai VenueWhite9,50,000
    Hyundai i20Red9,00,000
    Kia SonetWhite10,00,000
    Kia SeltosBlack8,00,000
    Swift DezireRed7,95,000

    To show the first three Names and Colors of Cars from the table, you would execute:

    SELECT TOP 3 Car_Name, Car_Color FROM Cars;

    This would output:

    Car NameCar Color
    Hyundai CretaWhite
    Hyundai VenueWhite
    Hyundai i20Red

    Example 2:

    Consider a table named Employee with four columns:

    Emp_IdEmp_NameEmp_SalaryEmp_City
    201Abhay25,000Goa
    202Ankit45,000Delhi
    203Bheem30,000Goa
    204Ram29,000Goa
    205Sumit40,000Delhi

    To show details of the first four employees from Goa, you would execute:

    SELECT TOP 4 * FROM Employee WHERE Emp_City = \'Goa\';

    This would output:

    Emp_IdEmp_NameEmp_SalaryEmp_City
    201Abhay25,000Goa
    203Bheem30,000Goa
    204Ram29,000Goa

    Syntax of ROWNUM Keyword in WHERE Clause in Oracle:

    SELECT column_Name1,column_Name2, ....., column_NameN FROM table_name WHERE ROWNUM <= value;

    Example of ROWNUM Keyword in WHERE Clause in Oracle:

    Consider the Cars table again. To show the first three records of Cars using the ROWNUM keyword in Oracle, you would execute:

    SELECT * FROM Cars WHERE ROWNUM <= 3;

    This would output the first three records of the table.

    SQL SELECT FIRST

    The SQL FIRST() function is utilized to retrieve the first value of the selected column. However, it\'s important to note that the FIRST() function is only supported by MS Access.

    Syntax of SQL SELECT FIRST() function:

    SELECT FIRST(column_name) FROM table_name;

    If you want to retrieve the first value of the

    customer_name

    column from the

    customers

    table, you would use the following query:

    SELECT FIRST(customer_name) AS first_customer FROM customers;

    Example using the CUSTOMERS table:

    Consider the CUSTOMERS table:

    CUSTOMER_NAMEAGEADDRESSEXPENDITURE
    KAMAL SHARMA26GHAZIABAD6000
    ROBERT PETT23NEWYORK26000
    SHIKHA SRIVASTAV22DELHI9000

    To retrieve the first value of the

    CUSTOMER_NAME

    column from the

    customers

    table, you would execute:

    SELECT FIRST(CUSTOMER_NAME) AS first_customer FROM CUSTOMERS;

    This query would result in:

    first_customer
    KAMAL SHARMA

    SQL SELECT LAST

    The SQL LAST() function is employed to retrieve the last value from the specified column of a table. However, it\'s important to note that this SQL function is only supported in Microsoft Access database. Other databases like Oracle and MySQL have alternative methods for achieving similar results, such as using ORDER BY and LIMIT clauses.

    Syntax of the LAST() Function:

    SELECT LAST(Field_Name) FROM Table_Name;

    In the syntax:

    • LAST keyword denotes the last row to be shown from the table in the output.
    • Field_Name denotes the column whose value we want to retrieve.

    Example using the CUSTOMERS table:

    Consider the CUSTOMERS table:

    CUSTOMER_NAMEAGEADDRESSEXPENDITURE
    KAMAL SHARMA26GHAZIABAD6000
    ROBERT PETT23NEWYORK26000
    SHIKHA SRIVASTAV22DELHI9000

    To retrieve the last value of the

    CUSTOMER_NAME

    column from the

    CUSTOMERS

    table, you would execute:

    SELECT LAST(CUSTOMER_NAME) AS Last_Customer FROM CUSTOMERS;

    This query would result in:

    Last_Customer
    SHIKHA SRIVASTAV

    For databases like MySQL and Oracle, the equivalent syntax would involve using ORDER BY and LIMIT or ROWNUM in the query.

    Syntax of LIMIT Clause in MySQL:

    SELECT column_Name FROM Table_Name ORDER BY Column_Name DESC LIMIT 1;

    Example using MySQL:

    SELECT Emp_City FROM Employee ORDER BY Emp_City DESC LIMIT 1;

    This query would return the last value of the

    Emp_City

    column from the

    Employee

    table.

    Syntax of ROWNUM keyword in Oracle:

    SELECT Column_Name FROM Table_Name ORDER BY Column_Name DESC WHERE ROWNUM <= 1;

    Example using Oracle:

    SELECT Car_Name FROM Cars ORDER BY Car_Name DESC WHERE ROWNUM <= 1;

    This query would retrieve the last name of the car from the

    Car_Name

    column of the

    Cars

    table.

    SQL SELECT RANDOM

    The SQL SELECT RANDOM() function retrieves a random row from a database table. It\'s commonly utilized in scenarios like online exams where random questions need to be displayed.

    Selecting a random record from a table can be achieved using various SQL syntaxes, each specific to different database servers.

    MySQL Syntax:

    SELECT column FROM table ORDER BY RAND() LIMIT 1;

    This query randomly selects one row from the table by ordering the rows randomly and then limiting the result to just one row.

    Microsoft SQL Server Syntax:

    SELECT TOP 1 column FROM table ORDER BY NEWID();

    In Microsoft SQL Server, NEWID() generates a unique value of type uniqueidentifier. By ordering the rows using NEWID(), a random row is selected.

    Oracle Syntax:

    SELECT column FROM (SELECT column FROM table ORDER BY dbms_random.value) WHERE rownum = 1;

    Oracle\'s dbms_random.value function generates a random number. By ordering the rows by this random number and then selecting the first row using rownum, a random row is obtained.

    PostgreSQL Syntax:

    SELECT column FROM table ORDER BY RANDOM() LIMIT 1;

    In PostgreSQL, the RANDOM() function is used to generate a random value. By ordering the rows by this random value and then limiting the result to one row, a random row is selected.

    SQL SELECT IN

    The IN operator in SQL is used within a query to streamline the process of specifying multiple conditions, thereby reducing the need for numerous OR conditions. It is applicable in SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statements.

    Advantages of SQL SELECT IN:

    • It simplifies the use of multiple OR operators.

    Syntax for SQL IN:

    Expression IN (value1, value2, ..., valuen);

    Example:

    Suppose we have a table named students and we want to retrieve records where the student_name matches any of the specified names: Amit, Raghav, or Rajeev.

    Query:

    SELECT * FROM students WHERE student_name IN (\'Amit\', \'Raghav\', \'Rajeev\');

    Example with Numeric Values:

    Consider a table named marks, and we want to retrieve records where the roll_no matches any of the specified values: 001, 023, or 024.

    Query:

    SELECT * FROM marks WHERE roll_no IN (001, 023, 024);

    SQL SELECT from Multiple Tables

    Retrieving fields from multiple tables in SQL involves using join queries to gather data from different sources.

    Example:

    SELECT orders.order_id, suppliers.name FROM suppliers INNER JOIN orders ON suppliers.supplier_id = orders.supplier_id ORDER BY order_id;

    Now, let\'s consider three tables: customer1, customer2, and product.

    customer1 table:

    Cus_id Name1
    1 Jack
    2 Jill

    customer2 table:

    Cus_id Name2
    1 Sandy
    2 Venus

    product table:

    P_id Cus_id P_name
    1 1 Laptop
    2 2 Phone
    3 P1 Pen
    4 P2 Notebook

    Example Syntax to SELECT from Multiple Tables:

    SELECT p.p_id, p.cus_id, p.p_name, c1.name1, c2.name2 FROM product AS p LEFT JOIN customer1 AS c1 ON p.cus_id=c1.cus_id LEFT JOIN customer2 AS c2 ON p.cus_id = c2.cus_id;

    This query retrieves data from the product table and left joins it with both customer1 and customer2 tables based on the common cus_id column, allowing us to combine information from multiple sources into one result set.

    Result:

    P_id Cus_id P_name Name1 Name2
    1 1 Laptop Jack
    2 2 Phone Jill
    3 P1 Pen Sandy
    4 P2 Notebook Venus

    SQL SELECT DATE Examples

    To retrieve all records after \'2013-12-12\':

    SELECT * FROM table_name WHERE your_date_column >= \'2013-12-12\';

    To retrieve records after \'2013-12-12\' and before \'2013-12-13\':

    SELECT * FROM table_name WHERE your_date_column < \'2013-12-13\' AND your_date_column >= \'2013-12-12\';

    If you want to compare dates within the query using BETWEEN operator:

    SELECT * FROM table_name WHERE your_date_column BETWEEN \'2012-12-12\' AND \'2013-12-12\';

    For finding records for a particular date:

    SELECT * FROM table_name WHERE CAST(DATEDIFF(day, 0, your_date) AS datetime) = \'2012-12-12\';

    SQL SELECT SUM Examples

    Example 1:

    Suppose we have a table named employees with columns ID, EMPLOYEE_NAME, and SALARY. To find the total salary of all employees whose salary is above 20000:

    SELECT SUM(salary) AS "Total Salary" FROM employees WHERE salary > 20000;

    Example 2:

    You can also use DISTINCT with SUM() to calculate the sum of distinct values:

    SELECT SUM(DISTINCT salary) AS "Total Salary" FROM employees WHERE salary > 20000;

    Example 3:

    Using GROUP BY with SUM() can be useful to calculate the sum for each group:

    Suppose we have a table named order_details with columns ID, DEPARTMENT, DATE, and DAILY_SALES. To calculate the total sales for each department:

    SELECT DEPARTMENT, SUM(DAILY_SALES) AS "Total Sales" FROM order_details GROUP BY DEPARTMENT;

    This will provide a result with the total sales for each department:

    DEPARTMENTTotal Sales
    Mechanical360
    Electrical210
    Electronics320

    SQL SELECT NULL Examples

    Selecting Records with NULL Values:

    To select records where a particular column has NULL values, you can use the IS NULL condition in the WHERE clause.

    Example:

    Suppose we have a students table with columns SIR_NAME, NAME, and MARKS. To retrieve records where the MARKS column is NULL:

    SELECT SIR_NAME, NAME, MARKS FROM STUDENTS WHERE MARKS IS NULL;

    This will return records where the MARKS column has NULL values:

    SIR_NAMENAMEMARKS
    SHARMAAMAR
    TYAGISEEMA

    Selecting Records with Non-NULL Values:

    To select records where a particular column has non-NULL values, you can use the IS NOT NULL condition in the WHERE clause.

    Example:

    To retrieve records where the MARKS column is not NULL:

    SELECT SIR_NAME, NAME, MARKS FROM STUDENTS WHERE MARKS IS NOT NULL;

    This will return records where the MARKS column has non-NULL values:

    SIR_NAMENAMEMARKS
    SINGHRAMAN5.5
    JAISWALVICKY6.2

    SQL WHERE Clause

    In SQL, the WHERE clause is used to filter records based on specified conditions. It allows you to retrieve or manipulate only those records that meet the specified criteria.

    Syntax:

    SELECT column1, column2, ... columnN FROM table_name WHERE condition;

    Conditional Operators:

    • = : Equal to
    • > : Greater than
    • < : Less than
    • >= : Greater than or equal to
    • <= : Less than or equal to
    • <> or != : Not equal to

    Example:

    Suppose we have a students table with columns student_id, name, and age. We can use the WHERE clause to filter records based on specific conditions.

    Equal to:

    SELECT * FROM students WHERE age = 20;

    SQL AND

    The SQL AND condition is used to specify multiple conditions in a SQL query, where all conditions must be met for a row to be included in the result set.

    Syntax:

    SELECT columns FROM tables WHERE condition1 AND condition2;

    The AND operator requires that both conditions specified in the WHERE clause be true for a row to be returned.

    Example:

    Suppose we have a table named employees with columns employee_id, first_name, last_name, and department. We want to retrieve employees who work in the IT department and are located in Mumbai.

    ID First_Name Last_Name Department Location
    1 Harshad Kuwar Marketing Pune
    2 Anurag Rajput IT Mumbai
    3 Chaitali Tarle IT Chennai
    4 Pranjal Patil IT Chennai
    5 Suraj Tripathi Marketing Pune
    6 Roshni Jadhav Finance Bangalore
    7 Sandhya Jain Finance Bangalore

    SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department = \'IT\' AND location = \'Mumbai\';

    3 Chaitali Tarle IT Chennai
    4 Pranjal Patil IT Chennai

    This query will return all employees who work in the IT department and are located in Mumbai.

    In the given example, the AND condition ensures that only rows where both conditions (department = \'IT\' and location = \'Mumbai\') are true are included in the result set.

    The output will contain employee details such as ID, first name, last name, department, and location.

    The SQL AND condition is essential for filtering data based on multiple criteria in SQL queries.

    SQL OR

    The SQL OR condition is utilized in SQL queries to retrieve records where at least one of the specified conditions is met. It can be applied in various SQL statements such as SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE.

    Syntax for the OR condition:

    SELECT columns FROM tables WHERE condition1 OR condition2;

    In the provided example, suppose we have a table named employees with columns ID, First_Name, Last_Name, Department, and Location. We aim to extract employees who either belong to the IT department or are located in Chennai.

    ID First_Name Last_Name Department Location
    1 Harshad Kuwar Marketing Pune
    2 Anurag Rajput IT Mumbai
    3 Chaitali Tarle IT Chennai
    4 Pranjal Patil IT Chennai
    5 Suraj Tripathi Marketing Pune
    6 Roshni Jadhav Finance Bangalore
    7 Sandhya Jain Finance Bangalore

    Example Query:

    SELECT * FROM employees WHERE Department = \'IT\' OR Location = \'Chennai\';

    This query will return records of employees who work in the IT department or are located in Chennai.

    ID First_Name Last_Name Department Location
    2 Anurag Rajput IT Mumbai
    3 Chaitali Tarle IT Chennai
    4 Pranjal Patil IT Chennai

    In the provided output, all three employees from the IT department are listed, as well as those located in Chennai. This outcome is due to the usage of the OR operator, which includes records in the result set if at least one condition is satisfied.

    The SQL OR condition offers flexibility in constructing queries to retrieve specific records based on multiple criteria.

    SQL WITH Clause Example

    The SQL WITH clause, also known as Common Table Expression (CTE), is utilized to define a temporary result set that can be referenced multiple times within the main SQL query. Introduced by Oracle in Oracle 9i Release 2 database, it enhances the readability and reusability of complex SQL queries.

    Syntax for the SQL WITH clause:

    When using a single sub-query alias:

    WITH <alias_name> AS (
        sql_sub-query_statement
    )
    SELECT column_list
    FROM <alias_name> [table name]
    [WHERE <join_condition>];



    When employing multiple sub-query aliases:

    WITH <alias_name_A> AS (
        sql_sub-query_statement
    ),
    <alias_name_B> AS (
        sql_sub-query_statement_from_alias_name_A
        Or sql_sub-query_statement
    )
    SELECT <column_list>
    FROM <alias_name_A>, <alias_name_B>, [tablenames]
    [WHERE <join_condition>];

    Example:

    WITH TotalSalary AS ( SELECT SUM(Salary) AS Total_Salary FROM employee ), AverageSalary AS ( SELECT AVG(Salary) AS Average_Salary FROM employee ) SELECT Total_Salary, Average_Salary FROM TotalSalary, AverageSalary;

    In this example, TotalSalary and AverageSalary are temporary result sets defined using the WITH clause. These result sets are then referenced in the main query to retrieve the total salary and average salary of employees.

    SQL SELECT AS

    The AS keyword in SQL is utilized to temporarily assign a new name to a table column or even to the table itself. This feature aids in presenting query results more clearly and accurately, without necessitating permanent changes to column or table names.

    Syntax of SELECT AS:

    SELECT Column_Name1 AS New_Column_Name, Column_Name2 AS New_Column_Name FROM Table_Name;

    In this syntax:

    • Column_Name1

      is the original name of a column in the table.

    • New_Column_Name

      is the temporary name assigned to that column for the duration of the query.

    Example:

    Consider a table named orders with the following data:

    Day_of_order Customer Product Quantity
    11-09-2001 Ajeet Mobile 2
    13-12-2001 Mayank Laptop 20
    26-12-2004 Balaswamy Water cannon 35

    Suppose we want to rename the Day_of_order column to Date and the Customer column to Client.

    Query:

    SELECT Day_of_order AS \'Date\', Customer AS \'Client\', Product, Quantity FROM orders;

    Result:

    Date Client Product Quantity
    11-09-2001 Ajeet Mobile 2
    13-12-2001 Mayank Laptop 20
    26-12-2004 Balaswamy Water cannon 35

    In the result, Day_of_order is temporarily renamed as Date and Customer as Client.

    Note: AS in SQL is synonymous with SQL ALIAS.

    SQL HAVING Clause

    The HAVING clause in SQL is utilized to filter records after grouping has been applied by the GROUP BY clause in a SELECT statement. It enables the application of conditions to the groups formed by the GROUP BY clause, allowing for more precise data filtering.

    Difference between HAVING and WHERE Clause:

    HAVINGWHERE
    1. Used to filter data from grouped results.1. Used to filter data from individual rows of the table.
    2. Always employed with the GROUP BY clause.2. Can be used independently of the GROUP BY clause.
    3. Aggregation functions can be used in conditions.3. Aggregation functions are not allowed in conditions.
    4. Used with SELECT statement for group filtering.4. Employed with SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE statements.
    5. Applied after the GROUP BY clause.5. Applied before the GROUP BY clause.
    6. Operates on grouped data.6. Operates on individual rows.
    7. Acts as a post-filter.7. Acts as a pre-filter.

    Syntax of HAVING clause in SQL:

    SELECT column1, column2, ..., columnN, aggregate_function(column_name)
    FROM table_name
    GROUP BY column1
    HAVING condition;

    Examples of HAVING clause in SQL:

    Example 1: Sum of Employee Salaries by City

    Consider the following Employee table:

    Emp_IdEmp_NameEmp_SalaryEmp_City
    201Abhay2000Goa
    202Ankit4000Delhi
    203Bheem8000Jaipur
    204Ram2000Goa
    205Sumit5000Delhi

    To calculate the total salary of employees in each city:

    SELECT SUM(Emp_Salary), Emp_City FROM Employee GROUP BY Emp_City;

    Output:

    SUM(Emp_Salary)Emp_City
    4000Goa
    9000Delhi
    8000Jaipur

    To filter cities with a total salary exceeding 5000:

    SELECT SUM(Emp_Salary), Emp_City FROM Employee GROUP BY Emp_City HAVING SUM(Emp_Salary) > 5000;

    Output:

    SUM(Emp_Salary)Emp_City
    9000Delhi
    8000Jaipur
    @string/sql_where @string/sql_and @string/sql_or @string/sql_with @string/sql_as @string/sql_having

    SQL ORDER BY Clause Example

    The SQL ORDER BY clause is used to sort the records retrieved from a table based on the values in one or more columns. It allows sorting the data either in ascending (ASC) or descending (DESC) order.

    Syntax:

    SELECT column1, column2, ... FROM table_name ORDER BY column1 [ASC | DESC], column2 [ASC | DESC], ...;

    Examples:

    Example 1: Sorting by Name in Ascending Order

    Consider the "customers" table:

    ID NAME AGE ADDRESS SALARY
    1 Himani Gupta 21 Modinagar 22000
    2 Shiva Tiwari 22 Bhopal 21000
    3 Ajeet Bhargav 45 Meerut 65000
    4 Ritesh Yadav 36 Azamgarh 26000
    5 Balwant Singh 45 Varanasi 36000
    6 Mahesh Sharma 26 Mathura 22000
    7 Rohit Shrivastav 19 Ahemdabad 38000
    8 Neeru Sharma 29 Pune 40000
    9 Aakash Yadav 32 Mumbai 43500
    10 Sahil Sheikh 35 Aurangabad 68800

    Query:

    SELECT * FROM customers ORDER BY NAME ASC;

    Output:

    ID Name Age Address Salary
    9 Aakash Yadav 32 Mumbai 43500
    3 Ajeet Bhargav 45 Meerut 65000
    5 Balwant Singh 45 Varanasi 36000
    1 Himani Gupta 21 Modinagar 22000
    6 Mahesh Sharma 26 Mathura 22000
    8 Neeru Sharma 29 Pune 40000
    4 Ritesh Yadav 36 Azamgarh 26000
    7 Rohit Shrivastav 19 Ahemdabad 38000
    10 Sahil Sheikh 35 Aurangabad 68800
    2 Shiva Tiwari 22 Bhopal 21000

    Example 2: Sorting by Salary in Descending Order

    Query:

    SELECT * FROM customers ORDER BY SALARY DESC;

    Output:

    IDNAMEAGEADDRESSSALARY
    2Shiva Tiwari22Bhopal21000
    10 Sahil Sheikh 35 Aurangabad 68800
    9 Aakash Yadav 32 Mumbai 43500
    8 Neeru Sharma 29 Pune 40000
    7 Rohit Shrivastav 19 Ahemdabad 38000
    6 Mahesh Sharma 26 Mathura 22000
    5 Balwant Singh 45 Varanasi 36000
    4 Ritesh Yadav 36 Azamgarh 26000
    3Ajeet Bhargav45Meerut65000
    2Shiva Tiwari22Bhopal21000
    1Himani Gupta21Modinagar22000

    SQL ORDER BY Clause with Ascending Order

    Whenever sorting records based on specific columns in a SQL database table, the ORDER BY clause is utilized. This clause assists in arranging records in either ascending or descending order based on the values in the specified column.

    Syntax:

    • To sort records in ascending order using the ASC keyword:

    SELECT ColumnName1, ..., ColumnNameN FROM TableName ORDER BY ColumnName ASC;

    • To sort records in ascending order without explicitly specifying ASC:

    SELECT ColumnName1, ..., ColumnNameN FROM TableName ORDER BY ColumnName;

    Example:

    Consider a "customers" table with the following records:

    IDNAMEAGEADDRESSSALARY
    1Himani Gupta21Modinagar22000
    2Shiva Tiwari22Bhopal21000
    3Ajeet Bhargav45Meerut65000
    4 Ritesh Yadav 36 Azamgarh 26000
    5 Balwant Singh 45 Varanasi 36000
    6 Mahesh Sharma 26 Mathura 22000
    7 Rohit Shrivastav 19 Ahemdabad 38000
    8 Neeru Sharma 29 Pune 40000
    9 Aakash Yadav 32 Mumbai 43500
    10 Sahil Sheikh 35 Aurangabad 68800

    Example 1: Sorting by Name in Ascending Order

    Query:

    SELECT * FROM customers ORDER BY NAME ASC;

    ID Name Age Address Salary
    9 Aakash Yadav 32 Mumbai 43500
    3 Ajeet Bhargav 45 Meerut 65000
    5 Balwant Singh 45 Varanasi 36000
    1 Himani Gupta 21 Modinagar 22000
    6 Mahesh Sharma 26 Mathura 22000
    8 Neeru Sharma 29 Pune 40000

    10 Sahil Sheikh 35 Aurangabad 68800

    SQL ORDER BY Clause with Descending Order

    Whenever we need to sort records based on specific columns in a SQL database table, we utilize the ORDER BY clause in SQL. The ORDER BY clause assists us in sorting records based on a table\'s specific column. Initially, all values stored in the column on which we apply the ORDER BY clause are sorted. Then, the corresponding column values are displayed in the sequence obtained in the previous step. Using the ORDER BY clause, we can sort records in ascending or descending order as per our requirement. Records are sorted in ascending order when the ASC keyword is used with the ORDER BY clause, while the DESC keyword sorts records in descending order. If no keyword is specified after the column based on which we sort the records, sorting is done by default in ascending order.

    Syntax:

    To sort records in descending order:

    SELECT ColumnName1, ..., ColumnNameN FROM TableName ORDER BY ColumnName DESC;

    Example:

    Consider a "customers" table with the following records:

    IDNAMEAGEADDRESSSALARY
    1Himani Gupta21Modinagar22000
    2Shiva Tiwari22Bhopal21000
    3Ajeet Bhargav45Meerut65000
    4 Ritesh Yadav 36 Azamgarh 26000
    5 Balwant Singh 45 Varanasi 36000
    6 Mahesh Sharma 26 Mathura 22000
    7 Rohit Shrivastav 19 Ahemdabad 38000
    8 Neeru Sharma 29 Pune 40000
    9 Aakash Yadav 32 Mumbai 43500
    10 Sahil Sheikh 35 Aurangabad 68800

    Example:

    Write a query to sort the records in descending order of customer names stored in the customers table.

    Query:

    SELECT * FROM customers ORDER BY Name DESC;

    IDNAMEAGEADDRESSSALARY
    2Shiva Tiwari22Bhopal21000
    10 Sahil Sheikh 35 Aurangabad 68800
    9 Aakash Yadav 32 Mumbai 43500
    8 Neeru Sharma 29 Pune 40000
    7 Rohit Shrivastav 19 Ahemdabad 38000
    6 Mahesh Sharma 26 Mathura 22000
    5 Balwant Singh 45 Varanasi 36000
    4 Ritesh Yadav 36 Azamgarh 26000
    3Ajeet Bhargav45Meerut65000
    2Shiva Tiwari22Bhopal21000
    1Himani Gupta21Modinagar22000

    All records in the customers table are displayed in descending order of customer names.

    SQL ORDER BY RANDOM

    When you require a random record or row from a database, it\'s often for scenarios where you want to display random information like articles, links, or pages to users.

    To retrieve random rows from various databases, different queries are used according to their syntax.

    Selecting a Random Row with MySQL:

    SELECT column FROM table ORDER BY RAND() LIMIT 1;

    Selecting a Random Row with PostgreSQL:

    SELECT column FROM table ORDER BY RANDOM() LIMIT 1;

    Selecting a Random Row with SQL Server:

    SELECT TOP 1 column FROM table ORDER BY NEWID();

    Selecting a Random Row with Oracle:

    SELECT column FROM (SELECT column FROM table ORDER BY dbms_random.value) WHERE rownum = 1;

    Selecting a Random Row with IBM DB2:

    SELECT column, RAND() as IDX FROM table ORDER BY IDX FETCH FIRST 1 ROWS ONLY;

    Let\'s understand with a practical example using the MySQL database. Suppose we have a table named "items" with the following data:

    IDItem_NameItem_QuantityItem_PricePurchase_Date
    1Soap52002021-07-08
    2Toothpaste2802021-07-10
    3Pen10502021-07-12
    4Bottle12502021-07-13
    5Brush3902021-07-15

    To retrieve any random record from the "items" table, we can use the following query:

    SELECT * FROM items ORDER BY RAND() LIMIT 1;

    IDItem_NameItem_QuantityItem_PricePurchase_Date
    3Pen10502021-07-12

    This query will give us a random record from the table.

    SQL ORDER BY LIMIT

    SQL ORDER BY LIMIT

    Limiting the number of rows retrieved from a database is commonly used in scenarios like pagination, where only a certain number of records need to be displayed at a time, such as 10, 50, or 100 records per page.

    LIMIT Clause for Oracle SQL:

    In Oracle SQL, the LIMIT clause is emulated using the ROWNUM query because it is applied after the results are selected. Here\'s how you can achieve it:

    SELECT name, age FROM (SELECT name, age, ROWNUM r FROM (SELECT name, age FROM employee_data ORDER BY age DESC ) WHERE ROWNUM <= 40 ) WHERE r >= 21;

    This query retrieves rows 21 to 40 from the result set. Let\'s break down the query:

    • The innermost subquery sorts the records from the "employee_data" table by age in descending order.
    • The next subquery selects rows with ROWNUM less than or equal to 40 from the sorted result set.
    • Finally, the outermost query selects rows with ROWNUM from 21 to 40, effectively limiting the output to the desired range.

    SQL Sorting on Multiple Columns

    Sorting records based on multiple columns is a common requirement in SQL queries. It allows you to arrange the data in a specific order based on the values of multiple columns.

    Example Using Customer Table:

    Consider a table named "customers" with various columns. To retrieve all customers from this table and sort them by the "country" column in ascending order and then by the "Customer-Name" column in ascending order, you can use the following SQL statement:

    SELECT * FROM customers ORDER BY country ASC, Customer-Name ASC;

    In this query:

    • ORDER BY: Specifies the sorting order.
    • country ASC: Sorts the records by the "country" column in ascending order (ASC is optional, as it\'s the default sorting order).
    • Customer-Name ASC: Sorts the records further by the "Customer-Name" column in ascending order.

    This query will return all customers from the "customers" table, sorted first by country and then by customer name. The records will be ordered alphabetically by country, and for records with the same country, they will be sorted alphabetically by customer name within each country group.

    @string/order_by_clause @string/order_by_asc @string/order_by_desc @string/order_by_random @string/order_by_limit @string/order_by_multiple_cols

    SQL INSERT statement

    The SQL INSERT statement is a SQL query utilized for inserting single or multiple records into a table.

    There are two primary methods for inserting data into a table:

    Direct Insertion

    You can insert a row into the table using the SQL INSERT INTO command. There are two approaches to insert values:

    • Without Specifying Column Names:

      • INSERT INTO table_name VALUES (value1, value2, value3....);

    • With Specifying Column Names:

      • INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2, column3....) VALUES (value1, value2, value3.....);

    For example, suppose we have a table named STUDENTS with five records:

    INSERT INTO STUDENTS (ROLL_NO, NAME, AGE, CITY) VALUES (1, \'ABHIRAM\', 22, \'ALLAHABAD\'); INSERT INTO STUDENTS (ROLL_NO, NAME, AGE, CITY) VALUES (2, \'ALKA\', 20, \'GHAZIABAD\'); INSERT INTO STUDENTS (ROLL_NO, NAME, AGE, CITY) VALUES (3, \'DISHA\', 21, \'VARANASI\'); INSERT INTO STUDENTS (ROLL_NO, NAME, AGE, CITY) VALUES (4, \'ESHA\', 21, \'DELHI\'); INSERT INTO STUDENTS (ROLL_NO, NAME, AGE, CITY) VALUES (5, \'MANMEET\', 23, \'JALANDHAR\');

    This will result in the following table:

    ROLL_NONAMEAGECITY
    1ABHIRAM22ALLAHABAD
    2ALKA20GHAZIABAD
    3DISHA21VARANASI
    4ESHA21DELHI
    5MANMEET23JALANDHAR

    You can create a record in the CUSTOMERS table using a similar syntax:

    INSERT INTO CUSTOMERS VALUES (6, \'PRATIK\', 24, \'KANPUR\');

    This will result in the following table:
