    ROLL_NONAMEAGECITY
    1ABHIRAM22ALLAHABAD
    2ALKA20GHAZIABAD
    3DISHA21VARANASI
    4ESHA21DELHI
    5MANMEET23JALANDHAR
    6PRATIK24KANPUR

    Insertion through SELECT Statement

    SQL INSERT INTO SELECT Syntax:

    INSERT INTO table_name [(column1, column2, .... column)] SELECT column1, column2, .... Column N FROM table_name [WHERE condition];

    Inserting Multiple Rows in a Table

    In database management, developers often inquire whether it\'s feasible to insert multiple rows into a table using a single statement. Currently, developers typically resort to writing multiple insert statements, which can be tedious and time-consuming.

    Let\'s explore practical examples using MySQL to clarify this concept. We\'ll start by selecting a database and creating a table named \'student\'.

    USE dbs; CREATE TABLE student ( ID INT, Name VARCHAR(20), Percentage INT, Location VARCHAR(20), DateOfBirth DATE );

    The \'student\' table has been successfully created.

    Now, we\'ll demonstrate inserting multiple records into the \'student\' table with a single query:

    INSERT INTO student (ID, Name, Percentage, Location, DateOfBirth) VALUES (1, \'Manthan Koli\', 79, \'Delhi\', \'2003-08-20\'), (2, \'Dev Dixit\', 75, \'Pune\', \'1999-06-17\'), (3, \'Aakash Deshmukh\', 87, \'Mumbai\', \'1997-09-12\'), (4, \'Aaryan Jaiswal\', 90, \'Chennai\', \'2005-10-02\'), (5, \'Rahul Khanna\', 92, \'Ambala\', \'1996-03-04\'), (6, \'Pankaj Deshmukh\', 67, \'Kanpur\', \'2000-02-02\'), (7, \'Gaurav Kumar\', 84, \'Chandigarh\', \'1998-07-06\'), (8, \'Sanket Jain\', 61, \'Shimla\', \'1990-09-08\'), (9, \'Sahil Wagh\', 90, \'Kolkata\', \'1968-04-03\'), (10, \'Saurabh Singh\', 54, \'Kashmir\', \'1989-01-06\');

    This single query successfully inserts ten records into the \'student\' table.

    To verify the insertion of multiple records, we\'ll execute the following SELECT query:

    SELECT * FROM student;

    ID Name Percentage Location DateOfBirth
    1 Manthan Koli 79 Delhi 2003-08-20
    2 Dev Dixit 75 Pune 1999-06-17
    3 Aakash Deshmukh 87 Mumbai 1997-09-12
    4 Aaryan Jaiswal 90 Chennai 2005-10-02
    5 Rahul Khanna 92 Ambala 1996-03-04
    6 Pankaj Deshmukh 67 Kanpur 2000-02-02
    7 Gaurav Kumar 84 Chandigarh 1998-07-06
    8 Sanket Jain 61 Shimla 1990-09-08
    9 Sahil Wagh 90 Kolkata 1968-04-03
    10 Saurabh Singh 54 Kashmir 1989-01-06

    The query results confirm the successful insertion of all ten records.

    @string/insert_statement @string/insert_multiple_rows

    Updating Data in a Database Table

    The SQL UPDATE statement is utilized to modify the existing data in the database tables. This modification is done based on specified conditions using the WHERE clause.

    The general syntax of the UPDATE statement is:

    UPDATE table_name SET column_name1 = value1, column_name2 = value2, ..., column_nameN = valueN [WHERE condition];

    Let\'s look at an example:

    UPDATE students SET User_Name = \'beinghuman\' WHERE Student_Id = \'3\';

    Here\'s the table before and after the update:

    Before:

    Student_Id FirstName LastName User_Name
    1 Ada Sharma sharmili
    2 Rahul Maurya sofamous
    3 James Walker jonny

    After:

    Student_Id FirstName LastName User_Name
    1 Ada Sharma sharmili
    2 Rahul Maurya sofamous
    3 James Walker beinghuman

    If you want to update multiple fields simultaneously, separate each field assignment with a comma, like so:

    UPDATE students SET User_Name = \'beserious\', First_Name = \'Johnny\' WHERE Student_Id = \'3\';

    After this update, the table will look like this:

    Student_Id FirstName LastName User_Name
    1 Ada Sharma sharmili
    2 Rahul Maurya sofamous
    3 Johnny Walker beserious

    SQL UPDATE with JOIN

    The SQL UPDATE statement is utilized to modify the existing data in the database tables. This modification is done based on specified conditions using the WHERE clause.

    The general syntax of the UPDATE statement is:

    UPDATE table_name SET column_name1 = value1, column_name2 = value2, ..., column_nameN = valueN [WHERE condition];

    Let\'s look at an example:

    UPDATE students SET User_Name = \'beinghuman\' WHERE Student_Id = \'3\';

    Here\'s the table before and after the update:

    Before:

    Student_Id FirstName LastName User_Name
    1 Ada Sharma sharmili
    2 Rahul Maurya sofamous
    3 James Walker jonny

    After:

    Student_Id FirstName LastName User_Name
    1 Ada Sharma sharmili
    2 Rahul Maurya sofamous
    3 James Walker beinghuman

    If you want to update multiple fields simultaneously, separate each field assignment with a comma, like so:

    UPDATE students SET User_Name =\'beserious\', First_Name = \'Johnny\' WHERE Student_Id = \'3\';

    After this update, the table will look like this:

    Student_Id FirstName LastName User_Name
    1 Ada Sharma sharmili
    2 Rahul Maurya sofamous
    3 Johnny Walker beserious

    SQL UPDATE DATE

    Updating Date and Time Fields in a Database Table

    To update a date and time field in SQL, you can use the following query:

    UPDATE table_name SET date_column_name = \'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS\' WHERE Id = value;

    Here\'s an example to illustrate the syntax:

    Suppose we have a table where we want to update the date and time fields. If we want to modify the EndDate field for the row with Id = 1, we would use the following query:

    UPDATE table_name SET EndDate = \'2014-03-16 00:00:00.000\' WHERE Id = 1;

    Please note that you should replace \'table_name\' with the actual name of your table, \'date_column_name\' with the specific column you want to update, and \'Id\' with the identifier of the row you want to modify. Additionally, ensure that the date and time format \'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS\' matches the format of your database.

    This query will update the EndDate field in the specified row with the provided date and time value.

    @string/update_statement @string/sql_update_join @string/sql_update_date

    SQL DELETE :

    The SQL DELETE statement is employed to remove rows from a table. It allows you to delete one or more records from a table.

    SQL DELETE Syntax:

    The syntax for the SQL DELETE statement is as follows:

    DELETE FROM table_name [WHERE condition];

    Here,

    table_name

    represents the table from which rows are to be deleted. The WHERE clause in the SQL DELETE statement is optional.

    SQL DELETE Example:

    Let\'s consider a table named "EMPLOYEE":

    ID EMP_NAME CITY SALARY
    101 Adarsh Singh Obra 20000
    102 Sanjay Singh Meerut 21000
    103 Priyanka Sharma Raipur 25000
    104 Esha Singhal Delhi 26000

    Example of DELETE with WHERE clause:

    DELETE FROM EMPLOYEE WHERE ID = 101;

    Resulting table after the query:

    ID EMP_NAME CITY SALARY
    102 Sanjay Singh Meerut 21000
    103 Priyanka Sharma Raipur 25000
    104 Esha Singhal Delhi 26000

    Another example of DELETE statement:

    DELETE FROM EMPLOYEE;

    Resulting table after the query:

    ID EMP_NAME CITY SALARY

    This will delete all the records from the EMPLOYEE table.

    The WHERE clause in the SQL DELETE statement is optional and is used to specify conditions for the rows to be deleted. It helps prevent the deletion of all rows in the table. If you omit the WHERE clause, you risk deleting all rows in the table.

    Note: You cannot use the * (asterisk) symbol to delete all records. For example, the following statement is invalid:

    DELETE * FROM EMPLOYEE;

    Please ensure to replace

    table_name

    with the actual name of your table and adjust the conditions in the WHERE clause according to your requirements.

    Deleting a Row

    Let\'s consider an example with a table named "student_name":

    ID STUDENT_NAME ADDRESS
    001 AJEET MAURYA GHAZIABAD
    002 RAJA KHAN LUCKNOW
    003 RAVI MALIK DELHI

    If you wish to delete a student with ID 003 from the student_name table, the SQL DELETE query would appear as follows:

    DELETE FROM student_name WHERE id = \'003\';

    Resulting table after executing the SQL DELETE query:

    ID STUDENT_NAME ADDRESS
    001 AJEET MAURYA GHAZIABAD
    002 RAJA KHAN LUCKNOW

    Ensure to include single quotes around the value 003 since it is a string in the SQL syntax. This query will delete the row corresponding to the student with ID 003 from the student_name table.

    Deleting All Rows

    If you intend to delete all the rows from the "STUDENT_NAME" table, you would use the following SQL query:

    DELETE FROM STUDENT_NAME;

    This query will remove all records from the "STUDENT_NAME" table, leaving it empty. After executing this query, the resulting table would appear as follows:

    ID STUDENT_NAME ADDRESS

    The table would be empty, with no rows remaining.

    Please ensure to use this query cautiously as it permanently deletes all data from the specified table. This action cannot be undone, so it\'s essential to proceed with caution.

    Deleting Duplicate Rows

    In SQL, if you encounter a situation where there are multiple duplicate records in a table, it\'s essential to handle them carefully to ensure that you retrieve only unique records.

    One approach to deal with duplicate rows is by using the DISTINCT keyword. It is utilized along with the SELECT statement to eliminate duplicate records and retrieve only unique ones.

    Syntax:

    The basic syntax to eliminate duplicate records from a table is:

    SELECT DISTINCT column1, column2, ...., columnN FROM table_name WHERE [conditions];

    Example:

    Consider an example of a "STUDENT" table:

    ROLL_NO NAME PERCENTAGE ADDRESS
    1 AJEET MAURYA 72.8 ALLAHABAD
    2 CHANDAN SHARMA 63.5 MATHURA
    3 DIVYA AGRAWAL 72.3 VARANASI
    4 RAJAT KUMAR 72.3 DELHI
    5 RAVI TYAGI 75.5 HAPUR
    6 SONU JAISWAL 71.2 GHAZIABAD

    Let\'s use a SELECT query to observe the duplicate percentage records:

    SELECT PERCENTAGE FROM STUDENTS ORDER BY PERCENTAGE;

    Result:

    PERCENTAGE
    63.5
    71.2
    72.3
    72.3
    72.8
    75.5

    Now, let\'s use the SELECT query with the DISTINCT keyword to eliminate duplicate entries:

    SELECT DISTINCT PERCENTAGE FROM STUDENTS ORDER BY PERCENTAGE;

    Result:

    PERCENTAGE
    63.5
    71.2
    72.3
    72.8
    75.5

    By using DISTINCT, we retrieved only unique percentage values, eliminating the duplicate entries.

    Deleting a Database

    To remove or delete a database in SQL, you can use the DROP DATABASE statement. It is crucial to exercise caution when using this command as it permanently deletes the entire database and all its associated objects, including tables, indexes, views, procedures, and triggers.

    Syntax:

    DROP DATABASE database_name;

    Note:

    • It\'s important to ensure that the database name specified in the DROP DATABASE statement is unique within the database management system (DBMS).
    • Executing the DROP DATABASE command will result in the loss of all data and objects contained within the specified database. Therefore, it should be used with extreme care.

    Example:

    DROP DATABASE my_database;

    This statement will delete the database named "my_database" from the DBMS. All tables, indexes, and other objects contained within "my_database" will be permanently removed.

    Please remember to use the DROP DATABASE command cautiously to avoid unintended data loss.

    Deleting a View

    Before discussing SQL DELETE VIEW, let\'s understand what a SQL view is:

    A view in SQL is a virtual table generated as a result set of a stored query. It does not physically store data but instead represents the result of a SELECT query on one or more tables.

    Syntax for creating a SQL view:

    CREATE VIEW view_name AS SELECT columns FROM tables WHERE conditions;

    To delete a SQL view, you can use the SQL DROP VIEW statement. Here\'s the syntax:

    SQL DROP VIEW syntax:

    DROP VIEW view_name;

    Why use the SQL DROP VIEW statement?

    The SQL DROP VIEW statement is used when a view is no longer needed or useful. It allows you to remove the view permanently from the database.

    Additionally, if modifications are required within a view, you may choose to drop the existing view and recreate it with the necessary changes.

    Example:

    Suppose we have a view named "my_view" that we want to delete:

    DROP VIEW my_view;

    Executing this command will permanently delete the view named "my_view" from the database.

    It\'s important to exercise caution when using the DROP VIEW statement as it permanently removes the view and its definition from the database.

    DELETE JOIN Operation

    Performing a delete operation using a JOIN in SQL can be a necessary task, especially when dealing with complex conditions.

    In such scenarios, there are typically three tables involved: table1, table2, and the target table.

    SQL Syntax for DELETE JOIN:

    DELETE [target table] FROM [table1] INNER JOIN [table2] ON [table1.joining_column] = [table2.joining_column] WHERE [condition];

    Example:

    Suppose we have two tables, "orders" and "order_details", and we want to delete orders that have no corresponding details in the "order_details" table:

    DELETE orders FROM orders LEFT JOIN order_details ON orders.order_id = order_details.order_id WHERE order_details.order_id IS NULL;

    In this example, the DELETE JOIN operation removes records from the "orders" table where there is no matching "order_id" in the "order_details" table.

    Note:

    Using DELETE JOIN requires caution, especially when dealing with data deletion. Always double-check the WHERE clause to ensure you are deleting the correct records.

    @string/delete_statement @string/sql_delete_table @string/sql_delete_row @string/sql_delete_all_rows @string/delete_duplicate_rows @string/sql_delete_database @string/sql_delete_view @string/sql_delete_join

    SQL JOIN

    In SQL, the JOIN operation combines records from two or more tables in a database.

    ANSI standard SQL defines five types of JOIN:

    • Inner join
    • Left outer join
    • Right outer join
    • Full outer join
    • Cross join

    During a JOIN operation, rows from multiple tables are merged into a single result set.

    Why SQL JOIN is used?

    SQL JOIN is utilized when you need to access data from more than one table through a SELECT statement. It allows you to combine the rows of multiple tables into a single result set.

    The joining of two or more tables is typically based on a common field between them.

    SQL INNER JOIN:

    SQL INNER JOIN, also known as a simple join, is the most common type of join.

    Example:

    Consider two tables: "Staff" and "Payment"

    Staff table:
    ID Staff_NAME Staff_AGE STAFF_ADDRESS Monthly_Package
    1 ARYAN 22 MUMBAI 18000
    2 SUSHIL 32 DELHI 20000
    3 MONTY 25 MOHALI 22000
    4 AMIT 20 ALLAHABAD 12000
    Payment table:
    Payment_ID DATE Staff_ID AMOUNT
    101 30/12/2009 1 3000.00
    102 22/02/2010 3 2500.00
    103 23/02/2010 4 3500.00

    To join these two tables using SQL JOIN:

    SELECT s.Staff_ID, s.Staff_NAME, s.Staff_AGE, p.AMOUNT FROM Staff s INNER JOIN Payment p ON s.ID = p.Staff_ID;

    Result:

    Staff_ID Staff_NAME Staff_AGE AMOUNT
    1 ARYAN 22 3000.00
    3 MONTY 25 2500.00
    4 AMIT 20 3500.00

    This query combines the "Staff" and "Payment" tables based on the common field "ID" from "Staff" and "Staff_ID" from "Payment", producing the desired result set.

    SQL OUTER JOIN

    In SQL, the OUTER JOIN operation combines records from two tables, including matching and non-matching records.

    There are three types of OUTER JOIN:

    1. Left Outer Join
    2. Right Outer Join
    3. Full Outer Join

    Consider the following tables with sample data:

    EmployeeID Employee_Name Employee_Salary
    1 Arun Tiwari 50000
    2 Sachin Rathi 64000
    3 Harshal Pathak 48000
    4 Arjun Kuwar 46000
    5 Sarthak Gada 62000
    6 Saurabh Sheik 53000
    7 Shubham Singh 29000
    8 Shivam Dixit 54000
    9 Vicky Gujral 39000
    10 Vijay Bose 28000
    DepartmentID Department_Name Employee_ID
    1 Production 1
    2 Sales 3
    3 Marketing 4
    4 Accounts 5
    5 Development 7
    6 HR 9
    7 Sales 10

    1. Left Outer Join:

    SELECT e.EmployeeID, e.Employee_Name, e.Employee_Salary, d.DepartmentID, d.Department_Name FROM Employee e LEFT OUTER JOIN Department d ON e.EmployeeID = d.Employee_ID;

    2. Right Outer Join:

    SELECT e.EmployeeID, e.Employee_Name, e.Employee_Salary, d.DepartmentID, d.Department_Name FROM Department d RIGHT OUTER JOIN Employee e ON e.EmployeeID = d.Employee_ID;

    3. Full Outer Join:

    MySQL doesn\'t support FULL OUTER JOIN directly. To achieve it, we can combine the results of LEFT OUTER JOIN and RIGHT OUTER JOIN using UNION:

    SELECT e.EmployeeID, e.Employee_Name, e.Employee_Salary, d.DepartmentID, d.Department_Name FROM Department d LEFT OUTER JOIN Employee e ON e.EmployeeID = d.Employee_ID UNION SELECT e.EmployeeID, e.Employee_Name, e.Employee_Salary, d.DepartmentID, d.Department_Name FROM Department d RIGHT OUTER JOIN Employee e ON e.EmployeeID = d.Employee_ID;

    SQL LEFT JOIN

    In SQL, the LEFT JOIN operation retrieves all records from the left table and the matched records from the right table. If there are no matches in the right table, NULL values are returned.

    Consider the following tables with sample data:

    EmployeeID Employee_Name Employee_Salary
    1 Arun Tiwari 50000
    2 Sachin Rathi 64000
    3 Harshal Pathak 48000
    4 Arjun Kuwar 46000
    5 Sarthak Gada 62000
    6 Saurabh Sheik 53000
    7 Shubham Singh 29000
    8 Shivam Dixit 54000
    9 Vicky Gujral 39000
    10 Vijay Bose 28000
    DepartmentID Department_Name Employee_ID
    1 Production 1
    2 Sales 3
    3 Marketing 4
    4 Accounts 5
    5 Development 7
    6 HR 9
    7 Sales 10

    Example:

    Write a query to perform a left join operation considering the employee table as the left table and the department table as the right table.

    Query:

    SELECT e.EmployeeID, e.Employee_Name, e.Employee_Salary, d.DepartmentID, d.Department_Name FROM employee e LEFT JOIN department d ON e.EmployeeID = d.Employee_ID;

    Output:
    EmployeeIDEmployee_NameEmployee_SalaryDepartmentIDDepartment_Name
    1Arun Tiwari500001Production
    2Sachin Rathi64000--
    3Harshal Pathak480002Sales
    4Arjun Kuwar460003Marketing
    5Sarthak Gada620004Accounts
    6Saurabh Sheik53000--
    7Shubham Singh290005Development
    8Shivam Dixit54000--
    9Vicky Gujral390006HR
    10Vijay Bose280007Sales

    This query retrieves EmployeeID, Employee_Name, Employee_Salary, DepartmentID, and Department_Name from the employee and department tables, performing a left join operation where EmployeeID matches Employee_ID. All records from the employee table are retrieved, and matching records from the department table are included. If there\'s no match in the department table, NULL values are displayed for DepartmentID and Department_Name.

    SQL RIGHT JOIN

    In SQL, the RIGHT JOIN operation retrieves all records from the right table and the matched records from the left table. If there are no matches in the left table, NULL values are returned.

    Consider the following tables with sample data:

    EmployeeID Employee_Name Employee_Salary
    1 Arun Tiwari 50000
    2 Sachin Rathi 64000
    3 Harshal Pathak 48000
    4 Arjun Kuwar 46000
    5 Sarthak Gada 62000
    6 Saurabh Sheik 53000
    7 Shubham Singh 29000
    8 Shivam Dixit 54000
    9 Vicky Gujral 39000
    10 Vijay Bose 28000
    DepartmentID Department_Name Employee_ID
    1 Production 1
    2 Sales 3
    3 Marketing 4
    4 Accounts 5
    5 Development 7
    6 HR 9
    7 Sales 10

    Example:

    Write a query to perform a right join operation considering the employee table as the left table and the department table as the right table.

    Query:

    SELECT e.EmployeeID, e.Employee_Name, e.Employee_Salary, d.DepartmentID, d.Department_Name FROM employee e RIGHT JOIN department d ON e.EmployeeID = d.Employee_ID;

    Output:
    EmployeeIDEmployee_NameEmployee_SalaryDepartmentIDDepartment_Name
    1Arun Tiwari500001Production
    3Harshal Pathak480002Sales
    4Arjun Kuwar460003Marketing
    5Sarthak Gada620004Accounts
    7Shubham Singh290005Development
    9Vicky Gujral390006HR
    10Vijay Bose280007Sales

    This query retrieves EmployeeID, Employee_Name, Employee_Salary, DepartmentID, and Department_Name from the employee and department tables, performing a right join operation where EmployeeID matches Employee_ID. All records from the department table are retrieved, and matching records from the employee table are included. If there\'s no match in the employee table, NULL values are displayed for EmployeeID, Employee_Name, and Employee_Salary.

    SQL FULL JOIN

    The SQL FULL JOIN combines the results of both Left and Right Outer Joins, returning all rows from both participating tables, regardless of whether there is a match or not. It fills NULL values in places where matches are not found.

    Syntax for Full Outer Join:

    SELECT * FROM table1 FULL OUTER JOIN table2 ON table1.column_name = table2.column_name;

    Here, table1 and table2 are the names of the tables participating in the join, and column_name is the column used for joining.

    Example:

    Consider two tables table_A and table_B:

    table_A
    A M
    1 m
    2 n
    4 o
    table_B
    A N
    2 p
    3 q
    5 r

    Resulting table:

    A M A N
    2 n 2 p
    1 m - -
    4 o - -
    - - 3 q
    - - 5 r

    Because this is a Full Join, all rows from both table_A and table_B are included in the output. The output contains values in all columns only where there are matches between table_A and table_B. For rows where there is no match, NULL values are filled in.

    SQL Cross Join

    The SQL Cross Join combines every row from the first table with every row from the second table, resulting in the Cartesian product of the two tables. Each row of the first table is paired with every row of the second table, resulting in a combination of all possible pairs.

    Syntax for Cross Join:

    SELECT * FROM table1 CROSS JOIN table2;

    Here, table1 and table2 are the names of the tables participating in the join.

    Example:

    Consider two tables MatchScore and Departments:

    Player Department_id Goals
    Franklin 1 2
    Alan 1 3
    Priyanka 2 2
    Rajesh 3 5
    Department_id Department_name
    1 IT
    2 HR
    3 Marketing

    Query:

    SELECT * FROM MatchScore CROSS JOIN Departments;

    Result:

    PlayerDepartment_idGoalsDepartment_idDepartment_name
    Franklin121IT
    Franklin122HR
    Franklin123Marketing
    Alan131IT
    Alan132HR
    Alan133Marketing
    Priyanka221IT
    Priyanka222HR
    Priyanka223Marketing
    Rajesh351IT
    Rajesh352HR
    Rajesh353Marketing
    @string/sql_join @string/sql_outer_join @string/sql_left_join @string/sql_right_join @string/sql_full_join @string/sql_cross_join

    SQL PRIMARY KEY

    A primary key (PK) is a column or set of columns that uniquely identifies each row in a table. It ensures the entity integrity of the table by enforcing uniqueness and data integrity constraints.

    When defining a primary key, a PRIMARY KEY constraint is used when creating or modifying a table. If multiple columns are used as a primary key, it\'s termed a composite primary key.

    When designing a composite primary key, it\'s advisable to use as few columns as possible to optimize storage and performance. A primary key cannot exceed a length of 900 bytes, cannot have null values, and must contain unique values.

    Key Points for Primary Key:

    • Ensures entity integrity.
    • Guarantees uniqueness of data.
    • Length of primary key cannot exceed 900 bytes.
    • Primary key columns cannot have null values.
    • No duplicate values are allowed for a primary key.
    • Each table can have only one primary key constraint.

    Main Advantage of Primary Key:

    The main advantage is fast access to data due to the uniqueness enforced by the primary key.

    In Oracle, a primary key cannot contain more than 32 columns.

    SQL Primary Key for One Column:

    MySQL:

    CREATE TABLE students ( S_Id INT NOT NULL, LastName VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, FirstName VARCHAR(255), Address VARCHAR(255), City VARCHAR(255), PRIMARY KEY (S_Id) );

    SQL Server, Oracle, MS Access:

    CREATE TABLE students ( S_Id INT NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY, LastName VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, FirstName VARCHAR(255), Address VARCHAR(255), City VARCHAR(255) );

    SQL Primary Key for Multiple Columns:

    MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle, MS Access:

    CREATE TABLE students ( S_Id INT NOT NULL, LastName VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, FirstName VARCHAR(255), Address VARCHAR(255), City VARCHAR(255), CONSTRAINT pk_StudentID PRIMARY KEY (S_Id, LastName) );

    SQL Primary Key using ALTER TABLE:

    Adding Primary Key on One Column:

    ALTER TABLE students ADD PRIMARY KEY (S_Id);

    Adding Primary Key on Multiple Columns:

    ALTER TABLE students ADD CONSTRAINT pk_StudentID PRIMARY KEY (S_Id, LastName);

    How to DROP a PRIMARY KEY constraint:

    MySQL:

    ALTER TABLE students DROP PRIMARY KEY;

    SQL Server / Oracle / MS Access:

    ALTER TABLE students DROP CONSTRAINT pk_StudentID;

    SQL FOREIGN KEY

    In relational databases, a foreign key is a column or set of columns used to establish a link between two tables. It serves to maintain referential integrity between related tables by enforcing relationships between them.

    In simpler terms, a foreign key in one table points to the primary key in another table.

    Let\'s illustrate this with an example involving two tables: "students" and "orders."

    Students Table:

    S_Id LastName FirstName City
    1 MAURYA AJEET ALLAHABAD
    2 JAISWAL RATAN GHAZIABAD
    3 ARORA SAUMYA MODINAGAR

    Orders Table:

    O_Id OrderNo S_Id
    1 99586465 2
    2 78466588 2
    3 22354846 3
    4 57698656 1

    Here, the "S_Id" column in the "Orders" table points to the "S_Id" column in the "Students" table.

    Key Points:

    • The "S_Id" column in the "Students" table is the PRIMARY KEY.
    • The "S_Id" column in the "Orders" table is a FOREIGN KEY.
    • The foreign key constraint ensures referential integrity and prevents actions that would destroy links between tables.
    • It also prevents invalid data from entering the foreign key column.

    SQL FOREIGN KEY Constraint on CREATE TABLE:

    MySQL:

    CREATE TABLE orders ( O_Id INT NOT NULL, Order_No INT NOT NULL, S_Id INT, PRIMARY KEY (O_Id), FOREIGN KEY (S_Id) REFERENCES Students (S_Id) );

    SQL Server / Oracle / MS Access:

    CREATE TABLE Orders ( O_Id INT NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY, Order_No INT NOT NULL, S_Id INT, FOREIGN KEY (S_Id) REFERENCES students (S_Id) );

    SQL FOREIGN KEY Constraint using ALTER TABLE:

    If the "Orders" table is already created and you want to add a FOREIGN KEY constraint on the "S_Id" column:

    MySQL / SQL Server / Oracle / MS Access:

    ALTER TABLE Orders ADD CONSTRAINT fk_PerOrders FOREIGN KEY (S_Id) REFERENCES Students (S_Id);

    DROP Syntax for FOREIGN KEY Constraint:

    MySQL:

    ALTER TABLE Orders DROP FOREIGN KEY fk_PerOrders;

    SQL Server / Oracle / MS Access:

    ALTER TABLE Orders DROP CONSTRAINT fk_PerOrders;

    Difference between Primary Key and Foreign Key:

    • A primary key cannot be null, whereas a foreign key can.
    • Primary key values are always unique, but foreign key values can be duplicated.
    • A primary key uniquely identifies records within a table, while a foreign key establishes a relationship with another table.
    • Each table can have only one primary key, but it can have multiple foreign keys.
    • By default, a primary key adds a clustered index, while a foreign key does not automatically create an index; you must manually create one.

    SQL Composite Key

    A composite key is a combination of two or more columns in a table that, when combined, uniquely identifies each row in the table. Unlike individual columns, the combination of columns in a composite key guarantees uniqueness.

    When more than one attribute is needed to uniquely identify an entity, a composite key is used. This key can be either a candidate key or a primary key.

    Columns forming a composite key can be of different data types.

    SQL Syntax to specify a composite key:

    CREATE TABLE TABLE_NAME (COLUMN_1 DATA_TYPE_1, COLUMN_2 DATA_TYPE_2, ... PRIMARY KEY (COLUMN_1, COLUMN_2, ...));

    Here, the composite key is created with COLUMN1 and COLUMN2.

    Examples:

    MySQL:

    CREATE TABLE SAMPLE_TABLE (COL1 INT, COL2 VARCHAR(30), COL3 VARCHAR(50), PRIMARY KEY (COL1, COL2));

    Oracle:

    CREATE TABLE SAMPLE_TABLE (COL1 NUMBER, COL2 VARCHAR2(30), COL3 VARCHAR2(50), PRIMARY KEY (COL1, COL2));

    SQL Server:

    CREATE TABLE SAMPLE_TABLE (COL1 INT, COL2 NVARCHAR(30), COL3 NVARCHAR(50), PRIMARY KEY (COL1, COL2));

    In these examples, the composite key consists of COL1 and COL2 columns.

    Using a composite key ensures that the combination of values in the specified columns uniquely identifies each row in the table, maintaining data integrity and facilitating effective database operations.

    Unique Key in SQL

    A unique key is a set of one or more fields or columns in a table that uniquely identify a record in a database table. Similar to a primary key, it ensures data integrity by enforcing uniqueness but differs in that it can accept only one null value and cannot have duplicate values.

    Key Points:

    • Unique key and primary key both ensure uniqueness for a column or set of columns.
    • Primary key constraint automatically defines a unique key constraint.
    • While there can be many unique key constraints for one table, there can only be one PRIMARY KEY constraint.

    SQL UNIQUE KEY Constraint on CREATE TABLE:

    To create a unique constraint on the "S_Id" column when creating the "students" table:

    SQL Server / Oracle / MS Access (Single Column):

    CREATE TABLE students ( S_Id INT NOT NULL UNIQUE, LastName VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, FirstName VARCHAR(255), City VARCHAR(255) );

    MySQL (Single Column):

    CREATE TABLE students ( S_Id INT NOT NULL, LastName VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, FirstName VARCHAR(255), City VARCHAR(255), UNIQUE (S_Id) );

    MySQL / SQL Server / Oracle / MS Access (Multiple Columns):

    CREATE TABLE students ( S_Id INT NOT NULL, LastName VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, FirstName VARCHAR(255), City VARCHAR(255), CONSTRAINT uc_studentId UNIQUE (S_Id, LastName) );

    SQL UNIQUE KEY Constraint on ALTER TABLE:

    To add a unique constraint on "S_Id" column after the table is created:

    MySQL / SQL Server / Oracle / MS Access (Single Column):

    ALTER TABLE students ADD UNIQUE (S_Id);

    MySQL / SQL Server / Oracle / MS Access (Multiple Columns):

    ALTER TABLE students ADD CONSTRAINT uc_StudentId UNIQUE (S_Id, LastName);

    DROP SYNTAX FOR A UNIQUE KEY Constraint:

    To drop a unique constraint:

    MySQL:

    ALTER TABLE students DROP INDEX uc_studentID;

    SQL Server / Oracle / MS Access:

    ALTER TABLE students DROP CONSTRAINT uc_studentID;

    Alternate Key in SQL

    An alternate key in SQL is a secondary key in a table that uniquely identifies each record, similar to a primary key. While the primary key uniquely identifies each row and is selected from among the candidate keys, the alternate keys are the remaining candidate keys that are not chosen as the primary key.

    Key Points:

    • An alternate key is any candidate key in a table that is not selected as the primary key.
    • It serves as an alternative means of uniquely identifying records in the table.
    • Like the primary key, an alternate key ensures data integrity by enforcing uniqueness.

    Example:

    Consider a table students with columns: Name, Roll No., ID, and Class. If Roll No. is chosen as the primary key, then Name, ID, and Class become alternate keys.

    Key Differences:

    • Primary keys are chosen from among the candidate keys and are used to uniquely identify each record. Alternate keys are the remaining candidate keys that are not selected as the primary key.
    • While primary keys uniquely identify records and cannot contain null values, alternate keys may accept null values but must enforce uniqueness when not null.

    SQL Syntax:

    There is no specific SQL syntax for defining alternate keys. Instead, it\'s a conceptual understanding within database design. Alternate keys are identified during the process of identifying candidate keys, and one of them is chosen as the primary key while the others become alternate keys.

    Example:

    Consider a scenario where Roll No. is chosen as the primary key in the students table:

    CREATE TABLE students ( Roll_No INT PRIMARY KEY, Name VARCHAR(255), ID INT UNIQUE, Class VARCHAR(50) );

    In this example, Roll No. is the primary key, while ID serves as an alternate key since it uniquely identifies each record but is not chosen as the primary key.

    @string/primary_key @string/foreign_key @string/composite_key @string/unique_key @string/alternate_key

    SQL String Functions

    SQL String functions are predefined functions in Structured Query Language (SQL) that facilitate string manipulation within a database. These functions accept string data types as input, process them, and return results based on the specified operations.

    Here is an overview of some of the most commonly used SQL string functions:

    • ASCII():
      Returns the ASCII value of the first character in a string.
    • CHAR_LENGTH() / CHARACTER_LENGTH():
      Returns the number of characters in a string.
    • CONCAT():
      Concatenates two or more strings together.
    • CONCAT_WS():
      Concatenates strings with a specified separator.
    • FIND_IN_SET():
      Returns the position of a string within a comma-separated list of strings.
    • FORMAT():
      Formats numbers with a specified number of decimal places.
    • INSERT():
      Inserts a substring into a string at a specified position.
    • INSTR():
      Returns the position of the first occurrence of a substring in a string.
    • LCASE() / LOWER():
      Converts a string to lowercase.
    • LEFT():
      Extracts a specified number of characters from the left side of a string.
    • LOCATE():
      Returns the position of the first occurrence of a substring in a string (an alias for INSTR()).
    • LPAD():
      Pads a string with another string to a specified length, starting from the left.
    • LTRIM():
      Removes leading spaces from a string.
    • MID() / SUBSTRING() / SUBSTR():
      Extracts a substring from a string, starting at a specified position.
    • POSITION():
      Returns the position of the first occurrence of a substring in a string (an alias for INSTR()).
    • REPEAT():
      Repeats a string a specified number of times.
    • REPLACE():
      Replaces all occurrences of a substring within a string with another substring.
    • REVERSE():
      Reverses a string.
    • RIGHT():
      Extracts a specified number of characters from the right side of a string.
    • RPAD():
      Pads a string with another string to a specified length, starting from the right.
    • RTRIM():
      Removes trailing spaces from a string.
    • SPACE():
      Returns a string consisting of a specified number of space characters.
    • STRCMP():
      Compares two strings.
    • SUBSTR() / SUBSTRING():
      Extracts a substring from a string, starting at a specified position.
    • SUBSTRING_INDEX():
      Returns a substring from a string before a specified number of occurrences of a delimiter.
    • UCASE() / UPPER():
      Converts a string to uppercase.

    LTRIM Function in SQL

    The LTRIM function in SQL is a string function used to remove leading spaces or specified characters from the left side of a string. It is particularly useful for cleaning up data where leading spaces need to be eliminated.

    Syntax of LTRIM String Function

    There are different syntax variations for the LTRIM function:

    1. Syntax 1: Using LTRIM with column name:

      2. SELECT LTRIM(Column_Name, string) AS Alias_Name FROM Table_Name;

    2. Syntax 2: Using LTRIM with a set of characters (string):

      3. SELECT LTRIM(Original_String, trimmed_string);

    3. Syntax 3: Using LTRIM with a single character:

      4. SELECT LTRIM(Original_String, trimmed_character);

    Examples of LTRIM String Function

    Here are some examples demonstrating the usage of the LTRIM function:

    • Removing leading spaces:

      • SELECT LTRIM(\' JAVATPOINT\',\' \');

    • Trimming space from the left side:

      • SELECT LTRIM(\' JAVATPOINT \');

    • Trimming a substring:

      • SELECT LTRIM(\'NEW DELHI IS THE CAPITAL OF INDIA\', \'NEW DELHI IS THE \')

    • Removing a specific symbol:

      • SELECT LTRIM(\'####98221545###\', \'#\');

    • Trimming a set of numbers:

      • SELECT LTRIM(\'2021JavaTpoint2021\', \'2021\');

    Example of LTRIM Function with Table

    Consider a table called Faculty_Info with columns Faculty_ID, Faculty_First_Name, Faculty_Last_Name, Faculty_Dept_Id, Faculty_Address, Faculty_City, and Faculty_Salary.

    Faculty_ID Faculty_First_Name Faculty_Last_Name Faculty_Dept_Id Faculty_Address Faculty_City Faculty_Salary
    1001 \' Arush \' \' Sharma \' 4001 \' Aman Vihar \' \' Delhi\' 20000
    1002 \' Bulbul \' \' Roy \' 4002 \' Nirman Vihar \' \' Delhi\' 38000
    1004 \' Saurabh \' \' Sharma \' 4001 \' Sector 128 \' \' Mumbai\' 45000
    1005 \' Shivani \' \' Singhania \' 4001 \' Vivek Vihar \' \' Kolkata\' 42000
    1006 \' Avinash \' \' Sharma \' 4002 \' Sarvodya Calony\' \' Delhi\' 28000
    1007 \' Shyam \' \' Besas \' 4003 \' Krishna Nagar \' \' Lucknow\' 35000

    In this table, we can use the LTRIM function to clean up leading spaces from the Faculty_First_Name and Faculty_Address columns:

    SELECT Faculty_Id, LTRIM(Faculty_First_Name) AS Faculty_First_Name, LTRIM(Faculty_Address) AS Faculty_Address FROM Faculty_Info;

    This query trims the leading spaces from the Faculty_First_Name and Faculty_Address columns, resulting in cleaner data for analysis or presentation.

    UPPER Function in SQL

    The UPPER function in SQL is used to convert all characters in a string to uppercase. It is particularly useful for standardizing text data or ensuring consistency in data presentation.

    Syntax of UPPER String Function

    The UPPER function can be used in different syntax formats:

    1. Syntax 1: Using UPPER with column names:

      2. SELECT UPPER(Column_Name) AS Alias_Name FROM Table_Name;

    2. Syntax 2: Using UPPER with a string:

      3. SELECT UPPER(String);

    3. Syntax 3: Using UPPER with an individual lowercase character:

      4. SELECT UPPER(lower_case_character);

    Examples of UPPER String Function

    Here are some examples illustrating the usage of the UPPER function:

    • Converting all characters to uppercase:

      • SELECT UPPER(\'javatpoint is a good website\');

    • Retaining symbols and integers:

      • SELECT UPPER(\'@#$12453@#\');

    • Converting lowercase letters to uppercase:

      • SELECT UPPER(\'New Delhi IS the Capital OF India\');

    • Converting a single character to uppercase:

      • SELECT UPPER(\'s\');

    Example of UPPER Function with Table

    Consider a table named Faculty_Info with columns Faculty_ID, Faculty_First_Name, Faculty_Last_Name, Faculty_Dept_Id, Faculty_Address, Faculty_City, and Faculty_Salary.

    Faculty_ID Faculty_First_Name Faculty_Last_Name Faculty_Dept_Id Faculty_Address Faculty_City Faculty_Salary
    1001 \'arush\' \'sharma\' 4001 \'aman vihar\' \'delhi\' 20000
    1002 \'bulbul\' \'roy\' 4002 \'nirman vihar\' \'delhi\' 38000
    1004 \'saurabh\' \'sharma\' 4001 \'sector 128\' \'mumbai\' 45000
    1005 \'shivani\' \'singhania\' 4001 \'vivek vihar\' \'kolkata\' 42000
    1006 \'avinash\' \'sharma\' 4002 \'sarvodya calony\' \'delhi\' 28000
    1007 \'shyam\' \'besas\' 4003 \'krishna nagar\' \'lucknow\' 35000

    In this table, we can use the UPPER function to convert the last name to uppercase:

    SELECT Faculty_Last_Name, UPPER(Faculty_Last_Name) AS UPPER_LastName FROM Faculty_Info;

    This query converts the last name of each faculty to uppercase, providing standardized data for analysis or presentation.

    RTRIM Function in SQL

    The RTRIM function in SQL is used to remove trailing spaces or specified characters from the right side of a string. It helps in cleaning up string data by eliminating unnecessary characters.

    Syntax of RTRIM String Function

    The RTRIM function can be used in different syntax formats:

    1. Syntax 1: Using RTRIM with column names:

      2. SELECT RTRIM(Column_Name, string) AS Alias_Name FROM Table_Name;

    2. Syntax 2: Using RTRIM with a string:

      3. SELECT RTRIM(Original_String, trimmed_string);

    3. Syntax 3: Using RTRIM with a single character:

      4. SELECT RTRIM(Original_String, trimmed_character);

    Examples of RTRIM String Function

    Here are some examples illustrating the usage of the RTRIM function:

    • Removing trailing spaces from a string:

      • SELECT RTRIM(\'JAVATPOINT \');

    • Trimming spaces from the right of a string:

      • SELECT RTRIM(\' JAVATPOINT \');

    • Trimming a substring from the right of a string:

      • SELECT RTRIM(\'NEW DELHI IS THE CAPITAL OF INDIA\', \'CAPITAL OF INDIA\');

    • Removing a symbol from the right of a string:

      • SELECT RTRIM(\'####98221545###\', \'#\');

    • Trimming a set of numbers from the right of a string:

      • SELECT RTRIM(\'2021JavaTpoint2021\', \'2021\');

    • Trimming a set of numbers from the right of a string:

      • SELECT RTRIM(\'JavaTpoint202120212021\', \'2021\');

    • Removing numbers from the right side of the string based on a trimmed string:

      • SELECT RTRIM(\'JavaTpoint90287\', \'0123456789\');

    Example of RTRIM Function with Table

    Consider the Faculty_Info table with columns Faculty_ID, Faculty_First_Name, Faculty_Last_Name, Faculty_Dept_Id, Faculty_Address, Faculty_City, and Faculty_Salary.

    Faculty_ID Faculty_First_Name Faculty_Last_Name Faculty_Dept_Id Faculty_Address Faculty_City Faculty_Salary
    1001 \'arush\' \'sharma\' 4001 \'aman vihar\' \'delhi\' 20000
    1002 \'bulbul\' \'roy\' 4002 \'nirman vihar\' \'delhi\' 38000
    1004 \'saurabh\' \'sharma\' 4001 \'sector 128\' \'mumbai\' 45000
    1005 \'shivani\' \'singhania\' 4001 \'vivek vihar\' \'kolkata\' 42000
    1006 \'avinash\' \'sharma\' 4002 \'sarvodya calony\' \'delhi\' 28000
    1007 \'shyam\' \'besas\' 4003 \'krishna nagar\' \'lucknow\' 35000

    We can use the RTRIM function to remove trailing spaces from the last name column:

    SELECT Faculty_Last_Name, RTRIM(Faculty_Last_Name) AS RTRIM_LastName FROM Faculty_Info;

    This query trims the spaces from the right side of the last name, providing clean and consistent data.

    @string/sql_string_functions @string/ltrim_function_in_sql @string/upper_function_in_sql @string/rtrim_function_in_sql

    MySQL Overview

    MySQL is a widely-used open-source relational database management system developed and supported by Oracle Corporation. It is renowned for its speed, scalability, and ease of use, making it a preferred choice for managing relational databases in various applications, especially in web development.

    Architecture

    • MySQL operates on a client-server architecture, where clients send requests to a central server over a network.
    • The server processes these requests, executes database operations, and returns results to the clients.

    Core Components

    • MySQL Server: Responsible for handling all database instructions and statements.
    • Clients: Interact with the server through graphical user interfaces (GUIs) or command-line interfaces, issuing SQL expressions to perform database operations.

    Features

    • Compatibility: Compatible with various operating systems and programming languages.
    • Performance: Known for its speed and performance, even with large datasets.
    • Customizability: Users can modify and customize the software to suit their specific requirements.
    • Language Support: Integrates seamlessly with popular programming languages like PHP.
    • Scalability: Can support large databases with millions of rows and offers options for scalability.

    History

    The history of MySQL dates back to its inception in 1979 when Michael Widenius developed an in-house database tool called UNIREG. Over the years, MySQL evolved into a robust RDBMS:

    1. MySQL AB founded in 1995 to further develop and support the software.
    2. Went open-source in 2000.
    3. Launched headquarters in addition to Swedish headquarters in 2002.
    4. Acquired by Sun Microsystems in 2008.
    5. Oracle Corporation acquired Sun Microsystems in 2009, further cementing MySQL\'s position as a leading RDBMS.

    Key Features

    • Relational Database Management System (RDBMS): MySQL operates as an RDBMS, relying on SQL queries to manage and access table records.
    • Ease of Use: It offers simplicity in usage, requiring only basic knowledge of SQL for building and interacting with databases through simple SQL statements.
    • Security: MySQL incorporates a robust data security layer, encrypting passwords and safeguarding sensitive data from unauthorized access.
    • Client/Server Architecture: Following a client/server architecture, MySQL comprises a database server and multiple clients (application programs) that communicate with the server to query data and make changes.
    • Free to Download: MySQL is freely available for download from its official website, making it accessible at no cost.
    • Scalability: With support for multi-threading, MySQL can scale easily to handle large amounts of data, up to 50 million rows or more.
    • Speed: MySQL is renowned for its fast performance, backed by numerous benchmark tests.
    • High Flexibility: Supporting a wide range of embedded applications, MySQL offers high flexibility in various usage scenarios.
    • Compatibility: MySQL is compatible with various operating systems such as Windows, Linux, UNIX, FreeBSD, etc., making it versatile in deployment.
    • Transaction Support: MySQL allows transactions to be rolled back, committed, and provides crash recovery features.
    • Memory Efficiency: It exhibits high memory efficiency with minimal memory leakage issues.
    • High Performance: MySQL delivers high-performance results due to its unique storage engine architecture, fast loading utilities, and efficient cache memory usage.
    • High Productivity: Features like Triggers, Stored Procedures, and Views enhance developer productivity.
    • Platform Independence: MySQL can be downloaded, installed, and executed on most operating systems, ensuring platform independence.
    • Partitioning: This feature enhances performance and facilitates efficient management of large databases.
    • GUI Support: MySQL provides a comprehensive graphical user interface tool called "MySQL Workbench," offering SQL development, data modeling, migration, and administration capabilities.
    • Dual Password Support: MySQL version 8.0 introduced support for dual passwords, enhancing security measures.

    Drawbacks

    • Lack of Support in Older Versions: Versions prior to 5.0 may lack support for features like ROLE, COMMIT, and stored procedures.
    • Efficiency in Handling Large Databases: MySQL may not handle very large databases as efficiently as other systems.
    • Transaction Handling: It may not handle transactions as efficiently and could be prone to data corruption.
    • Development and Debugging Tools: MySQL may lack robust developing and debugging tools compared to paid databases.
    • SQL Check Constraints: MySQL does not support SQL check constraints.

    MySQL Versioning

    Versioning is a crucial process in software development, where unique version names or numbers are assigned to distinct sets of software programs as they are developed and released. Typically, the initial release of a software program is denoted by version 1.0. Each company adopts its own method for assigning version numbers or names to their software, and there is no industry-standard rule for formatting these version identifiers.

    MySQL, a popular relational database management system (RDBMS), follows this versioning practice. The latest supported version of MySQL is v5.8, encompassing numerous essential changes, including additions, removals, bug fixes, and security enhancements, from MySQL 8.0 to MySQL 8.0.21. This version was made available in April 2018 and is scheduled to end support in April 2026.

    When installing MySQL, users must choose between two distribution formats: development release and General Availability (GA) release. The development release provides access to the latest features but is not recommended for production use, while the GA release, also known as the production or stable release, is suitable for production environments.

    New Features in MySQL 8.0

    Feature Description
    Data Dictionary Implements a transactional data dictionary to store information about database objects, replacing previous versions\' metadata files and non-transactional tables.
    Atomic DDL Statement Combines storage engine operations, data dictionary updates, and binary log operations associated with a Data Definition Language (DDL) operation into a single atomic transaction.
    Upgrade Procedures Simplifies the upgrade process by automatically upgrading the data dictionary table at startup, eliminating the need for manual invocation of the mysql_upgrade command by database administrators.
    Security and Account Management Enhancements improve security and provide greater flexibility in account management for database administrators.
    Resource Management Introduces support for creating and managing resource groups, allowing threads to be assigned to specific groups for resource utilization control.
    Table Encryption Management Global management of table encryption through encryption defaults, facilitating schema and tablespace-level encryption.
    InnoDB Enhancements Various enhancements and improvements to the InnoDB storage engine, including enhancements to auto-increment counters, index tree corruption handling, and tablespace encryption.
    Character Set Support Changes the default character set from latin1 to utf8mb4, providing additional collations.
    JSON Enhancements Introduces enhancements to MySQL\'s JSON functionality, including new operators, aggregate functions, utility functions, and sorting improvements.
    Data Type Support Adds support for using expressions as default values in data type specifications.
    Optimizer Enhancement Enhancements include support for invisible indexes, descending indexes, functional indexes, and constant folding optimizations.
    Window Function Support Adds support for new window functions such as RANK(), LAG(), and NTILE().

    Deprecations and Removals in MySQL 8.0

    While MySQL 8.0 brings numerous advancements, it also deprecates and removes certain features:

    • Deprecated Features: Deprecated features include character sets, authentication mechanisms, account management options, SQL modifiers, environment variables, and several functions and statements.
    • Removed Features: Removed features include system variables, encryption-related functions, query cache-related functionality, installation utilities, and obsolete components.

    Release History of Previous MySQL Versions

    Version Name Released Date End of Support Description
    MySQL 5.1 14-11-2008 December 2013 Includes releases from MySQL 5.0 to MySQL 5.1.73.
    MySQL 5.5 03-12-2010 December 2018 Encompasses releases from MySQL 5.5 to MySQL 5.5.62.
    MySQL 5.6 05-02-2013 February 2021 Contains releases from MySQL 5.6 to MySQL 5.5.45.
    MySQL 5.7 21-10-2015 October 2023 Includes releases from MySQL 5.7 to MySQL 5.6.27.
    MySQL 8.0 19-04-2018 April 2026 Encompasses releases from MySQL 8.0 to MySQL 8.0.21.

    MySQL Data Types

    Numeric Data Types

    • TINYINT: Small integer with a storage range of -128 to 127 (signed) or 0 to 255 (unsigned).
    • SMALLINT: Small integer with a storage range of -32768 to 32767 (signed) or 0 to 65535 (unsigned).
    • MEDIUMINT: Medium-sized integer with a storage range of -8388608 to 8388607 (signed) or 0 to 16777215 (unsigned).
    • INT: Normal-sized integer with a storage range of -2147483648 to 2147483647 (signed) or 0 to 4294967295 (unsigned).
    • BIGINT: Large integer with a storage range of -9223372036854775808 to 9223372036854775807 (signed) or 0 to 18446744073709551615 (unsigned).
    • FLOAT: Floating-point number with customizable display length and decimal precision.
    • DOUBLE: Double-precision floating-point number.
    • DECIMAL: Unpacked floating-point number with customizable display length and decimal precision.
    • BIT: Used for storing bit values.

    Date and Time Data Types

    • YEAR
    • DATE
    • TIME
    • DATETIME
    • TIMESTAMP

    String Data Types

    • CHAR
    • VARCHAR
    • TINYTEXT
    • TEXT
    • MEDIUMTEXT
    • LONGTEXT
    • BINARY
    • VARBINARY
    • ENUM
    • SET

    Binary Large Object (BLOB) Data Types

    • TINYBLOB
    • BLOB
    • MEDIUMBLOB
    • LONGBLOB

    Spatial Data Types

    • GEOMETRY
    • POINT
    • POLYGON
    • LINESTRING
    • GEOMETRYCOLLECTION
    • MULTILINESTRING
    • MULTIPOINT
    • MULTIPOLYGON

    JSON Data Type

    Introduced in MySQL version 5.7.8, the JSON data type enables efficient storage and retrieval of JSON documents. This native data type offers automatic validation and optimized storage for JSON data.

    User-Defined Variable

    User-defined variables enable the passing of values between statements within MySQL. These variables are prefixed with the \'@\' symbol and can store values such as integers, floats, decimals, strings, or NULL. Here\'s how to declare and initialize a user-defined variable using the SET or SELECT statement:

    -- Using SET statement SET @var_name = value; -- Using SELECT statement SELECT @var_name := value;

    Example 1:

    SET @name = \'peter\'; SELECT @name;

    Local Variable

    Local variables are not prefixed with the \'@\' symbol and are strongly typed. They are scoped within a stored program block and are primarily used in stored procedures. Local variables are declared using the DECLARE keyword, optionally with a DEFAULT value. Here\'s the syntax for declaring a local variable:

    DECLARE variable_name datatype(size) [DEFAULT default_value];

    Example:

    DECLARE total_price DECIMAL(8,2) DEFAULT 0.0;

    System Variable

    System variables are predefined variables that configure MySQL\'s operation. They can be modified dynamically using the SET statement without requiring a server restart. System variables can be GLOBAL, SESSION, or a combination of both types. Global variables persist throughout the server\'s lifecycle, while session variables are active only for a specific session. You can view system variable names and their values using the SHOW VARIABLES command or by querying the @@var_name syntax.

    Example:

    SHOW VARIABLES LIKE \'%wait_timeout%\'; SELECT @@key_buffer_size;

    MySQL Installation Guide

    Prerequisites:

    • MySQL Setup Software
    • Microsoft .NET Framework 4.5.2
    • Microsoft Visual C++ Redistributable for Visual Studio 2019
    • At least 4 GB of RAM (6 GB recommended)

    Download MySQL:

    1. Visit the official MySQL website and navigate to the download section.
    2. Choose the community server edition for your operating system, such as Windows.
    3. Select the desired version number of the MySQL community server to download.

    Installing MySQL on Windows:

    1. After downloading the setup, unzip the file and double-click the MSI installer (.exe) file.
    2. Choose the setup type in the wizard. Opt for the "Full" option to install all MySQL products and features.
    3. Proceed through the installation wizard, resolving any requirements or dependencies as prompted.
    4. Confirm the installation of the selected products when prompted.
    5. Configure the MySQL Server by selecting the appropriate options, such as standalone MySQL server or InnoDB Cluster, and specify connectivity options.
    6. Set the MySQL Root Password and configure the Windows Service to start the server.
    7. Apply the server configuration and complete the installation.
    8. Optionally, configure the MySQL Router if needed.
    9. Connect to the MySQL server using the root password set during installation.
    10. Verify the applied configurations and execute them.
    11. Complete the installation wizard and finish the MySQL package installation.

    Verify MySQL Installation:

    1. Open the MySQL Command Line Client and enter your password if required.
    2. Verify the installation by executing SQL commands, such as

      show databases

      , to view existing databases.

    MySQL Connection Methods

    1. MySQL Server Connection Using Command-line Client:

    The MySQL command-line client allows interaction with the database server in both interactive and non-interactive modes. You can access this program in the bin directory of your MySQL installation folder.

    To open the MySQL command prompt:

    mysql

    If MySQL is in your PATH, you can use the following command to connect to the MySQL Server:

    mysql -u root -p

    In this command,

    -u root

    specifies connecting with the root user account, and

    -p

    prompts MySQL to request a password. After entering the password, you\'ll be connected to the MySQL server, where you can execute commands like

    SHOW DATABASES

    to display available databases.

    2. Connect to Database Server Using MySQL Workbench:

    MySQL Workbench provides a graphical interface to connect to MySQL servers. Follow these steps:

    1. Launch MySQL Workbench and navigate to the Database menu.
    2. Choose the "Connect to Database" option or press CTRL+U.
    3. Fill in the connection details, such as connection name, username, and password.
    4. Test the connection and save the configuration.
    5. Click on the newly created connection to access the databases and execute queries.

    3. Connect to MySQL Server Using PHP Script:

    You can use PHP scripts to connect to a MySQL server. Here\'s a basic example:

    <?php $servername = \'localhost:3306\'; $username = \'your_username\'; $password = \'your_password\'; $conn = mysql_connect($servername, $username, $password); if(! $conn ) { die(\'Connection failed: \' . mysql_error()); } echo \'Connection is successful\'; mysql_close($conn); ?>

    Replace

    your_username

    and

    your_password

    with your MySQL username and password. This script attempts to connect to the MySQL server, and if successful, displays a message indicating the connection is successful.

    @string/what_is_mysql @string/mysql_features @string/mysql_versions @string/mysql_data_types @string/mysql_variables @string/install_mysql @string/mysql_connection

    MySQL Workbench (Download and Installation)

    MySQL Workbench is a versatile visual database design and administration tool developed and maintained by Oracle. It offers various functionalities such as SQL development, data modeling, data migration, and server administration tools for managing MySQL databases. Available for major operating systems like Mac OS, Windows, and Linux, MySQL Workbench fully supports MySQL Server version 5.6 and higher.

    MySQL Workbench Functionalities:

    • SQL Development: Allows executing SQL queries and managing database connections via a built-in SQL editor.
    • Data Modeling (Design): Enables creating and editing database schema graphically, performing reverse and forward engineering, and comprehensive table editing.
    • Server Administration: Facilitates administering MySQL Server instances, including user management, audit data inspection, database health monitoring, backup, and recovery.
    • Data Migration: Supports migrating data and objects from various RDBMS like Microsoft SQL Server, SQLite, PostgreSQL, etc., to MySQL.
    • MySQL Enterprise Support: Offers support for MySQL Enterprise products such as MySQL Firewall, MySQL Enterprise Backup, and MySQL Audit.

    MySQL Workbench Editions:

    • Community Edition: Open-source and freely downloadable under the GPL license, supported by a large developer community.
    • Standard Edition: Commercial edition designed for high-performance and scalable Online Transaction Processing (OLTP) applications.
    • Enterprise Edition: Commercial edition with advanced features, management tools, and technical support for achieving scalability, security, and reliability.

    MySQL Workbench Environment Setup:

    Prerequisites:

    • MySQL Server
    • MySQL Workbench
    • Microsoft .NET Framework 4.5.2
    • Microsoft Visual C++ Redistributable for Visual Studio 2019
    • RAM: 4 GB (6 GB recommended)

    Installation Steps:

    1. Install the MySQL Community Server by running the installer.
    2. Choose the Custom setup type and select MySQL Server and Workbench components.
    3. Configure MySQL Server and Workbench settings according to your requirements.
    4. Execute the installation process, including downloading and installing necessary components.
    5. Complete the installation by applying server configuration and finishing the product configuration.
    6. Launch MySQL Workbench and set up database connections using the provided credentials.

    MySQL Workbench Administration Tool:

    The Administration Tool provides essential functionalities for securing and managing databases, including:

    • User Administration: Managing user accounts, privileges, and passwords.
    • Server Configuration: Configuring advanced server settings for optimal performance.
    • Database Backup and Restoration: Importing/exporting MySQL dump files for backup and restoration.
    • Server Logs: Viewing server logs for monitoring and troubleshooting purposes.
    • Performance Dashboard: Providing statistical insights into server performance.

    MySQL Workbench Database Operations:

    • Create, Alter, Drop Database: Creating a new database and altering or dropping existing ones using MySQL Workbench\'s intuitive interface.
    • Create, Alter, Drop Table: Creating, modifying, and deleting tables within a database, including altering column properties and table structure.
    • Insert, Read, Update, Delete Data Rows: Inserting, retrieving, updating, and deleting data rows within tables using MySQL Workbench\'s visual interface.
    • Export and Import Database (Table): Exporting and importing databases or tables via MySQL Workbench\'s data export and import functionalities, allowing for flexible backup and restoration options.
    @string/mysql_workbench

    MySQL User Creation

    The MySQL user represents a record in the USER table of the MySQL server, containing login details, account privileges, and host information. Creating a user in MySQL is crucial for managing and accessing databases effectively.

    MySQL CREATE USER Statement

    The MySQL

    CREATE USER

    statement allows the creation of a new user account on the database server, offering options for authentication, SSL/TLS, resource limits, roles, and password management. It\'s essential to have appropriate privileges, such as the global

    CREATE USER

    privilege or the

    INSERT

    privilege for the MySQL system schema, to execute this statement. Using the

    IF NOT EXISTS

    clause prevents errors when attempting to create a user that already exists, instead issuing a warning.

    Why Users are Required in MySQL Server?

    Upon MySQL server installation, only a ROOT user account exists, providing full control over databases. However, to grant database access to others while limiting control, non-root users with specific privileges are created.

    Syntax:

    CREATE USER [IF NOT EXISTS] account_name IDENTIFIED BY \'password\';

    In the syntax, account_name consists of a username and a hostname, separated by \'@\'. The username represents the user\'s name, while the hostname signifies the host from which the user can connect to the database server. The hostname is optional; omitting it allows connections from any host on the server.

    Example:

    CREATE USER peter@localhost IDENTIFIED BY \'jtp12345\';

    Granting Privileges to the MySQL New User

    MySQL provides various privileges for new user accounts, including:

    • ALL PRIVILEGES: Grants all privileges to the user account.
    • CREATE, DROP, DELETE, INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE: Grants specific privileges for database operations.

    Example:

    GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON * . * TO peter@localhost;

    Flushing Privileges and Checking Existing Privileges

    To apply changes to privileges immediately, the

    FLUSH PRIVILEGES

    command is used. To view existing privileges for a user, the

    SHOW GRANTS

    command is employed.

    Example:

    FLUSH PRIVILEGES; SHOW GRANTS FOR peter@localhost;

    MySQL DROP USER

    The MySQL

    DROP USER

    statement enables the removal of one or more user accounts and their associated privileges from the database server. If the specified account does not exist, an error is generated.

    To execute the

    DROP USER

    statement, it is necessary to possess the global

    CREATE USER

    privilege or the

    DELETE

    privilege for the MySQL system schema.

    Syntax:

    DROP USER \'account_name\';

    The account_name is specified in the format username@hostname, where username represents the account to be deleted and hostname signifies the server name associated with the user account.

    MySQL DROP USER Example:

    Below are the steps to delete an existing user from the MySQL server database:

    1. Open the MySQL server using the mysql client tool.
    2. Enter the password for the account.
    3. Execute the following command to display all users in the current MySQL server.

    SELECT user FROM mysql.user;

    To drop a user account, execute the following statement.

    DROP USER \'martin@localhost\';

    To remove multiple user accounts simultaneously, separate the account_names with a comma and execute the DROP USER statement.

    DROP USER \'john@localhost\', \'peter@localhost\';

    After successfully executing the DROP USER command, re-running the

    SELECT user FROM mysql.user;

    command will confirm the removal of the specified user accounts.

    Display List of MySQL Users

    To display the list of all users in a MySQL database server, you can use a query to retrieve data from the

    mysql.user

    table. Unfortunately, MySQL does not provide a built-in command like

    SHOW USERS

    to directly list all users. However, you can use the following query to achieve this:

    SELECT user FROM mysql.user;

    This query retrieves the user column from the

    mysql.user

    table, which contains the names of all users in the database server.

    How to Use the Query:

    1. Open the MySQL server using the mysql client tool and log in as an administrator.

      2. mysql -u root -p

    2. Enter your password when prompted.
    3. Switch to the

      mysql

      database, which contains user account information.

      4. use mysql;

    4. Execute the query to list all users.

      5. SELECT user FROM user;

    After executing this query, you will receive output similar to the following, displaying the list of users:

    +------------------+ | user | +------------------+ | user1 | | user2 | | user3 | | root | | admin | +------------------+

    If you want to retrieve more information about the users, you can execute a query to describe the structure of the

    mysql.user

    table:

    DESC mysql.user;

    To get selected information such as the hostname, password expiration status, and account locking, you can execute a query like this:

    SELECT user, host, account_locked, password_expired FROM mysql.user;

    To show the current user, you can use either the

    USER()

    or

    CURRENT_USER()

    function:

    SELECT USER();

    or

    SELECT CURRENT_USER();

    If you want to see currently logged-in users, you can query the

    information_schema.processlist

    table:

    SELECT user, host, db, command FROM information_schema.processlist;

    Change MySQL User Password

    To change the password of a user account in MySQL, you must ensure that you have the necessary details of the user account and consider any applications or services that rely on this account\'s password. MySQL provides several methods to change user passwords:

    UPDATE Statement:

    This method involves updating the user table directly and then flushing privileges to ensure the changes take effect. However, this method may vary depending on the MySQL version you are using.

    For versions prior to 5.7.6:

    USE mysql; UPDATE user SET password = PASSWORD(\'new_password\') WHERE user =\'username\' AND host = \'hostname\'; FLUSH PRIVILEGES;

    For versions 5.7.6 and higher:

    USE mysql; UPDATE user SET authentication_string = PASSWORD(\'new_password\') WHERE user = \'username\' AND host = \'hostname\'; FLUSH PRIVILEGES;

    SET PASSWORD Statement:

    This method allows you to set the password directly using the SET PASSWORD statement. No need to flush privileges separately.

    SET PASSWORD FOR \'username\'@\'hostname\' = PASSWORD(\'new_password\');

    For versions 5.7.6 and higher, you can use:

    SET PASSWORD FOR \'username\'@\'hostname\' = \'new_password\';

    ALTER USER Statement:

    Another approach is to use the ALTER USER statement, which specifies the user and sets the new password.

    ALTER USER \'username\'@\'hostname\' IDENTIFIED BY \'new_password\';

    When executing any of these statements, ensure you replace \'username\' with the actual username and \'hostname\' with the host from which the user connects. Additionally, replace \'new_password\' with the desired new password for the user account.

    If you need to reset the MySQL root account password, you might need to stop and restart the MySQL database server without grant table validation, depending on the situation.

    @string/mysql_create_user @string/mysql_drop_user @string/mysql_show_user @string/change_user_password

    Create MySQL Database

    Creating a database in MySQL is a fundamental operation that involves storing records in an organized manner for efficient data management. MySQL provides various methods for creating databases, including using the MySQL Command Line Client or MySQL Workbench.

    MySQL Command Line Client:

    To create a new database using the MySQL Command Line Client, you can utilize the CREATE DATABASE statement with the following syntax:

    CREATE DATABASE [IF NOT EXISTS] database_name [CHARACTER SET charset_name] [COLLATE collation_name];

    Explanation of Parameters:

    • database_name:

      Specifies the name of the new database. It should be unique within the MySQL server instance. The IF NOT EXISTS clause prevents an error if the database already exists.

    • charset_name:

      Optional parameter indicating the character set to be used for storing characters. If not provided, MySQL uses the default character set.

    • collation_name:

      Optional parameter specifying how characters should be compared within the character set.

    Example:

    CREATE DATABASE employeesdb;

    After executing this statement, you can verify the creation of the database using SHOW CREATE DATABASE employeedb; and SHOW DATABASES; commands. Finally, you can access the created database using USE employeedb;.

    MySQL Workbench:

    MySQL Workbench is a graphical tool for database design, development, and administration. It provides a user-friendly interface for creating databases.

    Here\'s how to create a database using MySQL Workbench:

    1. Launch MySQL Workbench and log in.
    2. Navigate to the Schema menu under the Navigation tab.
    3. Right-click under the Schema menu and select "Create Schema" or click the database icon.
    4. Enter the new database name (e.g., employeedb) and use default settings for character set and collation.
    5. Click "Apply" and then "Finish" to complete the process.

    The newly created database will appear in the Schema menu. You can view additional information about the database by selecting it and clicking on the \'i\' icon.

    MySQL Workbench provides comprehensive tools for database administration, including table creation, data manipulation, and user management.

    =

    MySQL USE Database

    The "SELECT Database" operation in MySQL is employed to choose a specific database to operate on. This command becomes necessary when dealing with multiple databases hosted on a MySQL Server.

    To accomplish this, the SQL command USE is utilized to designate a particular database for operations.

    Syntax:

    USE database_name;

    Example:

    Let\'s consider an example where we want to work with a database named "customers":

    USE customers;

    By executing this query, we instruct MySQL to switch the current context to the "customers" database, allowing subsequent operations to be performed within this specific database.

    This approach streamlines database management in scenarios where there are multiple databases hosted on the MySQL Server, ensuring that operations are directed to the intended database.

    MySQL SHOW DATABASES

    When operating within the MySQL server environment, a common requirement is to display or list the databases hosted on the server. This task is accomplished using the

    SHOW DATABASES

    command, which provides a comprehensive listing of all available databases. Below is a detailed guide on how to execute this command and perform pattern matching for more refined database listings.

    Listing Databases:

    To list all databases stored on the MySQL server, the following command is utilized:

    mysql> SHOW DATABASES;

    Upon executing this command, a display of all databases hosted on the server is provided, facilitating easy navigation and management of database resources.

    Alternative Command:

    MySQL also supports an alternative command,

    SHOW SCHEMAS

    , which serves as a synonym for

    SHOW DATABASES

    and yields identical results. Both commands are interchangeable, providing flexibility in database management tasks.

    Pattern Matching for Filtered Listings:

    MySQL\'s

    SHOW DATABASES

    command offers the capability to filter database listings using pattern matching techniques, enhancing search precision. Pattern matching can be achieved using the

    LIKE

    clause or the more flexible

    WHERE

    clause within the SQL statement.

    Syntax:

    mysql> SHOW DATABASES LIKE pattern; OR, mysql> SHOW DATABASES WHERE expression;

    Pattern matching with the

    "%"

    sign enables matching of zero, one, or multiple characters, thereby refining the search results based on specified patterns.

    Example:

    mysql> SHOW DATABASES LIKE "%schema";

    This command will display databases matching the pattern

    "%schema"

    , presenting a filtered database listing.

    Advanced Search with WHERE Clause:

    For more complex search criteria, the

    WHERE

    clause can be utilized with the

    SHOW DATABASES

    command. By querying the

    schemata

    table within the information schema, intricate search conditions can be applied to retrieve targeted database information.

    mysql> SELECT schema_name FROM information_schema.schemata WHERE schema_name LIKE \'s%\';

    Executing this statement will yield databases whose schema names begin with the letter

    "s"

    , providing a tailored database listing based on specific search criteria.

    MySQL DROP DATABASE

    To efficiently remove a MySQL database, the MySQL

    DROP DATABASE

    command can be employed. This command effectively deletes the specified database, along with all its associated tables, indexes, and constraints, permanently eliminating all stored data. However, caution must be exercised when using this command, as irreversible data loss will occur. If the database does not exist on the MySQL server, executing the

    DROP DATABASE

    statement will result in an error.

    Using MySQL Command Line Client:

    The

    DROP DATABASE

    statement is utilized to delete an existing database in MySQL, employing the following syntax:

    DROP DATABASE [IF EXISTS] database_name;

    Alternatively, the

    DROP SCHEMA

    statement can be used interchangeably with the same syntax, as "schema" and "database" are synonyms in MySQL.

    Parameter Explanation:

    • database_name: Specifies the name of the database to be deleted. This name should be unique within the MySQL server instance.
    • IF EXISTS: An optional parameter used to prevent errors when attempting to remove a database that does not exist.

    Example:

    After connecting to the MySQL console and verifying available databases using the

    SHOW DATABASES

    statement, execute the following command to delete a database named "mytestdb_copy":

    DROP DATABASE mytestdb_copy;

    Upon successful execution, the specified database will be removed from the server.

    Using MySQL Workbench:

    MySQL Workbench provides a graphical interface for database management tasks, including database deletion. Follow these steps to drop a database:

    1. Navigate to the Schema menu in the Navigation tab.
    2. Right-click on the database to be deleted, e.g., "testdb_copy," and select the "Drop Schema" option.
    3. Confirm the deletion process through the displayed dialog box. For a safer approach, review the generated SQL statement before executing it.
    4. Click the "Execute" button to proceed with the deletion.

    Upon successful execution, MySQL Workbench will confirm the deletion of the database, and it will no longer be listed in the Schemas tab.

    Copy MySQL Database

    Creating a copy or clone of a MySQL database involves duplicating the database structure and its data into a new database. This process is useful for various purposes, including data backups and structural modifications. Here\'s how you can copy a MySQL database to another one:

    Using SQL Commands:

    1. First, create a new database using the

      CREATE DATABASE

      statement:

      2. CREATE DATABASE new_database_name;

    2. Next, use the

      mysqldump

      tool to export the structure and data of the original database into an SQL file:

      3. mysqldump -u username -p original_database_name > /path/to/dumpfile.sql

    3. Once the dump file is created, import it into the newly created database using the

      mysql

      command:

      4. mysql -u username -p new_database_name < /path/to/dumpfile.sql

    Using MySQL Workbench:

    1. Open MySQL Workbench and connect to your MySQL server.
    2. From the Navigator panel, right-click on the database you want to copy and select "Dump SQL File."
    3. Choose the location to save the SQL dump file and click "Start Dump."
    4. Once the dump process is complete, close the "Dump Data" wizard.
