    Common MySQL Queries

    Here are common MySQL queries for performing various database operations:

    MySQL Create Database:

    To create a new database:

    CREATE DATABASE db1;

    MySQL Select/Use Database:

    To select a database to work with:

    USE db1;

    MySQL Create Table:

    To create a new table:

    CREATE TABLE customers ( id INT(10), name VARCHAR(50), city VARCHAR(50), PRIMARY KEY (id) );

    MySQL Alter Table:

    To alter an existing table (e.g., add a column):

    ALTER TABLE customers ADD age VARCHAR(50);

    MySQL Insert Query:

    To insert records into a table:

    INSERT INTO customers VALUES (101, \'rahul\', \'delhi\');

    MySQL Update Query:

    To update records in a table:

    UPDATE customers SET name=\'bob\', city=\'london\' WHERE id=101;

    MySQL Delete Query:

    To delete records from a table:

    DELETE FROM customers WHERE id=101;

    MySQL Select Query:

    To fetch records from a table:

    SELECT * FROM customers;

    MySQL Truncate Table Query:

    To remove all records from a table (without removing the table structure):

    TRUNCATE TABLE customers;

    MySQL Drop Query:

    To drop a table:

    DROP TABLE customers;

    These queries provide a basic understanding of how to perform common database operations in MySQL. Adjust the table names, column names, and values as needed for your specific use case.

    Constraints in MySQL

    Constraints in MySQL are essential for maintaining data integrity and enforcing rules on table columns. They can be divided into column-level constraints, which apply to individual columns, and table-level constraints, which apply to the entire table.

    Column Level Constraints:

    To create column-level constraints:

    CREATE TABLE new_table_name ( col_name1 datatype constraint, col_name2 datatype constraint, col_name3 datatype constraint, ......... );

    Table Level Constraints:

    To create table-level constraints:

    CREATE TABLE new_table_name ( col_name1 datatype, col_name2 datatype, col_name3 datatype, ......... constraint_name constraint (column1, column2, ...), ......... );

    Common Constraints in MySQL:

    • NOT NULL: Ensures a column cannot have NULL values.
    • UNIQUE: Ensures all values in a column are unique.
    • CHECK: Enforces a condition on column values.
    • DEFAULT: Provides a default value for a column.
    • PRIMARY KEY: Identifies each record uniquely and must contain unique values.
    • AUTO_INCREMENT: Automatically generates a unique value for each new row.
    • ENUM: Limits the values that can be inserted into a column.
    • INDEX: Improves query performance by creating an index on a column or set of columns.
    • FOREIGN KEY: Establishes a relationship between two tables, enforcing referential integrity.

    These constraints help maintain data consistency and accuracy, ensuring that the database remains reliable and secure.

    MySQL INSERT Statement

    The MySQL INSERT statement is utilized to add or store data in MySQL tables within the database. Insertions can be performed in two ways: inserting a single record or inserting multiple records using a single query.

    Syntax:

    Insert record in a single row:

    INSERT INTO table_name (field1, field2,...fieldN) VALUES (value1, value2,...valueN);

    Insert record in multiple rows:

    INSERT INTO table_name VALUES (value1, value2,...valueN), (value1, value2,...valueN), ... (value1, value2,...valueN);

    In the syntax:

    • We specify the table name and a comma-separated list of columns (optional).
    • We provide a list of corresponding values for the columns after the VALUES clause.
    • If inserting multiple records, each row is enclosed within parentheses and separated by commas.

    MySQL INSERT Example:

    Let\'s create a table named "People" in the database:

    CREATE TABLE People( id int NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT, name varchar(45) NOT NULL, occupation varchar(35) NOT NULL, age int, PRIMARY KEY (id) );

    Single Record Insertion:

    INSERT INTO People (id, name, occupation, age) VALUES (101, \'Peter\', \'Engineer\', 32);

    Multiple Records Insertion:

    INSERT INTO People VALUES (102, \'Joseph\', \'Developer\', 30), (103, \'Mike\', \'Leader\', 28), (104, \'Stephen\', \'Scientist\', 45);

    Partial Fields Insertion:

    INSERT INTO People (name, occupation) VALUES (\'Stephen\', \'Scientist\'), (\'Bob\', \'Actor\');

    Inserting Date in MySQL Table:

    We can also insert dates into MySQL tables using the INSERT statement. MySQL provides various date types such as DATE, TIMESTAMP, DATETIME, and YEAR. The default format for dates in MySQL is YYYY-MM-DD.

    Basic Syntax:

    INSERT INTO table_name (column_name, column_date) VALUES (\'DATE: Manual Date\', \'2008-07-04\');

    If inserting a date in the mm/dd/yyyy format:

    INSERT INTO table_name VALUES (STR_TO_DATE(date_value, format_specifier));

    These INSERT statements can be verified by querying the "People" table using SELECT * FROM People;.

    MySQL UPDATE Query

    The MySQL UPDATE query is a Data Manipulation Language (DML) statement used to modify data in MySQL tables within the database. It allows us to change existing records over time, making necessary updates to values in tables.

    Syntax:

    The generic syntax of the UPDATE command to modify data in a MySQL table is as follows:

    UPDATE table_name SET column_name1 = new_value1, column_name2 = new_value2, ... [WHERE condition];

    In the syntax:

    • table_name: Name of the table where the update operation will be performed.
    • column_name: Name of the column(s) to be updated.
    • new_value: New value(s) to be assigned to the specified column(s).
    • WHERE: Optional clause used to specify conditions for updating rows. If omitted, all rows in the table will be updated.

    Example:

    Let\'s consider a table named "trainer" in the "testdb" database for the examples.

    Update Single Column:

    UPDATE trainer SET email = \'mike@tutorialandexamples.com\' WHERE course_name = \'Java\';

    Update Multiple Columns:

    UPDATE People SET name = \'Mary\', occupation = \'Content Writer\' WHERE id = 105;

    UPDATE Statement to Replace String:

    UPDATE Trainer_table SET email = REPLACE(email,\'@javatpoint.com\',\'@tutorialandexample.com\') WHERE course_name = \'Testing\';

    In these examples:

    • The first query updates the email ID for the Java course.
    • The second query updates both the name and occupation columns where the ID is 105.
    • The third query demonstrates how to replace part of a string within a column.

    After successful execution, the

    SELECT * FROM table_name;

    query can be used to verify the changes made to the table.

    These UPDATE statements help maintain data accuracy and integrity by modifying records as needed within MySQL tables.

    MySQL DELETE Statement

    The MySQL DELETE statement is employed to remove records from a MySQL table that are no longer needed in the database. This query deletes entire rows from the table and provides the count of deleted rows. It also permits the deletion of multiple records from the table within a single query, which is advantageous when removing large numbers of records. The DELETE statement in MySQL can also remove data based on specified conditions.

    It\'s crucial to note that once records are deleted using this query, they cannot be recovered. Therefore, it\'s recommended to create a backup of your database before performing any deletions. Database backups enable the restoration of data when needed in the future.

    Syntax:

    The syntax for using the DELETE statement is as follows:

    DELETE FROM table_name WHERE condition;

    In this syntax:

    • table_name: Specifies the name of the table from which data will be deleted.
    • condition: Optional condition specifying which records should be deleted. If omitted, the DELETE statement will remove all records from the table.

    If deletion from multiple tables is required using a single DELETE query, the JOIN clause must be added to the DELETE statement.

    If the intention is to delete all records from a table without needing to know the count of deleted rows, the TRUNCATE TABLE statement provides better performance.

    Examples:

    Let\'s use the "Employees" and "Payment" tables for demonstrating the DELETE statement. Suppose these tables contain the following data:

    Delete a Specific Record:

    DELETE FROM Employees WHERE emp_id = 107;

    Delete All Records:

    DELETE FROM Employees;

    Delete Records with LIMIT Clause:

    DELETE FROM Employees ORDER BY name LIMIT 3;

    Delete Records with JOIN Clause:

    DELETE Employees, Payment FROM Employees INNER JOIN Payment ON Employees.emp_id = Payment.emp_id WHERE Employees.emp_id = 102;

    After execution of these queries, the respective records will be deleted from the tables as per the specified conditions.

    MySQL SELECT Statement

    The SELECT statement in MySQL is fundamental for fetching data from one or more tables in a database. It allows retrieval of records based on specified criteria and can work with various scripting languages like PHP, Ruby, and more.

    Syntax:

    The general syntax of the SELECT statement in MySQL is as follows:

    SELECT field_name1, field_name2, ... field_nameN FROM table_name1, table_name2, ... [WHERE condition] [GROUP BY field_name(s)] [HAVING condition] [ORDER BY field_name(s)] [OFFSET M] [LIMIT N];

    In this syntax:

    • field_name(s): Specifies the columns to be returned in the result set. Use an asterisk (*) to retrieve all fields of a table.
    • table_name(s): Names of the tables from which data is fetched.
    • WHERE: Optional clause specifying conditions for matching records.
    • GROUP BY: Optional clause used for grouping data based on one or more columns.
    • HAVING: Optional clause that works with the GROUP BY clause to filter grouped rows.
    • ORDER BY: Optional clause for sorting records in the result set.
    • OFFSET: Optional parameter specifying the row to start returning results from. Default is zero.
    • LIMIT: Optional parameter limiting the number of returned records.

    Examples:

    Let\'s consider the table "employee_detail" with the provided data for demonstrating the SELECT statement.

    Retrieve a Single Column:

    SELECT Name FROM employee_detail;

    Query Multiple Columns:

    SELECT Name, Email, City FROM employee_detail;

    Fetch Data from All Columns:

    SELECT * FROM employee_detail;

    Using Aggregate Function with HAVING Clause:

    SELECT Name, City, SUM(working_hours) AS "Total working hours" FROM employee_detail GROUP BY Name HAVING SUM(working_hours) > 5;

    Retrieving Records from Multiple Tables with JOIN:

    SELECT cust_name, city, order_num, order_date FROM customer INNER JOIN orders ON customer.cust_id = orders.order_id WHERE order_date < \'2020-04-30\' ORDER BY cust_name;

    These examples illustrate how the SELECT statement works in MySQL, providing the flexibility to retrieve data based on specific criteria from one or more tables in a database.

    MySQL REPLACE Statement

    The REPLACE statement in MySQL serves as an extension of the SQL Standard, functioning similarly to the INSERT statement. However, it has a unique feature: if an old row matches the new record in the table for a PRIMARY KEY or a UNIQUE index, the old row is deleted before the new row is added.

    This functionality becomes necessary when updating existing records in a table to ensure they stay updated. Attempting to use a standard INSERT query for this purpose would result in a Duplicate entry error for PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE key constraints. In such cases, the REPLACE statement comes to the rescue, as it handles two possible actions:

    • If no matching value is found in the existing data row, a standard INSERT statement is performed.
    • If a duplicate record is found, the REPLACE command first deletes the existing row and then adds the new record to the table.

    In essence, the REPLACE statement combines the functionalities of DELETE and INSERT.

    Syntax:

    The syntax of the REPLACE statement in MySQL is as follows:

    REPLACE [INTO] table_name(column_list) VALUES(value_list);

    Example:

    Let\'s illustrate the usage of the REPLACE statement in MySQL with an example. First, we create a table named "Person":

    CREATE TABLE Person ( ID int AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, Name varchar(45) DEFAULT NULL, Email varchar(45) DEFAULT NULL UNIQUE, City varchar(25) DEFAULT NULL );

    Next, we populate the table with some records:

    INSERT INTO Person(ID, Name, Email, City) VALUES (1,\'Mike\', \'mike@javatpoint.com\', \'California\'), (2, \'Alexandar\', \'alexandar@javatpoint.com\', \'New York\'), (3, \'Adam\', \'adam@javatpoint.com\', \'Los Angeles\'), (4, \'Peter\', \'Peter@javatpoint.com\', \'Alaska\');

    Now, let\'s replace the city of the person with ID 4:

    REPLACE INTO Person (id, city) VALUES(4,\'Amsterdam\');

    After execution, we query the table again to verify the replacement.

    Additional Features:

    We can use the REPLACE statement to update a row\'s data in a table:

    REPLACE INTO Person SET ID = 1, Name = \'Mike\', City = \'Birmingham\';

    The REPLACE statement can be used to insert data from a SELECT statement:

    REPLACE INTO Person(Name, City) SELECT Name, City FROM Person WHERE id = 2;

    MySQL INSERT ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE Statement

    The Insert on Duplicate Key Update statement extends the functionality of the INSERT statement in MySQL. When the ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE clause is specified in a SQL statement and a row would cause a duplicate error value in a UNIQUE or PRIMARY KEY index column, the existing row is updated with the new row values.

    In essence, if we attempt to insert new values into a table and encounter a duplicate row in a UNIQUE or PRIMARY KEY column, the ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE clause allows us to update the existing row with the new values instead of causing an error.

    For example, if a column col1 is defined as UNIQUE and contains the value 10 in table tab1, executing the following two statements would have similar effects:

    INSERT INTO tab1 (col1, col2, col3) VALUES (10, 20, 30) ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE col3 = col3 + 1; UPDATE tab1 SET col3 = col3 + 1 WHERE col1 = 1;

    This ensures that if the inserted row matches with more than one unique index in the table, the ON DUPLICATE KEY statement only updates the first matched unique index. Therefore, it is not recommended to use this statement on tables containing more than one unique index.

    Syntax:

    The syntax for the Insert on Duplicate Key Update statement in MySQL is as follows:

    INSERT INTO table (column_names) VALUES (data) ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE column1 = expression, column2 = expression…;

    In this syntax, the INSERT statement is followed by the ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE clause, which includes column-value pair assignments for updating existing rows. The VALUES() function retrieves column values from the INSERT portion and is particularly useful for multi-row inserts.

    MySQL provides information about the affected rows with the ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE statement:

    • If a new row is inserted into a table, it returns one affected row.
    • If an existing row is updated in a table, it returns two affected rows.
    • If an existing row is updated using its current values in the table, it returns zero affected rows.

    Example:

    Let\'s illustrate the usage of the INSERT ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE clause in MySQL with an example.

    First, we create a table named "Student":

    CREATE TABLE Student ( Stud_ID int AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, Name varchar(45) DEFAULT NULL, Email varchar(45) DEFAULT NULL, City varchar(25) DEFAULT NULL );

    Next, we insert data into the table:

    INSERT INTO Student(Stud_ID, Name, Email, City) VALUES (1, \'Stephen\', \'stephen@javatpoint.com\', \'Texas\'), (2, \'Joseph\', \'joseph@javatpoint.com\', \'Alaska\'), (3, \'Peter\', \'peter@javatpoint.com\', \'California\');

    After verification of the insert operation, we have three rows in the table.

    Next, we attempt to add another row with a duplicate value in the Stud_ID column:

    INSERT INTO Student(Stud_ID, Name, Email, City) VALUES (4, \'John\', \'john@javatpoint.com\', \'New York\') ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE City = \'California\';

    MySQL updates the existing row with the new values instead of inserting a duplicate row, and we receive a message indicating that two rows were affected.

    MySQL INSERT IGNORE Statement

    The INSERT IGNORE statement in MySQL offers a unique feature that allows the insertion of rows into a table while ignoring any invalid rows. This is particularly useful when inserting multiple rows in bulk, as it ensures that the operation continues uninterrupted even if some rows violate constraints or duplicate existing data.

    Explanation:

    • Handling Duplicate Values: When inserting rows into a table with a primary key or unique constraint, attempting to insert a duplicate key would normally result in an error. However, with INSERT IGNORE, MySQL ignores such errors and continues inserting the remaining rows without halting the operation.
    • Ignoring NULL Values: If a column in the table has a NOT NULL constraint and an INSERT IGNORE statement attempts to insert a NULL value into that column, MySQL ignores the error and proceeds with the insertion of other rows.
    • Partitioned Tables: When inserting data into a partitioned table, INSERT IGNORE ensures that if the entered values do not match the format of the listed partitions, it does not interrupt the insertion process but simply ignores the problematic rows.

    Syntax:

    The syntax for using INSERT IGNORE in MySQL is as follows:

    INSERT IGNORE INTO table_name (column_names) VALUES (value_list), (value_list), ...;

    In this syntax:

    • table_name

      is the name of the table into which you want to insert data.

    • column_names

      specifies the columns into which you want to insert values.

    • value_list

      represents the values to be inserted into the respective columns.

    Example:

    Let\'s illustrate the usage of INSERT IGNORE with an example:

    Suppose we have a table named "Student" with columns Stud_ID, Name, Email, and City, where Email has a UNIQUE constraint:

    CREATE TABLE Student ( Stud_ID int AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, Name varchar(45) DEFAULT NULL, Email varchar(45) NOT NULL UNIQUE, City varchar(25) DEFAULT NULL );

    After inserting some initial records into the table, we can attempt to insert additional rows, including some that violate the UNIQUE constraint:

    INSERT IGNORE INTO Student (Stud_ID, Name, Email, City) VALUES (4, \'Donald\', \'donald@javatpoint.com\', \'New York\'), (5, \'Joseph\', \'Joseph@javatpoint.com\', \'Chicago\');

    MySQL will successfully insert the rows that do not violate constraints and generate a warning for the rows that were ignored due to constraint violations.

    The INSERT IGNORE statement ensures that the insertion process continues smoothly, even in the presence of errors, providing a convenient way to handle bulk inserts without interruptions.

    MySQL INSERT INTO SELECT Statement

    The INSERT INTO SELECT statement in MySQL provides a convenient way to copy data from one table to another. This feature is particularly useful when you need to transfer or summarize data from one table to another quickly and efficiently.

    Explanation:

    • Copying Data: The INSERT INTO SELECT statement allows you to insert values into a table by selecting data from another table. This means you can copy data from one table and insert it into another in a single query.
    • Matching Columns: When using INSERT INTO SELECT, the columns in the source table must match the columns in the target table. If necessary, you can specify specific columns to copy data from using the column list.
    • Filtering Data: You can apply conditions using the WHERE clause to filter the data being copied from the source table. This allows you to selectively insert only the rows that meet specific criteria.

    Syntax:

    The basic syntax for using INSERT INTO SELECT in MySQL is as follows:

    INSERT INTO target_table (column_list) SELECT column_list FROM source_table WHERE condition;

    In this syntax:

    • target_table

      is the name of the table where you want to insert the data.

    • source_table

      is the name of the table from which you want to select the data.

    • column_list

      specifies the columns you want to insert data into or select data from.

    • condition

      is an optional clause that allows you to filter the data being selected.

    Example:

    Let\'s illustrate the usage of INSERT INTO SELECT with an example:

    Suppose we have a table named "person" with columns id, name, email, and city. We want to copy data from this table to another table named "person_info" but only for persons located in the city of "Texas".

    We\'ll first create the "person_info" table with the same structure as the "person" table:

    CREATE TABLE person_info ( person_id int AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, person_name varchar(45) NOT NULL, email varchar(45) NOT NULL, city varchar(25) NOT NULL );

    Next, we\'ll use the INSERT INTO SELECT statement to copy the desired data from the "person" table to the "person_info" table:

    INSERT INTO person_info (person_name, email, city) SELECT name, email, city FROM person WHERE city = \'Texas\';

    After executing this statement, only the data for persons located in the city of "Texas" will be copied from the "person" table to the "person_info" table.

    Additionally, if you want to copy all data from the "person" table to the "person_info" table without any filtering, you can use the

    INSERT INTO ... SELECT * FROM ...

    syntax:

    INSERT INTO person_info SELECT * FROM person;

    This statement will copy all rows and columns from the "person" table to the "person_info" table.

    @string/mysql_queries @string/mysql_constraints @string/mysql_insert_record @string/mysql_update_record @string/mysql_delete_record @string/mysql_select_record @string/mysql_replace @string/insert_on_duplicate_key_update @string/mysql_insert_ignore @string/insert_into_select

    MySQL WHERE Clause

    The MySQL WHERE clause is an essential component used with the SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements to filter the results or specify conditions for performing operations on the database tables.

    Table Structure:

    officer_id officer_name address
    1 John Mau
    2 Alice Lucknow
    3 Bob Delhi
    4 David Lucknow
    5 Emma Mau

    MySQL WHERE Clause with Single Condition:

    Example of retrieving data from the table "officers" where address is \'Mau\':

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE address = \'Mau\';

    MySQL WHERE Clause with AND Condition:

    Example of retrieving data from the table "officers" where address is \'Lucknow\' and officer_id is less than 5:

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE address = \'Lucknow\' AND officer_id < 5;

    MySQL WHERE Clause with OR Condition:

    Example of retrieving data from the table "officers" where address is \'Lucknow\' or \'Mau\':

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE address = \'Lucknow\' OR address = \'Mau\';

    MySQL WHERE Clause with a Combination of AND and OR Conditions:

    Example of retrieving data from the table "officers" where (address is \'Mau\' and officer_name is \'Ajeet\') or officer_id is less than 5:

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE (address = \'Mau\' AND officer_name = \'Ajeet\') OR (officer_id < 5);

    MySQL DISTINCT Clause

    The MySQL DISTINCT clause serves to eliminate duplicate records from the result set obtained from a SELECT query, ensuring that only unique records are retrieved. It is solely used in conjunction with the SELECT statement.

    MySQL DISTINCT Clause with Single Expression:

    Consider the following table:

    officer_id officer_name address
    1 John Mau
    2 Alice Lucknow
    3 Bob Delhi
    4 David Lucknow
    5 Emma Mau

    Query:

    SELECT DISTINCT address FROM officers;

    Output:

    address
    Mau
    Lucknow
    Delhi

    MySQL DISTINCT Clause with Multiple Expressions:

    Query:

    SELECT DISTINCT officer_name, address FROM officers;

    Output:

    officer_name address
    John Mau
    Alice Lucknow
    Bob Delhi
    David Lucknow
    Emma Mau

    MySQL FROM Clause

    The MySQL FROM clause is a crucial component used to specify the tables from which records should be retrieved in a query. Additionally, it facilitates the retrieval of records from multiple tables by utilizing JOIN conditions.

    MySQL FROM Clause: Retrieve Data from One Table:

    Consider the following query, which demonstrates retrieving data from a single table:

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE officer_id <= 3;

    MySQL FROM Clause: Retrieve Data from Two Tables with INNER JOIN:

    In this example, we retrieve data from two tables, "officers" and "students," using INNER JOIN:

    SELECT officers.officer_id, students.student_name FROM students INNER JOIN officers ON students.student_id = officers.officer_id;

    MySQL FROM Clause: Retrieve Data from Two Tables Using Outer Join:

    The following query demonstrates retrieving data from two tables using LEFT OUTER JOIN:

    SELECT officers.officer_id, students.student_name FROM officers LEFT OUTER JOIN students ON officers.officer_id = students.student_id;

    This query fetches records from the "officers" table and matches them with records from the "students" table based on the specified join condition.

    MySQL ORDER BY Clause

    The MySQL ORDER BY clause is essential for sorting records in ascending or descending order.

    MySQL ORDER BY: Without Using ASC/DESC Attribute:

    If you use the MySQL ORDER BY clause without specifying the ASC and DESC modifiers, you\'ll get the result in ascending order by default.

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE address = \'Lucknow\' ORDER BY officer_name;

    MySQL ORDER BY: With ASC Attribute:

    To retrieve data in ascending order explicitly, you can use the ASC attribute.

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE address = \'Lucknow\' ORDER BY officer_name ASC;

    MySQL ORDER BY: With DESC Attribute:

    Conversely, to retrieve data in descending order, you can use the DESC attribute.

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE address = \'Lucknow\' ORDER BY officer_name DESC;

    MySQL ORDER BY: Using Both ASC and DESC Attributes:

    You can also combine ASC and DESC attributes to specify the sorting order for multiple columns.

    SELECT officer_name, address FROM officers WHERE officer_id < 5 ORDER BY officer_name DESC, address ASC;

    MySQL GROUP BY Clause

    The MySQL GROUP BY clause is utilized to collect data from multiple records and group the result by one or more columns. It is commonly used in conjunction with aggregate functions like COUNT, SUM, MIN, MAX, AVG, etc.

    MySQL GROUP BY Clause with COUNT Function:

    Consider an "officers" table with the following records:

    officer_id officer_name address
    1 John New York
    2 Alice New York
    3 Bob Chicago
    4 Jane Chicago
    5 Sam Chicago

    Now, let\'s count the repetitive number of cities in the "address" column:

    SELECT address, COUNT(*) FROM officers GROUP BY address;

    MySQL GROUP BY Clause with SUM Function:

    Consider an "employees" table with the following data:

    emp_id emp_name working_hours
    1 John 8
    2 Alice 7
    3 Bob 6
    4 Jane 8
    5 Sam 7

    Now, let\'s use the SUM function to calculate the total working hours of each employee:

    SELECT emp_name, SUM(working_hours) AS "Total working hours" FROM employees GROUP BY emp_name;

    MySQL GROUP BY Clause with Other Aggregate Functions:

    Similarly, you can use other aggregate functions like MIN, MAX, and AVG with the GROUP BY clause to perform various calculations on grouped data.

    MySQL HAVING Clause

    The MySQL HAVING Clause is used in conjunction with the GROUP BY clause to filter the groups of returned rows based on specified conditions. It operates similarly to the WHERE clause but is applied after the data has been grouped.

    Example:

    Consider an "employees" table with the following data:

    emp_id emp_name working_hours
    1 John 8
    2 Alice 7
    3 Bob 6
    4 Jane 8
    5 Sam 7

    Here\'s an example of using the HAVING clause with the SUM function to return the emp_name and the sum of their working hours for employees whose total working hours are greater than 5:

    SELECT emp_name, SUM(working_hours) AS "Total working hours" FROM employees GROUP BY emp_name HAVING SUM(working_hours) > 5;

    This query returns the emp_name and the total working hours for employees whose total working hours are greater than 5. Only the groups that meet the condition specified in the HAVING clause are included in the result set.

    @string/mysql_where @string/mysql_distinct @string/mysql_from @string/mysql_order_by @string/mysql_group_by @string/mysql_having

    MySQL GRANT Statement

    The GRANT statement in MySQL allows system administrators to assign privileges and roles to MySQL user accounts, enabling them to perform specific actions on the database.

    Syntax:

    GRANT privilege_name(s) ON object TO user_account_name;

    Parameters:

    • privilege_name(s): Specifies the access rights or privileges to be granted to user accounts. Multiple privileges can be granted by separating them with a comma.
    • object: Determines the level of privilege being granted, such as global, database, table, column, stored routine, or proxy.
    • user_account_name: Specifies the name of the user account to which the access rights will be granted.

    Privilege Levels:

    MySQL supports different privilege levels, including global, database, table, column, stored routine, and proxy privileges. Each level provides different levels of access to the database objects.

    Examples:

    Suppose we want to grant privileges to a user named "john@localhost". Here\'s how we can do it:

    1. Granting privileges on a specific database to the user:

      2. GRANT ALL ON mystudentdb.* TO john@localhost;

    2. Granting EXECUTE privilege on a stored procedure or function to the user:

      3. GRANT EXECUTE ON PROCEDURE mydb.myprocedure TO john@localhost;

    3. Granting proxy privileges to a user to act as a proxy for other users:

      4. GRANT PROXY ON root TO peter@localhost;

    4. Granting privileges on specific columns of a table to the user:

      5. GRANT SELECT (col1), INSERT (col1, col2), UPDATE (col2) ON mydb.mytable TO john@localhost;

    After executing the GRANT statements, the specified user will have the assigned privileges and roles on the database objects, allowing them to perform the permitted actions.

    MySQL REVOKE Statement

    The REVOKE statement in MySQL is used to remove privileges and roles from user accounts, effectively restricting their access to certain actions within the database.

    Syntax:

    REVOKE privilege_name(s) ON object FROM user_account_name;

    Parameters:

    • privilege_name(s): Specifies the access rights or privileges to be revoked from user accounts. Multiple privileges can be revoked by separating them with a comma.
    • object: Determines the level of privilege from which access rights are being revoked, such as global, database, table, column, stored routine, or proxy.
    • user_account_name: Specifies the name of the user account from which the access rights will be revoked.

    Privilege Levels:

    MySQL supports various privilege levels, including global, database, table, column, stored routine, and proxy privileges. Each level allows different levels of access to database objects.

    Examples:

    Below are examples of using the REVOKE statement:

    1. Revoking all privileges from a specific database for a user:

      2. REVOKE ALL ON mystudentdb.* FROM john@localhost;

    2. Revoking EXECUTE privilege on a stored procedure or function from a user:

      3. REVOKE EXECUTE ON PROCEDURE mydb.myprocedure FROM john@localhost;

    3. Revoking proxy privileges from a user:

      4. REVOKE PROXY ON root FROM peter@localhost;

    4. Revoking privileges on specific columns of a table from a user:

      5. REVOKE SELECT (col1), INSERT (col1, col2), UPDATE (col2) ON mydb.mytable FROM john@localhost;

    After executing the REVOKE statements, the specified user will no longer have the revoked privileges and roles on the database objects, thereby restricting their access to the permitted actions.

    @string/mysql_grant_privilege @string/mysql_revoke_privilege

    MySQL IF() Function

    In MySQL, the IF() function operates within the context of control flow, allowing for conditional evaluations and returning values based on specified conditions. It is akin to an IF-ELSE statement in other programming languages.

    Syntax:

    IF(expression1, expression2, expression3)

    Parameters:

    • expression1: Required. This is the condition that is evaluated. If it evaluates to true (not null and not zero), expression2 is returned; otherwise, expression3 is returned.
    • expression2: Optional. It is the value returned if expression1 evaluates to true.
    • expression3: Optional. It is the value returned if expression1 evaluates to false.

    Default Return Type:

    The return type of the IF() function depends on the data types of expression2 and expression3. If both are strings or produce a string, the result is always a string. If they produce a floating-point value, the result is a floating-point value. If they produce an integer, the result is an integer.

    MySQL Version Support:

    The IF() function is supported in various versions of MySQL, including MySQL 3.23.3 to MySQL 8.0.

    Examples:

    1. Checking if 200 is greater than 350:

      2. SELECT IF(200 > 350, \'YES\', \'NO\');

      Output: NO

    2. Checking if 251 is equal to 251:

      3. SELECT IF(251 = 251, \'Correct\', \'Wrong\');

      Output: Correct

    3. Comparing two strings:

      4. SELECT IF(STRCMP(\'Rinky Ponting\', \'Yuvraj Singh\') = 0, \'Correct\', \'Wrong\');

      Output: Wrong

    4. Using IF() function in a SELECT statement with a table:

      5. SELECT lastname, IF(age > 20, "Mature", "Immature") AS Result FROM student;

      Output: Displays the last name of students, marking them as "Mature" if their age is greater than 20, and "Immature" otherwise.

    MySQL IFNULL() Function

    The IFNULL() function in MySQL is a useful tool for handling NULL values within expressions. It allows for conditional evaluation, returning one value if a given expression is not NULL, and another if it is NULL.

    Syntax:

    IFNULL(Expression1, Expression2)

    Parameters:

    • Expression1: Required. This expression is checked for NULL values.
    • Expression2: Required. It is the value returned if Expression1 is NULL.

    MySQL Version Support:

    The IFNULL() function is supported in various versions of MySQL, including MySQL 3.23.3 to MySQL 8.0.

    Examples:

    1. Checking if 0 is NULL:

      2. SELECT IFNULL(0, 5);

      Output: 0

    2. Checking if "Hello" is NULL:

      3. SELECT IFNULL("Hello", "javaTpoint");

      Output: Hello

    3. Checking if NULL is NULL:

      4. SELECT IFNULL(NULL, 5);

      Output: 5

    4. Utilizing IFNULL() function with a table:

      5. CREATE TABLE `student_contacts` ( `studentid` int unsigned NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT, `contactname` varchar(45) NOT NULL, `cellphone` varchar(20) DEFAULT NULL, `homephone` varchar(20) DEFAULT NULL ); SELECT contactname, IFNULL(cellphone, homephone) AS phone FROM student_contacts;

      This query retrieves data from the student_contacts table, displaying contact names along with their phone numbers. If the cellphone is NULL, it returns the homephone number instead.

    MySQL NULLIF() Function

    The NULLIF() function in MySQL is a control flow function used for comparison between two expressions. It returns NULL if the two expressions are equal; otherwise, it returns the first expression. This function is particularly useful for handling division by zero errors and comparing values.

    Syntax:

    NULLIF(Expression1, Expression2)

    Parameters:

    • Expression1: Required. The first expression for comparison.
    • Expression2: Required. The second expression for comparison.

    MySQL Version Support:

    The NULLIF() function is supported in various versions of MySQL, including MySQL 3.23.3 to MySQL 8.0.

    Examples:

    1. Checking if "javaTpoint" equals "javaTpoint":

      2. SELECT NULLIF("javaTpoint", "javaTpoint");

      Output: NULL

    2. Comparing "Hello" with "404":

      3. SELECT NULLIF("Hello", "404");

      Output: Hello

    3. Comparing integer values 9 and 5:

      4. SELECT NULLIF(9,5);

      Output: 9

    4. Preventing division by zero error using NULLIF():

      5. SELECT 1/NULLIF(0,0);

      Output: NULL

    5. Utilizing NULLIF() function with a table:

      6. SELECT cust_name, occupation, qualification, NULLIF(qualification, "Btech") AS result FROM myproductdb.customer;

      This query retrieves data from the customer table, checking the qualification column against "Btech". If the qualification is "Btech", it returns NULL; otherwise, it returns the qualification value.

    MySQL CASE Expression

    The MySQL CASE expression is a versatile control flow function that allows for the implementation of if-else or if-then-else logic within a query. It facilitates the evaluation of multiple conditions and returns a result based on the first condition that evaluates to true. The CASE expression can be used in various contexts such as SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY clauses, etc.

    Simple CASE statement:

    In this method, a single value is compared to a series of values or conditions, and the corresponding result is returned when a match is found.

    CASE value WHEN [compare_value] THEN result [WHEN [compare_value] THEN result ...] [ELSE result] END

    Searched CASE statement:

    This method involves evaluating a series of conditions, and the result is returned based on the condition that evaluates to true. If none of the conditions are true, the ELSE clause (if provided) is executed.

    CASE WHEN [condition] THEN result [WHEN [condition] THEN result ...] [ELSE result] END

    The return type of the CASE expression depends on the context in which it is used. For instance, if used in a string context, it returns a string result, and if used in a numeric context, it returns an integer, float, or decimal value.

    The CASE statement is supported across various versions of MySQL, including MySQL 3.23.3 to MySQL 8.0.

    Example:

    Here\'s an example demonstrating the use of the CASE statement with a table named \'students\', where the \'class\' column contains abbreviations for student departments. The CASE statement is used to replace these abbreviations with the full department names:

    SELECT studentid, firstname, CASE class WHEN \'CS\' THEN \'Computer Science\' WHEN \'EC\' THEN \'Electronics and Communication\' ELSE \'Electrical Engineering\' END AS department FROM students;

    Executing the above query will produce output where the \'department\' column contains the full department names instead of abbreviations.

    MySQL IF Statement

    The IF statement in MySQL is a fundamental construct used in stored programs to implement conditional logic. It allows for the execution of specific SQL statements based on certain conditions. The IF statement can be used in three forms: IF-THEN, IF-THEN-ELSE, and IF-THEN-ELSEIF-ELSE, and must be terminated with END IF. Let\'s explore each of these forms in detail:

    IF-THEN Statement:

    The IF-THEN statement executes a set of SQL queries based on a specified condition. If the condition evaluates to true, the statements between IF-THEN and END IF are executed.

    IF condition THEN statements; END IF;

    Example:

    DELIMITER $$ CREATE PROCEDURE myResult(original_rate NUMERIC(6,2), OUT discount_rate NUMERIC(6,2)) NO SQL BEGIN IF (original_rate > 200) THEN SET discount_rate = original_rate * 0.5; END IF; SELECT discount_rate; END$$ DELIMITER $$

    IF-THEN-ELSE Statement:

    The IF-THEN-ELSE statement allows for the execution of alternative statements when the condition specified in the IF block evaluates to false. If the condition is true, the statements between IF-THEN and ELSE are executed; otherwise, the statements after ELSE are executed.

    IF condition THEN statements; ELSE else-statements; END IF;

    Example:

    DELIMITER $$ CREATE PROCEDURE myResult(original_rate NUMERIC(6,2), OUT discount_rate NUMERIC(6,2)) NO SQL BEGIN IF (original_rate > 200) THEN SET discount_rate = original_rate * 0.5; ELSE SET discount_rate = original_rate; END IF; SELECT discount_rate; END$$ DELIMITER $$

    IF-THEN-ELSEIF-ELSE Statement:

    The IF-THEN-ELSEIF-ELSE statement allows for the execution of statements based on multiple conditions. It evaluates each condition sequentially and executes the corresponding statements when a condition evaluates to true. If none of the conditions are true, the statements within the ELSE block are executed.

    IF condition THEN statements; ELSEIF elseif-condition THEN elseif-statements; ... ELSE else-statements; END IF;

    Example:

    DELIMITER $$ CREATE PROCEDURE myResult(original_rate NUMERIC(6,2), OUT discount_rate NUMERIC(6,2)) NO SQL BEGIN IF (original_rate > 500) THEN SET discount_rate = original_rate * 0.5; ELSEIF (original_rate <= 500 AND original_rate > 250) THEN SET discount_rate = original_rate * 0.8; ELSE SET discount_rate = original_rate; END IF; SELECT discount_rate; END$$ DELIMITER $$

    These examples demonstrate the usage of the IF statement in MySQL stored procedures to implement conditional logic based on various conditions.

    @string/mysql_if @string/mysql_ifnull @string/mysql_nullif @string/mysql_case @string/mysql_if_statement MySQL AND Condition Example

    MySQL AND Condition Example

    Consider a table named \'employees\' with the following structure and data:

    employee_id employee_name department
    1 John Sales
    2 Alice Marketing
    3 Bob Sales
    4 Jane HR
    5 Sam Marketing

    Example 1:

    Retrieve employees from the \'employees\' table who work in the \'Sales\' department and have an employee_id greater than 2.

    SQL Query:

    SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department = \'Sales\' AND employee_id > 2;

    Output:

    employee_id employee_name department
    3 Bob Sales

    Example 2:

    Retrieve employees from the \'employees\' table who work in the \'Marketing\' department and have an employee_id less than or equal to 3.

    SQL Query:

    SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department = \'Marketing\' AND employee_id <= 3;

    Output:

    employee_id employee_name department
    1 John Sales
    2 Alice Marketing

    MySQL OR Condition Example

    Consider a table "cus_tbl" with the following data:

    cus_id cus_firstname cus_lastname
    1 John Doe
    2 Jane Smith
    3 Ajeet Kumar
    4 Ajeet Sharma

    Execute the following query:

    SELECT * FROM cus_tbl WHERE cus_firstname = \'Ajeet\' OR cus_id > 100;

    Output:

    cus_id cus_firstname cus_lastname
    3 Ajeet Kumar
    4 Ajeet Sharma

    Note: In the above example, you can see that the second condition "cus_id > 100" is logically incorrect for the provided data. However, the query still displays the correct result because of the OR condition. At least one of the conditions (cus_firstname = \'Ajeet\' or cus_id > 100) needs to be true for a record to be selected.

    MySQL AND OR Combination Example

    Consider a table "students" with the following data:

    student_id student_name course_name
    1 Aryan Java
    2 Bob Python
    3 Alice Java

    Execute the following query:

    SELECT * FROM students WHERE (course_name = \'Java\' AND student_name = \'Aryan\') OR (student_id < 2);

    Output:

    student_id student_name course_name
    1 Aryan Java
    2 Bob Python

    In this example, we are selecting records where either:

    • The course_name is \'Java\' and the student_name is \'Aryan\'.
    • The student_id is less than 2.

    Using parentheses ensures that the conditions are evaluated in the correct order, as specified by the logical requirements.

    MySQL Boolean Handling

    MySQL Boolean Representation

    Execute the following query to see how MySQL handles Boolean literals:

    SELECT TRUE, FALSE, true, false, True, False;

    After successful execution, MySQL will display the integer values corresponding to the Boolean literals true and false.

    MySQL Boolean Example

    We can store Boolean values in MySQL tables using the TINYINT data type. Let\'s create a table named "students" to demonstrate this:

    CREATE TABLE students ( student_id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, name VARCHAR(40) NOT NULL, age VARCHAR(3), pass BOOLEAN );

    In the above query, the "pass" field is defined as a BOOLEAN, which internally gets represented as TINYINT.

    Let\'s insert some data into the "students" table:

    INSERT INTO students(name, pass) VALUES(\'Peter\', true), (\'John\', false);

    Executing the following query retrieves data from the "students" table:

    SELECT student_id, name, pass FROM students;

    This will return the student data along with their pass status, represented as 0 for false and 1 for true.

    Boolean Operators in MySQL

    MySQL allows us to use operators with Boolean values. For example, to retrieve students who passed, you can execute:

    SELECT student_id, name, pass FROM students WHERE pass = TRUE;

    Alternatively, you can use the IS operator to compare boolean values:

    SELECT student_id, name, pass FROM students WHERE pass IS TRUE;

    To retrieve students who did not pass, you can use:

    SELECT student_id, name, pass FROM students WHERE pass IS FALSE;

    OR

    SELECT student_id, name, pass FROM students WHERE pass IS NOT TRUE;

    These queries will return the corresponding student records based on their pass status.

    MySQL LIKE Condition Examples

    Consider a table "officers" with the following data:

    officer_name address
    John Lucknow
    Mary Lucknow
    Alice Lucanow

    MySQL LIKE Condition 1: Retrieve officers whose address starts with "Luck":

    SELECT officer_name FROM officers WHERE address LIKE \'Luck%\'; Output: +--------------+ | officer_name | +--------------+ | John | | Mary | +--------------+

    MySQL LIKE Condition 2: Retrieve officers whose address is "Lucanow":

    SELECT officer_name FROM officers WHERE address LIKE \'Luc_now\'; Output: +--------------+ | officer_name | +--------------+ | Alice | +--------------+

    MySQL LIKE Condition 3: Retrieve officers whose address does not start with "Luck":

    SELECT officer_name FROM officers WHERE address NOT LIKE \'Luck%\'; Output: +--------------+ | officer_name | +--------------+ | Alice | +--------------+

    These examples demonstrate how to use the LIKE condition in MySQL to perform pattern matching and retrieve specific data based on patterns within text fields.

    MySQL IN Condition Example

    Consider a table "officers" with the following data:

    officer_name designation
    Ajeet Manager
    Vimal Supervisor
    Deepika Officer

    MySQL IN Condition Example: Select officers with names \'Ajeet\', \'Vimal\', or \'Deepika\':

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE officer_name IN (\'Ajeet\', \'Vimal\', \'Deepika\'); Output: +--------------+-------------+ | officer_name | designation | +--------------+-------------+ | Ajeet | Manager | | Vimal | Supervisor | | Deepika | Officer | +--------------+-------------+

    Advantages over OR condition: Using IN condition simplifies the query structure and enhances readability compared to using multiple OR conditions. It may also provide performance benefits, especially with large datasets.

    MySQL ANY Operator Example

    Consider two tables, table1 and table2, with numeric values. Let\'s say we want to retrieve values from table1 where the value is greater than any value in table2.

    Example:

    SELECT num_value FROM table1 WHERE num_value > ANY (SELECT num_val FROM table2);

    This query will return true if any value in table2 is less than the corresponding value in table1, based on the comparison.

    The ANY operator in MySQL serves similar functions as the ALL operator, but it returns true if any of the subquery values satisfy the condition. It is important to understand that IN and = ANY are synonyms when used with subqueries, but they are not interchangeable when used with expression lists.

    Advantages of using the ANY operator include:

    • Simplifying complex conditions in SQL queries.
    • Providing a concise syntax for comparing values in subqueries.
    • Facilitating the selection of rows based on multiple conditions.

    MySQL EXISTS Operator Example

    The EXISTS operator in MySQL is a Boolean operator used to determine the presence or absence of data in a subquery. It returns true if the subquery returns any records and false if it does not. This operator can be combined with SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE, and INSERT statements to perform various operations based on the existence of data.

    Syntax:

    SELECT col_names FROM tab_name WHERE [NOT] EXISTS ( SELECT col_names FROM tab_name WHERE condition );

    Here, the NOT operator can be used to negate the EXISTS operator, returning true when the subquery does not return any rows.

    The EXISTS operator improves query performance by terminating execution once a matching result is found in the subquery. It ignores the select list in the subquery, making it efficient for evaluating conditions.

    Example:

    -- Find customers who have placed at least one order SELECT name, occupation FROM customer WHERE EXISTS ( SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer.cust_id = orders.cust_id ); -- Find customers who have not placed any orders SELECT name, occupation FROM customer WHERE NOT EXISTS ( SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer.cust_id = orders.cust_id ); -- Delete a record from the orders table based on a condition DELETE FROM orders WHERE EXISTS ( SELECT * FROM customer WHERE order_id = 3 ); -- Check if a row exists in a table SELECT EXISTS(SELECT * FROM customer WHERE cust_id = 104) AS Result;

    The EXISTS operator provides a powerful way to check for the existence of data in subqueries, allowing for efficient query optimization and precise data manipulation. It is a valuable tool for performing conditional operations in MySQL.

    MySQL NOT Operator Example

    In MySQL, the NOT condition serves as the negation operator, reversing the outcome of a specified condition in a SQL statement. Let\'s explore how it can be used with various comparison operators:

    1. MySQL NOT Operator with IN Condition:

    Using the NOT operator with the IN condition to retrieve records where the officer_name is not among specified values:

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE NOT officer_name IN (\'Ajeet\', \'Vimal\', \'Deepika\');

    2. MySQL NOT Operator with IS NULL Condition:

    Using the NOT operator with the IS NULL condition to retrieve records where the officer_name is not NULL:

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE NOT officer_name IS NULL;

    3. MySQL NOT Operator with LIKE Condition:

    Using the NOT operator with the LIKE condition to retrieve records where the officer_name does not match the specified pattern:

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE NOT officer_name LIKE \'A%\';

    4. MySQL NOT Operator with BETWEEN Condition:

    Using the NOT operator with the BETWEEN condition to retrieve records that fall outside of the specified range for the officer_id:

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE NOT officer_id BETWEEN 100 AND 200;

    MySQL Not Equal Operator Examples

    The MySQL Not Equal operator, represented by either

    <>

    or

    !=

    , functions as an inequality operator, comparing two expressions and returning a set of rows where the expressions are not equal. Let\'s explore some examples:

    Example 1: Using

    <>

    or

    !=

    Operator to Retrieve Students Not Residing in England

    SELECT * FROM students WHERE city <> "England"; -- OR SELECT * FROM students WHERE city != "England";

    Example 2: Using

    <>

    Operator with GROUP BY Clause to Retrieve Customers without Cellphone Numbers and with Unique Income Values

    SELECT * FROM customers JOIN contacts ON customer_id = contact_id WHERE cellphone <> "Null" GROUP BY income;

    Example 3: Using

    !=

    Operator with JOIN Statement to Retrieve Records where Cellphone Number is Not Null

    SELECT * FROM customers JOIN contacts ON customer_id = contact_id WHERE cellphone != "Null";

    Example 4: Using

    !=

    Operator with Multiple Conditions in the WHERE Clause to Retrieve Customer Details

    SELECT * FROM customers WHERE income > 40000 AND occupation != "Developer";

    MySQL IS NULL Condition Example

    The MySQL

    IS NULL

    condition is employed to verify if a particular expression holds a

    NULL

    value. Let\'s consider a scenario where we have a table named officers with officer details. To retrieve records where the officer name is

    NULL

    , we can use the

    IS NULL

    condition:

    Example:

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE officer_name IS NULL;

    This query will return all rows from the officers table where the

    officer_name

    column holds a

    NULL

    value.

    By employing the MySQL

    IS NULL

    condition, we can effectively identify and handle

    NULL

    values within our database tables, ensuring accurate data retrieval and manipulation.

    MySQL IS NOT NULL Condition Example

    The MySQL

    IS NOT NULL

    condition is utilized to verify if a specific expression does not hold a

    NULL

    value. Let\'s consider a scenario where we have a table named officers containing officer details. To retrieve records where the officer name is not

    NULL

    , we can use the

    IS NOT NULL

    condition:

    Example:

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE officer_name IS NOT NULL;

    This query will return all rows from the officers table where the

    officer_name

    column does not hold a

    NULL

    value.

    By utilizing the MySQL

    IS NOT NULL

    condition, we can effectively filter out

    NULL

    values from our query results, ensuring accurate data retrieval and analysis.

    MySQL BETWEEN Condition Example

    The MySQL

    BETWEEN

    condition is employed to extract values from an expression within a specified range. Let\'s explore some examples:

    (i) MySQL BETWEEN condition with numeric values:

    Consider a table named officers with the following data:

    SELECT * FROM officers WHERE officer_id BETWEEN 1 AND 3;

    The query retrieves rows where the

    officer_id

    falls within the range of 1 to 3.

    (ii) MySQL BETWEEN condition with date values:

    The BETWEEN condition can also be applied to date comparisons.

    Consider a table named employees with date-related data:

    SELECT * FROM employees WHERE working_date BETWEEN CAST(\'2015-01-24\' AS DATE) AND CAST(\'2015-01-25\' AS DATE);

    This query fetches records where the

    working_date

    falls within the inclusive range of \'2015-01-24\' and \'2015-01-25\'.

    By utilizing the MySQL

    BETWEEN

    condition, we can efficiently filter data within specific ranges, whether numeric or date-based, enhancing query precision and relevance.

    MySQL Joins Example

    MySQL joins are integral for retrieving data from multiple tables, allowing complex queries that correlate information across related datasets. There are three primary types of MySQL joins: INNER JOIN, LEFT OUTER JOIN, and RIGHT OUTER JOIN.

    MySQL INNER JOIN (Simple Join):

    The INNER JOIN is used to retrieve rows from multiple tables where the join condition is met. It returns only the rows for which there is a match in both tables.

    SELECT columns FROM table1 INNER JOIN table2 ON table1.column = table2.column;

    MySQL LEFT OUTER JOIN:

    The LEFT OUTER JOIN retrieves all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table. If there\'s no match, NULL values are returned for the right table columns.

    SELECT columns FROM table1 LEFT [OUTER] JOIN table2 ON table1.column = table2.column;

    MySQL RIGHT OUTER JOIN:

    The RIGHT OUTER JOIN is similar to LEFT OUTER JOIN, but it retrieves all rows from the right table and matching rows from the left table. If there\'s no match, NULL values are returned for the left table columns.

    SELECT columns FROM table1 RIGHT [OUTER] JOIN table2 ON table1.column = table2.column;

    These examples illustrate how MySQL joins can be used to retrieve data from multiple tables, allowing for complex data correlations.

    MySQL Inner Join Example

    The MySQL Inner Join operation is utilized to retrieve records from tables that satisfy a specified condition, thus concealing irrelevant rows and columns. Although MySQL treats it as the default join type, the Inner Join keyword can be used to enhance query readability.

    MySQL Inner Join Syntax:

    The Inner Join keyword is integrated into the SELECT statement, typically following the FROM clause. Here\'s the syntax:

    SELECT columns FROM table1 INNER JOIN table2 ON condition1 INNER JOIN table3 ON condition2 ...;

    In this syntax:

    • First, the desired columns are selected.
    • Then, the tables to be joined are specified, followed by the Inner Join clause.
    • Finally, the condition for joining tables is provided after the ON keyword, determining the matching rows between the specified tables.

    MySQL Inner Join Example:

    Let\'s consider two tables, "students" and "technologies", with the following data:

    SELECT students.first_name, students.last_name, students.city, technologies.technology_name FROM students INNER JOIN technologies ON students.student_id = technologies.student_id;

    This query fetches student first names, last names, cities, and associated technologies.

    MySQL Inner Join with Group By Clause:

    Inner Join can also be combined with the GROUP BY clause. This statement returns student IDs, technology names, cities, and institute names.

    SELECT students.student_id, technologies.technology_name, students.city, students.institute_name FROM students INNER JOIN technologies ON students.student_id = technologies.student_id GROUP BY students.student_id;

    This query retrieves unique student IDs along with their associated technology, city, and institute names.

    Understanding Left Joins in MySQL

    Understanding Left Joins in MySQL

    To understand left joins in MySQL, it\'s essential to grasp the concept of retrieving records from multiple tables while preserving all records from the left table and matching records from the right table.

    Key Points about Left Joins:

    • A left join returns all records from the left table and only matching records from the right table, or null if no matching record is found.
    • It ensures that all records from the left table are included in the result set.
    • Left joins are useful for scenarios where you want to retrieve data even if there are no matching records in the right table.

    Syntax:

    SELECT columns FROM table1 LEFT JOIN table2 ON Join_Condition;

    In the syntax:

    • table1

      : The left-hand table.

    • table2

      : The right-hand table.

    • LEFT JOIN

      : Specifies the type of join to perform.

    • ON Join_Condition

      : Specifies the condition to match rows between the tables.

    Left Join Example:

    Suppose we have two tables, customers and orders, with the following data:

    customers orders
    customer_id cust_name order_id price date
    101 John 501 2500 2023-01-10
    102 Mary 502 3000 2023-01-15
    103 Alice NULL NULL NULL

    To select records from both tables using a left join:

    SELECT customers.customer_id, cust_name, price, date FROM customers LEFT JOIN orders ON customers.customer_id = orders.customer_id;

    This query will return:

    customer_id cust_name price date
    101 John 2500 2023-01-10
    102 Mary 3000 2023-01-15
    103 Alice NULL NULL

    This indicates that all customers are returned, even if they have no orders, in which case the price and date columns show as NULL.

    MySQL Right Join Example

    The MySQL Right Join is a type of join used to combine records from two tables based on a specified condition, returning all rows from the right-hand table and only those rows from the left-hand table that fulfill the join condition. If no matching records are found in the left table, it returns null values. It\'s also referred to as a Right Outer Join.

    MySQL RIGHT JOIN Syntax:

    The Right Join syntax involves selecting columns from the tables being joined and specifying the join condition. Here\'s how it looks:

    SELECT column_list FROM Table1 RIGHT [OUTER] JOIN Table2 ON join_condition;

    In this syntax:

    • Table1 represents the left-hand table.
    • Table2 represents the right-hand table.
    • The Right Join clause returns all records from Table2 and matched records from Table1 based on the specified join condition.

    MySQL RIGHT JOIN Examples:

    RIGHT JOIN for joining two tables:

    Consider two tables, "customers" and "orders", with the following data:

    SELECT * FROM orders RIGHT JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;

    This query retrieves customer IDs, names, prices, and dates, ensuring that all order records are included, even if there are no corresponding customers.

    RIGHT JOIN with WHERE Clause:

    The WHERE clause allows for further filtering of results. Here\'s how it can be used with Right Join:

    SELECT * FROM orders RIGHT JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id WHERE orders.price BETWEEN 2500 AND 5000;

    This statement retrieves records where the price is between 2500 and 5000.

    Understanding Cross Joins in MySQL

    To understand cross joins in MySQL, it\'s essential to grasp the concept of combining all possible combinations of rows from two or more tables, effectively providing the Cartesian product of the associated tables.

    Key Points about Cross Joins:

    • A cross join returns every possible combination of rows from the contributing tables.
    • It produces a result set that contains all rows from the first table multiplied by all rows from the second table.
    • Cross joins are useful for scenarios where you need to consider every combination of rows from different tables.

    Syntax:

    SELECT column-lists FROM table1 CROSS JOIN table2;

    In the syntax:

    • column-lists

      : The list of columns or fields that you want to retrieve.

    • table1, table2

      : The tables from which you want to fetch records.

    Cross Join Example:

    Suppose we have two tables, customers and contacts, with the following data:

    customers contacts
    customer_id cust_name income contact_id email phone
    101 John 50000 201 john@example.com 123456789
    102 Mary 60000 202 mary@example.com 987654321
    103 Alice 55000 203 alice@example.com 456123789

    To fetch all possible combinations of records from both tables, execute the following query:

    SELECT * FROM customers CROSS JOIN contacts;

    This query will return a result set with 9 rows, representing every possible combination of rows from the customers and contacts tables.

    Understanding Self Joins in MySQL

    A SELF JOIN is a type of join operation in MySQL where a table is joined with itself. This can be useful when you need to compare rows within the same table or when you need to retrieve related data from the same table. To perform a SELF JOIN, you typically use table aliases to distinguish between the different instances of the same table within the query.

    SELF JOIN Syntax:

    The syntax for a SELF JOIN in MySQL is similar to that of joining two different tables, except that you use table aliases to reference the same table multiple times. Here\'s the general syntax:

    SELECT s1.col_name, s2.col_name... FROM table1 AS s1, table1 AS s2 WHERE s1.common_col_name = s2.common_col_name;

    In this syntax:

    • s1, s2

      : These are table aliases used to reference the same table (

      table1

      in this case) multiple times within the query.

    • common_col_name

      : This is the column that is used to match rows between the two instances of the same table.

    SELF JOIN Example:

    Suppose we have a table named student with columns student_id, name, and course_id. Let\'s say we want to retrieve student IDs and names where the student IDs are the same but the course IDs are different. We can use a SELF JOIN to achieve this. Here\'s an example query:

    SELECT s1.student_id, s1.name FROM student AS s1, student AS s2 WHERE s1.student_id = s2.student_id AND s1.course_id <> s2.course_id;

    This query will return student IDs and names where the student IDs are the same but the course IDs are different.

    Conclusion:

    A SELF JOIN allows you to join a table with itself, which can be useful in various scenarios such as comparing rows within the same table or retrieving related data from the same table. By understanding the syntax and examples provided, you can effectively use SELF JOINs in your MySQL queries when needed.

    DELETE JOIN Operations in MySQL

    To achieve a DELETE JOIN operation in MySQL, you can utilize either INNER JOIN or LEFT JOIN in combination with the DELETE statement. This allows you to delete rows from one table based on a condition that involves another table.

    DELETE JOIN with INNER JOIN

    With INNER JOIN, you can delete rows from one table and its matching rows from another table that satisfy a specified condition.

    Syntax:

    DELETE target_table FROM table1 INNER JOIN table2 ON table1.joining_column = table2.joining_column WHERE condition;

    Here\'s a breakdown of the syntax:

    • target_table: The table from which you want to delete rows.
    • table1 and table2: The tables you\'re joining.
    • joining_column: The column used to join the tables.
    • condition: The condition that determines which rows to delete.

    Example:

    Suppose you have tables named students and contacts with the following data:

    students contacts
    student_id name college_id cellphone
    1 Alice 1 1234567890
    2 Bob 2 NULL
    3 Charlie 3 9876543210
    4 Dave 4 NULL

    To delete rows from both tables where students.student_id matches contacts.college_id and students.student_id = 4, you can execute:

    DELETE students, contacts FROM students INNER JOIN contacts ON students.student_id = contacts.college_id WHERE students.student_id = 4;

    DELETE JOIN with LEFT JOIN

    Using LEFT JOIN with DELETE allows you to delete rows from the left table that do not have matching rows in the right table.

    Syntax:

    DELETE target_table FROM table1 LEFT JOIN table2 ON table1.joining_column = table2.joining_column WHERE table2.joining_column IS NULL;

    In this syntax:

    • target_table: The table from which you want to delete rows.
    • table1 and table2: The tables being joined.
    • joining_column: The column used for joining.
    • table2.joining_column IS NULL: Ensures that you\'re deleting rows where there\'s no match in the right table.

    Example:

    Suppose you have tables named customers and contacts with the following data:

    customers contacts
    customer_id cellphone contact_id name
    1 1234567890 1 Alice
    2 NULL 2 Bob
    3 9876543210 3 Charlie
    4 NULL

    To delete customers who don\'t have a cellphone number (where customers.cellphone is NULL), you can execute:

    DELETE customers FROM customers LEFT JOIN contacts ON customers.customer_id = contacts.contact_id WHERE customers.cellphone IS NULL;

    MySQL UPDATE JOIN Operation

    To perform a MySQL UPDATE JOIN operation, you can utilize the UPDATE statement in combination with INNER JOIN or LEFT JOIN to update records in one table based on data from another table. Here\'s a detailed explanation of how to use UPDATE JOIN in MySQL, along with examples:

    Syntax:

    UPDATE table1 INNER JOIN table2 ON table1.column = table2.column SET table1.column1 = value1, table1.column2 = value2, ... WHERE condition;

    In this syntax:

    • table1: The target table you want to update.
    • table2: The table you\'re joining with.
    • column: The column used for joining.
    • value1, value2, ...: The new values you want to set for columns in table1.
    • condition: The condition that determines which rows to update.

    Explanation:

    • The UPDATE statement is used to modify data in the target table (table1).
    • The INNER JOIN or LEFT JOIN clause is used to join table1 with table2 based on a specified condition.
    • The SET clause is used to specify the columns in table1 that you want to update along with their new values.
    • The WHERE clause is optional and is used to specify additional conditions for updating rows.

    Example:

    Let\'s consider two tables, Performance and Employees, as described in your example:

    UPDATE Employees e INNER JOIN Performance p ON e.performance = p.performance SET e.salary = e.salary + (e.salary * p.percentage);

    This query updates the salary column in the Employees table based on the percentage column from the Performance table. Each employee\'s salary is increased by the percentage specified in the Performance table for their respective performance.

    LEFT JOIN Example:

    Suppose you want to update the salary for newly hired employees, whose performance records are not available in the Performance table. You can use a LEFT JOIN to update these records:

    UPDATE Employees e LEFT JOIN Performance p ON e.performance = p.performance SET e.salary = e.salary * 1.025 WHERE p.percentage IS NULL;

    In this query, newly hired employees are identified by the absence of corresponding records in the Performance table (p.percentage IS NULL). Their salary is increased by 2.5% using the SET clause.

    These examples demonstrate how to perform UPDATE JOIN operations in MySQL using INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN clauses, allowing you to update records in one table based on data from another table.

    Understanding Equijoins in MySQL

    To understand equijoins in MySQL, it\'s essential to grasp the concept of joining tables based on common column values. An equijoin, a type of inner join, specifically combines tables by matching values in specified columns.

    Key Points about Equijoin:

    • It matches rows from different tables based on equality of specified columns.
    • The result set only includes records where values in the specified columns match.
    • Equijoins can involve more than two tables.
    • Column names don\'t have to be identical across tables, as long as they represent the same type of data.

    Syntax:

    SELECT column_name(s) FROM table_name1, table_name2, ..., table_nameN WHERE table_name1.column_name = table_name2.column_name;

    OR

    SELECT (column_list | *) FROM table_name1 JOIN table_name2 ON table_name1.column_name = table_name2.column_name;

    In the syntax:

    • Specify columns to include in the result set after the SELECT keyword.
    • List tables to join after the FROM keyword.
    • Define the join condition using the WHERE or ON clause, ensuring equality between columns from different tables.

    Equijoin Example:

    Suppose we have two tables, customer and balance, with the following data:

    customer balance
    account customer_name account_num balance
    101 John 101 5000
    102 Alice 102 8000
    103 Bob 103 3000

    To perform an equijoin and retrieve data from both tables, use the following query:

    SELECT cust.customer_name, bal.balance FROM customer AS cust, balance AS bal WHERE cust.account = bal.account_num;

    Alternatively, you can achieve the same result with the JOIN syntax:

    SELECT cust.customer_name, bal.balance FROM customer AS cust JOIN balance AS bal WHERE cust.account = bal.account_num;

    Equijoin Using Three Tables:

    Consider another table, cust_info, with the following structure:

    cust_info
    account_no mobile address
    101 1234567890 New York
    102 9876543210 Los Angeles
    103 NULL Chicago

    To perform an equijoin involving three tables, execute the following query:

    SELECT C.customer_name, C.account, B.balance, I.mobile FROM customer AS C, balance AS B, cust_info AS I WHERE C.account = B.account_num AND B.account_num = I.account_no;

    This query joins customer, balance, and cust_info tables based on matching account numbers and retrieves relevant data from all three tables.

    Understanding Natural Joins in MySQL

    A Natural Join in MySQL combines rows from two or more tables based on columns with the same name and data type. Unlike other types of joins, such as INNER or LEFT JOIN, a Natural Join does not require specifying the columns to join on using an ON or USING clause. Instead, it automatically joins tables based on matching column names.

    Natural Join Syntax:

    The syntax for a Natural Join in MySQL is straightforward:

    SELECT [column_names | *] FROM table_name1 NATURAL JOIN table_name2;

    In this syntax:

    • column_names

      : These are the columns you want to select from the resulting table. You can specify specific column names or use * to select all columns.

    • table_name1, table_name2

      : These are the names of the tables you want to join.

    Natural Join Example:

    Let\'s consider two tables, customer and balance, with columns id and account being common between them. Here\'s how you can perform a Natural Join:

    SELECT cust.customer_name, bal.balance FROM customer AS cust NATURAL JOIN balance AS bal;

    This query will automatically join the customer and balance tables based on the common column account, as they have the same name and data type.

    Conclusion:

    A Natural Join in MySQL simplifies the process of joining tables by automatically identifying and joining columns with the same name and data type. By understanding the syntax and examples provided, you can effectively use Natural Joins in your MySQL queries to combine data from multiple tables effortlessly.
